name: Three Sisters
review:
Dude like, this has to be the most niche game I've ever played, but the way it's so bizarre and sloppy kinda hooked me in??? I still wouldn't recommend this to anyone, but I can't say it's just 100% dogshlt. I guess I just happened to be part of the target audience.
Sometimes it IS interesting to look at a piece of media and just think to yourself "what the fuck is this?" and "what went through the mind of the devs?" like you have some edgy toxic crabs at one point, horrific realistic meat slop at another, then some horrific mexican clowns?? and they all look horrible, but for this game, it's its own charm. Then two seconds later the main characters will have some cool dialog or things happening that aren't shltpost-y and gives me whiplash.

I played for 17 hours, most of that was the very, very slow combat, which there is a lot of in the game; and many of which I had to fight multiple times because the enemy instantly killed me (which was funny the first times, but it became frustrating) and I had to reload a save, and go through the same 3-4 long fights before reaching some boss enemy and testing my chances again. Yeah if it was just the combat alone I prob would have dropped it earlier, but I was very interested in the world the game has, the different lockers kinda make me feel like i'm in Yume Nikki exploring the different worlds, I love that aspect of the game, sometimes I just wanted to grab cheat engine or overlevel myself just so I could "skip" the battles and explore freely.
Now I stopped playing because it felt like I've seen most of the game so far and don't see a goal or where to go or what to do, having my last things done being [spoiler] defeating Midas, and exploring some hills with bell guys (killed two eldritch horrors to get no reward at all haha) [/spoiler]

dude why did I play this for 17 hours...

name: Three Sisters
review:
I like the crappy visuals and the out-of-place music, but the banter is miserable.

In my first half-dozen combats, I had a crab tell me:

To Kill myself
That I was pathetic and it left the battle, granting me its XP (funny the first time, exhausting the next 12)
When it died it would haunt me, telling me to kill myself

Then the crab morphed into an Uber Crab and no matter how many times it duked me its XP and left, or I ran, I could never end the combat.

Easy uninstall, which is kind of a shame because I do think there's some neat stuff going on in here. I don't think I should have to put up with a Fornite lobby's worth of abuse to experience it - isn't there enough Fortnight lobby out there already? Perhaps the game creator could stand to tap someone else - hell, even ChatGPT - to come up with some better trash talk.

name: Three Sisters
review:
Honestly, I'm not entirely sure where I stand on this game. A lot of it I enjoyed, and the story seems quite interesting, but the excessive cringe of the enemies (oddly placed against a less-than-crude story plot) also gets old pretty early on, and definitely pulls you away from it. 

The story also is a bit too light to give you a clear path forward. You'll pretty much just wander in circles, not knowing what to do, until you figure out where to head next. The variety of maps and settings is surprisingly nice though, so it's not a total misery to get lost. All the enemies respawn every time you leave the area, which is great for leveling up, and torturous if you're not looking to do so.  (Some police officers that respawn every single time you leave a house or shop, and which absolutely cannot be escaped, definitely come to mind.) 

Leveling up is also frustratingly vague. Its hard to know which characters actually benefit from certain areas being increased, as quite a few of the attacks/moves seem too ineffective to be worth it. I pretty much stuck to about 20% of the available moves once the characters were fully leveled, as the battles took way too long if I tried experimenting with other moves and strategies. Disappointing, as the game really does show a lot of different moves and has the potential to be a really fun experience there.

Also, I think the game developer might have some urine-based fetishes, given how often it comes up in game. Which is every other minute. I mean, its really a lot, even in terms of intentional cringe. It gets a little too gross and creepy there, that's for sure. 

Aaaanyways, I eventually I stepped away from the game, and I haven't been super inclined to try it again, not in it's current state. However, I did really enjoy playing it for a time, and especially enjoyed the sister characters and the world design, travel, and lore. At first, I didn't even mind at first that the enemies are so obviously, intentionally cringey. It just became a bit too much given how often you have to deal with them. Funny for a while, but it pulls away from the story too much in the end. 

This game definitely has a ton of potential, but I can't quite recommend it yet, not when it could actually be something legitimately good with a bit more time. Hoping the development with this continues, as there really is a lot going for it.

name: Three Sisters
review:
I bought the game after seeing just a few screenshots. Some people may not like the style or how the game functions, but I can say I haven't played a game that actually made me laugh because of the dialogue. This game is clearly targeted toward people with a specific sense of humor, and I love the fever dream-esque theme. I'm always looking forward to what wild thing will happen next when I play. 

It is an old style RPG and whats so wrong with that? It adds to the charm. Controls are totally fine and there's no need to change it. Combat system is like any other RPG with some cool mechanics.

There is somewhat of a lack of enemy variety in the first two areas. It's funny, but it would really help if there were more variety.

The negative reviews boil down to people who're either:
A. Trash at video games
B. Clearly not the intended audience

It's pretty simple, If you love RPGs, funny and wild stuff, you'll enjoy yourself for sure.👍👍

name: Three Sisters
review:
Let me tell you a story. It was a crisp Sunday morning.
I had just arrived home, and I was sittin out on my porch smokin a cigar.
It was the last cigar I had, so I wanted to make it count.
I was doin a pretty fine job of that until, for just a moment...
I let go of the cigar while it wasn't in my mouth.
The cigar fell out of my hand and on to the porch,
Where it rolled off into the grass.
Upon realising that I didn't have any more cigars...
I was mighty upset.
I didn't have enough time to hike 15 miles to the general store.
The thought of not havin a cigar simply terrified me.
I immediately fell into a deep depression,
Distraught that I wouldn't get to have a cigar.
Less than four hours later, in a fit of cigar withdrawal madness,
I pulled a silly stunt.
Grabbed my trusty piece, played russian roulette all by myself.
Till the grim reaper showed up, and poof!
Just like that, I waved bye-bye.

name: Three Sisters
review:
Well I have various thing to highlight about this game, all positive btw.

Easy and familiar game mechanics if you have played other more popular games before. Character designs are very original, well made and funni. Like who tf would imagine an Ubers' car wheels would be the eyes of a crab.

On the other hand, this games comforts me. I like how its a mix of absurdity of mature themes with a drip of serious lore.As a person who is dealing with thought stuff I find this a really good game concept which even reflects irl human behaivour. Lots of ppl tend to laugh at their own traumatic situations as a way to relief to avoid constant overwhelming.

Is it worth the money? 
I must admit that if I never saw a random local online article that promotes low price/ free games I would have never discovered Three Sisters. 
I obtained this game for free and must say that is worth paying for it keeping in mind such things as:
* Is a game made with dedication from an small team.
* Frequent updates/ bug fixes.
* More gameplay will come someday.

Overall I find this game kind of unique and entretaining. Its just a game with joking manners with no "depth" most of the times. This review is kinda long cuz I like to get philosophical about anything and try to find meanings where they dont exist lol.

Just a quick reminder, its not the games' fault if you ignored all the warnings and started crying cuz you double-clicked Enter after an Ubercrab tells you to kys.

PD: It would be cool to have point decorations, I want little Vibias all over my profile.

name: Three Sisters
review:
Greetings Traveler, 

you saw this game and though to yourself "lemme check those negative reviews and see whats up.." ?

Let me be your friendly guide!

Now i do have to make the disclaimer that i actually already wrote a review but was kindly asked to put it down since it got too detailed upon unpolished gameplay and other things- understandable, i take no offense to that... actually it is kinda cute? knowing that the Dev damn well is active and caring. I still want to give my unpopular opinion that nobody asked for but this time i try to make it feel less like a gametest/bug report.

Alrighty- 

The BIGGEST issue i have will be at the end so you do not get spoiled by my opinion while i try to be very objective through this review of the early access... and damn, there is content.

Here we FREAKI- is that crab telling me to hang myself?

Three SIsters is appearently a sequel to another Game, which i did not play because this is the first i got in contact with.. but what is it?

At its core (in my very wrong opionion) this is close to a classic JRPG- travel with your party through the world, collect key items and fight in a turnbased system (which is somewhat interactive) against your enemies! A
Also the soundtrack is great- but it is music and therefor you kinda have to like that kind of music? its a taste thingy... but i do like the soundtrack and appearently it will be released as DLC.

Here i have to make the comment that the combat is difficult, i will take a closer look at the individual characters and theire use later.

Exploring maps if you are patient and don't stress it is fun- assuming you like 2D pixelmap exploring alá FinalFantasy/GoldenSun.

The Story is there and i like it a lot so far. it isn't forced down your throat and it isn't consuming a lot of time which makes it a nice pace. The characters feel real enough that i can actually like them- no need to agree but like... or hate? approve. i approve of them.

The occasional Dungeon with the Bossfight and looting is there- and even if we are still early in development it gives enough to do some optimization.

now to my personal expirience with the game and i will not bother to hide spoilers- proceed on your own risk.

You start out with One char- Vibia- who can't do anything but swing her Spear

Your Attacks have to be timed with a ring which will be rated and effective accordingly- a neat twist to the rather flat Pixel-JRPG's you usually encounter.

Spells later on have a button mash mechanic for their potency- again, not reinventing the wheel but it feels different and makes you more invested!

Here i have to make a little disclaimer that the first Part of the game is ROUGH! not only because you probably don't know the game but also because of something called "Action economy" usually known among Pen & Paper players. but once you get over the hurdle you feel like you are playing the game... or against the game? its challanging you alll the time.

After beating the first hurdle you get your other two Partymembers:
Lucia- who seemse to be crying on her splashart even when smiling
Catherine- damn she is sassy.

Lucia is providing support spells and  she is very usefull for exploring maps, there are some nice items for her and she feels like the most sane person!

Cathrine is the chlichee white mage with heals, revives and let's you know that you are screwed without her, she has also the ability to annoy enemies. 
her res-spell heals the target to full hp! which is very good to keep you from reloading saves.
she does not have any noteworthy equipment and i have to admit there isn't anything worth giving her- not even the books that increase your Mind.

There is also "Extra" characters and some of them are weird...

now to stats which you can boost as you want by applying skill points- you get 15 per Lv and as of right now you make the decision what to focus fairly early.

this makes the early game after aquiring the three sisters (hehe, he said the title) easy but at somepoint (mexico) you just struggle.

I invested most of my points in HP except for Cathy because here revive is expensive.

Exploring is nice and all, and a little bit of puzzling is also aviable involving backtracking- hate it or love it, i love it.

The shop is neat, but there are items i can't figure out for the life of me.

The lack of savepoints is understandable if you want a hard game but be prepared to be suddenly steamrolled if you venture too far.

now to the biggest issue i have with this game and why it is so disgusting to me...

i never installed it.

this may sound like some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ creepy pasta ♥♥♥♥ but it is the truth...

I never saw the storepage before, i never installed it... it just was there when i opened steam- already installed...

Is this a sign? are we living in a Simulation? Why is there a Stripper in the Crabcave?

Seriously tho... it creeped me out and that is the only reason why i don't recommend this game- anything else (assuming you like the genre and the crude humor) is top!

name: Three Sisters
review: If you're a fan of crazy RPG Maker titles with bizarre visuals and humor that feels like it's taken from the pages of It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia, this is the game you need to check out. The combat is fun and engaging, allowing you to go at it at your own pace, and the music is a medley of different styles and flavors, appropriate for every situation. The story is interesting and thought-provoking, while also having plenty of humor through one-liners and witty dialogue between characters. If you've picked this up for free, then all the better for you. If not, it's only $5, and I'm sure you've got a Lincoln sitting in your pocket to throw at this game. It's definitely worth a shot!

I found this game via an article on gamerant that was more about Steam having great prices and less about the game itself, and having played it for a bit I'm reasonably sure that this is a case of the game breaking containment. having looked quickly through the reviews, I saw no mention made of the article, which left a bad enough taste in my mouth that i felt the need to write.

Three Sisters reminds me of Toilet in Wonderland and the LISA series in the unapologetic presentation of its topics and the addicting nature of its game play. To fans of games like those, i would recommend this game highly.

Edit: it was absolutely intended as a compliment, dev!

[h1][b]Since nobody will read my review, here's a simple and basic chicken noodle soup recipe:[/b][/h1]

[h2]Ingredients:[/h2]
2 tablespoons olive oil or butter
1 small onion, chopped
2 carrots, sliced
2 celery stalks, sliced
3 cloves garlic, minced
8 cups chicken broth (or water with bouillon)
2 cups cooked shredded chicken (rotisserie or boiled)
2 cups egg noodles (or any pasta)
1 teaspoon salt (adjust to taste)
½ teaspoon black pepper
½ teaspoon dried thyme (optional)
½ teaspoon dried parsley (optional)

[h2]Instructions:[/h2]
[b]1. Sauté the Vegetables[/b]
• Heat oil or butter in a large pot over medium heat.
• Add onion, carrots, and celery.
• Cook for about 5 minutes until softened.
• Add garlic and cook for another minute.

[b]2. Add the Broth[/b]
• Pour in the chicken broth.
• Bring to a boil, then reduce the heat to a simmer.

[b]3. Cook the Noodles[/b]
• Add the noodles to the pot and cook according to package instructions (usually about 6-8 minutes) until tender.

[b]4. Add the Chicken[/b]
• Stir in the shredded chicken and season with salt, pepper, thyme, and parsley.
• Let it simmer for another 5 minutes.

[b]5. Serve[/b]
• Taste and adjust seasoning if needed.
• Serve hot and enjoy!

[h3]‪‪❤️❤️❤️ Please consider giving an award if you will cook this dish for your friends and loved ones. ❤️❤️❤️[/h3]

name: Three Sisters
review:
Three Sisters is genuinely unlike any game I've played before—it's strange, unsettling, yet oddly captivating. Right from the start, it immerses you into an eccentric world filled with bizarre visuals and characters who blur the line between humorous and unsettling. The artwork is distinctive, unsettlingly detailed, and strangely appealing despite its grotesque style.

Combat in Three Sisters feels chaotic and intense, pulling you into battles against uniquely disturbing foes. Every encounter feels impactful and rewarding, driving you to push forward despite the game's oddities.

The story initially seems random and absurd but quickly evolves into something deeply emotional and unexpectedly engaging. I found myself genuinely caring about the characters, each flawed and memorable in their own way.

In the end, Three Sisters left me reflective and intrigued, lingering in my thoughts well after finishing it. It's an unforgettable experience, balancing perfectly between disturbing and charming. Highly recommended for anyone seeking something truly unique.

name: Three Sisters
review:
I have seen the face of God, and it lies Three Sisters deep within a world of raw, unfiltered slop.

A world that was built by unseen hands that know only obsession, each sprite is meticulously crafted with a grotesque artistry that defies reason. Every frame pulses with some incomprehensible force, like staring into the abyss and seeing it wink back at you. And the music? Calling it “music” is like calling a house fire “warm lighting.” It’s a powerful schizophrenia soundscape of warped frequencies, distorted hymns, and screams torn straight from dying throats. These aren’t just noises, they mean something, even if you can’t understand what. It drills past your ears and into the soft, unguarded parts of your brain, resonating with the soul in just the ♂ right ♂ way. It shouldn’t work. None of this should work. But it does.

The game does not introduce you to its world. It throws you into it headfirst and expects you to keep up. And you will. Because how could you not? You’ll scramble through its unfamiliar, deranged, dangerous areas, grasping at whatever sense of reality remains, clutching onto any scrap of logic you can find. You’ll meet its inhabitants, freaks, weirdos, liars, drug addicts, men who should be dead and yet keep talking. You’ll listen to them. You’ll laugh at them. And you’ll wonder if maybe they’re laughing at you.

Then, there’s the combat. It’s more like a manic bipolar simulator of desperate violence, a 3 AM gas station drunken brawl between your party and the horrors that stand in their path. You fight things that should not be alive. Things with too many eyes, or not enough faces. Laughing things. Hungry things. Things that are in pain and would very much like you to feel the same. Every hit carries weight, every well timed strike lands with a satisfying crunch that makes your brain light up like a malfunctioning slot machine. The progression is intoxicating, with each fight another desperate claw forward, another hit of pure, uncut power. The game knows exactly how to keep you coming back, even when you swear you need a break. There is no break. Only forward.

And the story? Oh, the story. At first, it plays out like a bizarre, somewhat schizo, over the top edgy yet goofy comedy, it's pure chaos. You laugh, because of course you do, it’s funny. But then, something changes. The absurdity isn’t just absurd, it’s real. The game reaches into your chest and grips something tender, something real. The laughter doesn’t stop, but it starts to feel different. By the time you realize what’s happening, it’s already too late. You care. You care about these characters, these losers, these nobodies who somehow feel more real than anything else you’ve encountered in a game. They are yours.

By the end, I was sitting in the dark, staring at the screen, unsure of what I was even feeling. The credits rolled, but I wasn’t ready to let go. The game doesn’t end. It festers. It lingers. It grows. Like a parasite. Like a blessing.

Three Sisters should not exist. It makes no sense. It is grotesque, absurd, emotionally devastating, and weirdly beautiful. It is wrong in all the right ways.

I have never truly felt joy before this moment. I’ve got to go now. I hear the Uber Crabs calling. Deep down, I always knew this would happen.

Praise Billy.

name: Three Sisters
review:
Before writing my review, one question burned in the back of my mind, gnawing at me even as I lay in bed: What does it mean to be a "three-shitter"?

I couldn’t shake it. The thought kept resurfacing, demanding an answer.

So I picked up a copy of the hit video game, Three Sisters.

The game hooked me instantly with its wonderfully wacky sense of humor. I actually pointed at my screen when the malevolent pale crab told me to do horrendous things to myself.

The game’s music—crafted entirely from some of the most horrifying noises imaginable (yes, those are literally real dying screams in the soundtrack)—somehow delivers a uniquely euphoric experience that no other game has ever matched.

The combat is flawless! Every strike from Vibia and crew lands with bone-crunching satisfaction - you can practically feel the impact rattling your screen. What truly elevates it is the progression system: with each battle, you grow noticeably stronger, creating that perfect "just one more fight" addiction loop. It's absolute gaming crack.

The story starts as a goofy romp about misfits committing crimes, then suddenly suplexes your heart into the pavement with moments of raw, unflinching humanity. By the end, I was laughing through tears at 3 AM, emotionally compromised but utterly in love with this dysfunctional family of losers. It’s the perfect blend of absurd humor and genuine heart—like if Tarantino wrote a D&D campaign.

This game shouldn’t work. A soundtrack made of nightmares, combat that feels like breaking bones with a baseball bat, and a story that swings wildly between dick jokes and existential dread? Yet somehow, it all coalesces into something transcendent—a messy, beautiful masterpiece that burrows into your brain and refuses to leave. It’s crude, profound, broken, and perfect. I’ve never played anything like it, and I’ll be chasing this high for years.

name: Disc Jam
review:
[h1]Unfortunately the game's servers have been shut down so not all the mechanics are available anymore but you can still play online matches by connecting to player-hosted servers (also peer to peer maybe?)[/h1]



[h1]Rating[/h1]
7/10 (B-tier)
A very fun and competitive game with a high skill ceiling which is dragged down by a lack of variety and a tiny playerbase.

[h1]Details[/h1]
Disc Jam is a very fun, fast-paced and competitive multiplayer sports game.

[h3]Graphics[/h3]
It won't make you go "Wooow" but it is certainly well-made and doesn't look bad at all.

[h3]Performance[/h3]
It generally runs well and you can turn down the graphics quite a bit as well to improve it further if needed.

[h3]Music[/h3]
There are only three different tracks in the game so they do get stale after a while but they fit the game very well.

[h3]Sound[/h3]
The sound design is good.

[h3]Gameplay[/h3]
The matches can be very fun, especially when you're playing with your friends in 2v2. Disc Jam is easy to pick up but hard to master.
The controls are good but playing on keyboard is substantually harder than with a controller.
By playing random matches and voting on game streams, you can also earn an ingame currency. It can be used to get some nice random prizes which are cosmetics like skins, taunts and tags. This also provides another small reason for playing some more.
The characters are all very interesting and for the most part well-balanced with their personal strengths and weaknesses.

All in all I recommend this game because it is a lot of fun.

name: Disc Jam
review:
***
***Edit: The Online Servers are officially closed as of 30th September 2022. The game died a death but it was good while it lasted. It seems I've jammed my last disc, farewell partner. GBNF :(

[h1] Easy to learn, hard to master. [/h1]

This game is competitive, quick and deceptively skill-based with tight controls and satisfying effects. It shares similarities with Rocket League (in terms of the time matches take etc), but I'd say it's a lot easier to learn mechanics-wise. The matchmaking format works in the same way that Rocket League does with a point based ranking system, there are seasonal rewards based on your Rank in... ranked at the end of seasons.

If you want something quick to hop into when you've got an hour to burn, this is a good alternative to Rocket League and a breath of fresh air. It takes seconds to start a match, and matches tend to last around 5 minutes. The overall player count is somewhat low on PC, but Crossplay means that the playerbase is bolstered by PS4 players.

For less than £5 on sale, this is definitely worth a buy. It does contain micro-transactions, but they don't have a [i]huge[/i] bearing on the game (most of the unlockables are cosmetic, and you can earn everything with in-game currency without spending real dosh anyway).

One thing to note is the awful playercount for Matchmaking. A free weekend in January 2019 helped to bolster this, but it quickly returned to a small number of people playing. According to the Steam Charts, there is an average of less than 25 players a day. However the game currently supports cross-platform matchmaking, so the majority of your opponents will be PS4 players (No XBONE from what I've seen).

name: Disc Jam
review:
I highly recommend Disc Jam.  The game currently has 5 characters who have different attributes, 6 different servers worldwide and Twitch & Mix streaming integration that allows viewers to vote on matches.  You can also earn additional game currency if you have viewers.

The game itself is very good.  There are 2 game modes; 1v1 & 2v2.  You can play online multiplayer or local multiplayer but there aren't any bots so if you don't have a friend to play with and you can't find anyone online, you'll be confined to training mode which is a bit limited.

The games are best of 3 matches and it's usually over in about 5 minutes.  You earn a skill rating based on how many matches you win and the matchup system tries to match you up against players who have similar skill but the longer you wait for a match, the probability of facing an opponent who's either much better or much worse than you increases.

The main downside to this game is that the player base is pretty small and at times you might have trouble finding someone to play against.  Overall, this is a very fun game that's worth the price, in my opinion.

UPDATE - 05/18/18

As of Feb 2018, there is a single player mode called Ghost Arcade.  In this mode, the AI you play against is modelled after real players. The AI difficulty varies depending on your performance against them.

UPDATE - 01/11/19

There's a new 2v2 game mode called Disc Dodger.  The goal is to hit your opponents with the disc, while avoiding being hit by their throws.  It's a fun game mode and a nice alternative to the competitive ranked game modes.

UPDATE - 10/05/22

As of September 30, 2022 you can no longer play this game online.  The developers made the decision to no longer support online play, which is a bit odd since they specialize in netcode for real-time multiplayer games. (snapnet.dev)

The game had a very small player base and the developers were probably no longer making a significant profit from this game but completely abandoning the option to play online is unacceptable.  

A P2P multiplayer option could have replaced the servers, thereby, eliminating their server costs.  The game itself is great and for the most part, the developers have been reasonable and made good game play decisions so I will not change my review to negative.

However, it's not a good sign when a game developer simply stops supporting online play.  Usually, it's a sign that they will do the same thing with their future games.

There's no point in buying this game now as Ghost arcade and support for multiplayer via LAN does not justify the price.

name: Disc Jam
review:
The Good: A fun, solid spiritual successor to Windjammers that's easy to pick up and play and difficult to master

The bad: Built around a Overwatch/Rocket League model that requires multiple games to unlock anything, most of which are pallete swap skins, rarely used emotes, and easy to miss avatars/player titles. Or you can spend forever trying to get money to unlock the three extra characters. With a game built around online play, there aren't enough players to find a game regularly, except MAYBE on certain peak hours/days. You can play against ghosts of online players that somehow behave like the real players, so as long as you have some internet access, you can always play a game against bots, but without it, there's only offline couch co-op/Lan - I don't think there is offline bot play. These problems make it tough to recommend spending money on this game unless you get it on sale.

Oh, and it's one of those microtransactiony games, where you don't earn much out of standard play. The good news is that the highest cost currency bundle costs about $20, which I think will be enough to unlock the remaining characters and some loot boxes for skins and such, but i wouldn't be surprised if it didn't unlock everything. 

DiscJam is worth a purchase on sale, especially at less than $4. But the lack of players, near online-only requirement, and MTX model makes it tough to recommend @ full price.

name: Disc Jam
review:
So, it's kind of like tennis meets air hockey meets frisbee, and it's just as awesome as that sounds.

Firstly, the elephant in the room - there's not a massive playerbase right now, but there's certainly enough to be able to get a game at lower levels during peak times, and there's a mode that allows you play against a very tough AI, so if you're nervous about buying because you don't think you'll get a game, don't let that worry you too much.
As is sadly the case too often these days, a small vocal minority don't get a match within 30s of hitting the 'search' button on a Tuesday lunchtime, and instantly post a negative review claiming the game is dead, meaning people don't buy it because they think it's dead, and it becomes a self-fulfilling prophecy.

The game itself is fast, furious and clearly has a super-high skill ceiling, but it's simple enough that with an hour or two of play, you'll be having crazy back-and-forth see-saw rallys with your buddies that'll have you out of your seat.

There are a few minor annoyances - putting 'block' and 'throw' on the same button can result in you accidentally parrying the ball in the air rather than timing a perfect throw, and costing you a point in a rally you were on top of - considering there are two face buttons that aren't used at all, not to mention shoulder buttons etc, it seems silly not to at least give players the option of using one of them to avoid unnecessary frustration.

If you're a fan of Rocket League or similar sports-esque titles, I'd really recommend getting this. I picked it up on sale for £3, as I figured it was worth a look at that price - having now played it, I'd probably recommend it even at full price. A real hidden gem that deserves more people seeing it.

name: Disc Jam
review:
Great spiritual predecessor to Windjammers where you throw a disc and score goals in a manner similar to tennis. The game is really fun, but I have a few nitpicks: I doubt the microtransactions are bringing in much revenue at this point (and probably never were) and the online community is dead, so the developers should make it so everything is unlocked upon first launching the game. Also, when playing a private game with friends you don't return to the lobby post-game, meaning it's frustrating to play with friends since you cannot change teams/settings before each match.

I recommend it if you can buy it on sale and have some friends to play with. Content is slim and it's clear the developers are no longer doing basic upkeep, as there are still references to Mixer (a now dead streaming platform), and the most recent bulletin post is from March 2019; however, it's easy to pick up and games are quick, enjoyable, and intense.

Just bought it yesterday. Great game.

I used to love fantasy sport games, when companies were still making them (moment of silence for cyberball, pigskin, and mutant league). 

This one is pretty awesome. 

It's fast paced and high energy. I find myself shouting "ohhhh" when I miss the disc by a hair, or when I pull-off a tough win.  

Even with weak community, I'm very glad to have title in my library.

Worth the money for sure.

The  two things I think it needs:

- A storied career mode
- Easier unlocking of all the customizable bits
- More skins and such

It's fun to play a few matches at a time, but I don't want to play forever just to customize. And there aren't enough character options as it stands. 

Other than that, rock solid and hard to complain.

name: Disc Jam
review:
Disc Jam is a bit hard to review because the game is very bare bones, but what there is available is very good. The game is basically a mixture of tennis and air hockey where you try to score points by throwing a disc that bounces against walls into the opponent's court. The game, much like tennis, supports singles and doubles, but unlike tennis it also has a disc dodging mode that is more akin to dodgeball. The first two modes are the more interesting ones, but fun times can be had on all modes.

As for the game itself, I found it very addictive and relatively easy to pickup.  The game is mostly meant to be played online and, while many reviews state the game is dead online, I have not had the same experience. This may be because I allow for cross play with PS4 players, which are quite plentiful. In fact, I wait about 40 seconds or less to get into games. 

Also, there is a training mode and a mode called "Ghost Arcade" in which you play against bots that were made using previous player data. I find the AI in this mode is surprisingly challenging, to the  point that my score in that category is lower than my  best score in Season 1 (I ended up in gold)

In terms of potential grievances, characters and cosmetics need to be unlocked with in game currency that you earn by playing games. I would say that many may find the grind for in game currency to be unpalatable, and it will be needed to unlock 2 of the 6 available game characters. For reference, it took me a little over 10 hours t unlock one of the 2 characters, but I did spend about 10% of the characters cost on random cosmetics before realizing I needed the in game currency to unlock characters.

Another potential grievance is the perceived lack of courts, of which there are currently 2. My take on this is that tennis really has like 3 curt types, so it's not completely insane to have fewer court types.

Finally, one grievance I do personally have with this game is that the game doesn't have a "find next match" functionality,, s you can only either do a rematch or go to the main menu.

All in all, I think this game is well worth whatever I paid for it during the winter sale.

name: Disc Jam
review:
This is a game that was reported to be easy to learn and hard to master.  It is not.  I have played Rocket League for over 1550 hours at the time of this review; a game that takes precise inputs at precise times.  In comparison, to say that this game's time windows for inputs are small is an understatement.  They're absolutely miniscule.  I literally had to look up an explanation on how to get through the "advanced" tutorial ("advanced" because no game mechanics in a tutorial are advanced, advanced mechanics build from tutorial mechanics).  

In tutorial it said to swing your analog stick REALLY fast in an entire semicircle AFTER hitting X to throw the disc.  You match the floor arrows AFTER hitting throw.  I had to look up an explanation that literally said "Because the time windows are so small many people find it helpful to start the motion BEFORE you throw the disc."  Which was the only way I could possibly get it to work, and even then I couldn't get it to do the full 180 degrees semi-circle.  

That's tutorial.  Let alone in-game.  It should be noted that this game ONLY FUNCTIONS IF YOU ARE ONLINE.  So yeah, there's that.  But once you decide to go in-game, you get matched against someone else who promptly ROCKETS the disc around you 9 times in a row until you lose the whole game while they dance and taunt on the other side.  Fantastic first game experience.  Learning curve?  More like learning cliff.  

I bought this to BLOW OFF STEAM.  NOT to invest 100s of hours to have a moderate amount of fun in.  This game is only for try-hards.  And all of this is being said without mentioning the loot box fiasco.  

Stay away.  This is a joke.

name: Disc Jam
review:
Remember "Windjammers"? That's what they say, the folks who will tell you about this game.
"No." You'll probably say that in response.
Then they'll regail you with tales of a frisbee based competitive game in the arcades, fun and fast paced, simple and yet impossibly deep. They'll suggest you try Disc Jam.

They're right, you know. About everything. The game is very reminiscent, if shifted 90 degrees. The controls feel good, tight. The pace is spot on and the competition is exciting locally and online. Graphically it doesn't really stand out among the noise of other competitive local mp games that much (the characters are pretty milquetoast), but it has heart.

What it also has is lootboxes, and progression as slow as molasses. Don't come here looking for it to dole out rewards or points, or for the cool looking anything. Focus on the game, and you'll have a damn fine time. Recommended, for sure.

name: Disc Jam
review:
If you only play one Robot Frisbee Tennis game this year, make it Disc Jam.   It's a very polished and fun game.  As a fan of Rocket League I was naturally drawn to try this.  It's 1v1 and 2v2 matches.  You can team up or go it alone.  I had fun with regular matches but even more fun playing 1v1 with a friend.  It's cross platform. Also, you can invite and join friends even if you are not logged in to 'Steam Friends and Chat'   There are other game modes too.   

The tutorial could be improved.  Some tutorials tell you "Too early" or "Too Late" but for some, it says "Perfect" even if you are doing it wrong.  Tell us what we are doing wrong!!  The only other concern is the amount of people online has been low historically but it's recently spiked due to a promotional free weekend, something I suggest be done periodically.

review:
Fun to play, but it should definitely be free to play. Disc Jam is a nice competitive weird-version-of-tennis-but-isn't-tennis game, which makes it fun to play with a buddy in online 2 v 2's. The gameplay struggle this game has is desync and lag. Sometimes it clearly looks like a player is not in position to catch something, but somehow manages to(which throws off your reactions completely).

I would strongly suggest the creators of this game to make this f2p. It took a while to be put into matches during a free to play weekend, I'm estimating that queue times will take more than 5 minutes when it isn't. The cool part about this game is that it has crossplay to PS4, but I imagine it doesn't bring that many players either.

name: Disc Jam
review:
Played this game on a free weekend. It has the semblance of a good game but is buried under some questionable decisions.

Disc Jam is a cross between Windjammers and Lethal League with multiple tech and throw options. However, the problem is the window to do anything is ridiculously strict. I assume if I had more time with it, I could get the timing down but the problem there also is one of the buttons is both a throw and deflect move. What that means is when you think you've caught the disc, instead what you do is deflect it and it goes haywire and really messes you up instead of you just throwing it because you're trying to time a perfect throw with that button. Even if you remap that button, those commands will always go together. I would have preferred the deflect being on one of the triggers as both are a curve move that are influenced by the direction you're pressing and not what trigger you're holding down. Many of my opponents fell victim to this also and many points were scored by myself or the opponent attempting to do a perfect throw and instead, the shield making the disc flop to the ground and the other scoring because of that. Other times, there seemed to be lag where catching the disc would be extremely lenient in some cases.

There seems to be no quick play / casual mode. You are forced to play only ranked with other random players online. This might not matter to some but other players don't care about a medal on their profile and some could even be too coaxed up in what their rank is as opposed to just having fun. There are lobbies also but invite only.

The biggest problem I have is that this game is paid but also has loot boxes with paid currency. Sure, you can bring up the usual excuses of "it's just cosmetic", "it's optional" and "they need to make money somehow" but those arguments have been refuted numerous times (cosmetics are an incentive therefore you want to stand out and have good gear; it's either that or grind for ages; DLC packs are a much better option as you know what you're getting as opposed to paying money for currency and MAYBE getting what you want). There is the option of just buying stuff outright but two of the characters are 25k currency each. That's either hours and hours of grinding or £10. It's just iffy and I wish they monetized this in basically any other way.

Some positives though is that it does have an arcade mode (albeit not much added to it), the customisation is neat, having options for different discs and whatnot, the music is good and it has crossplay with the Switch and PS4 so if you know someone that might be interested in this but can't get it on PC.

I feel this game might be more fun with a friend for doubles but otherwise, nah. Not for me, unfortunately.

name: Disc Jam
review:
The idea and mechanics of this game are very interesting essentially it's a competitive frisbee game where you use lobs, bounces, quick shots and curve throws to score points by either hitting the back wall or hitting the ground in your opponents field. you can also use super shots that move in unpredictable ways by catching a disc in midair and returning it instantly or catching another supershot and returning it instantly and a perfect shot which moves faster by throwing a disc the moment you get it, you also have a shield which you can use to block shots upwards either to your field or the oponents depending on which direction you are moving allowing you to counter poorly made shots or set up supers for yourself, however the speed at which the discs move (especially super and perfect shots) feels way too high compared to your characters movement speed to make matters worse you also only have about a second-ish to throw your disc if you don't you'll just do a basic throw which clashes with the tacticle nature of your character abilities. to make matters worse curve shots require you to move the left stick in a specific motion like in fighting games which makes it even harder to pull off.

Over all I can see why people like this but it's just way too clunky and sluggish for me to recommend.

name: Disc Jam
review:
As a fan of the original Windjammers, I was really hyped to (casually) discover this game. A fast paced tennis-like with discs and skill moves, that can be picked up almost instantly but has lots of depth, is a good formula for a competitive game. Generally, it does plenty of things right and is quite entertaining. However, it's far from perfect.

First things I noticed immediately are the camera angle and the fast animations. The low camera angle is intuitive in making shots, but awkward to project incoming disc trajectories. Probably this is something I may get used to, but it's still a letdown. The animations on the other hand, are too fast, with too few in-between frames that give a cheap feeling.

The other major issue is matchmaking. I get it there are too few players, but at least make the experience a decent one. I have to manually start matchmaking at least 3-4 times to find a match. If one match drops, it should automatically restart and search for a new one.

Finally, the number of players. It looks like the game is slowly dying and that's a pity and loss of potential. I think a free-to-play model and some aggressive push into the competitive market would help, although maybe it's too late. Worth a try though.

name: Disc Jam
review:
Ok, I see a lot of mixed reviews. I really have to say I appreciate this game to the fullets because as of TODAY, i was JUST playing WINDJAMMERS in a retro arcade bar and I had no idea this game existed!

It does very well with mimicking the games mechanics and feel. It has the same clunky controls, lack of physics (aside from ragdoll) and fun special charged throws! What's amazing is that there are differnet characters that look and feel a bit different. 

The original Windjammers had a Strength and Speed ratio. It felt similar here.

Though only spending a bit of time in the game for free weekend, I have to say I can find myself loving this game but also fear the online games would have thin mortality and would be left with only friends to play with (which is cool, but it's not more than a casual game I think.) Doesn't seem to get too intense.

name: Disc Jam
review:
I remember playing this several months back and it was rough all the way around. Popped up today and I thought $5.99? What's the worst that could happen? Welllll......

While many more modes have been added since I last saw, including the most appreciated Training Mode, my biggest gripe was with the actual gameplay. The game looks fine for what it is, if a little on the bland side. The effects are bland, the character models are uninspired, the atmosphere is plain. The obvious wink to Windjammers is there and so is the allure however, Disc Jam seems to have the disc physics but the way it interacts with the 'player' is just wonky and clunky. Trying to pull off Wall Skips and other types of throws ends up with wildly misguided throws or the most aggravating 'disc to the back' on the return. In addition, it feels like catches that should be made fairly easily go sailing by resulting in the worst possible consequence (and it happens way too often as with the disc back bump...just dumb). Also, the disc seems unpredictable. I poof out ( the timing in this game is RAZOR THIN) a directional throw that doesn't seem to react at all as I had intended and not in a "I need to practice" way but more of "What in the blue crap just happened" way. There is just something "off" about this game. In closing, the game looks nice and controls nice (sometimes) but to be honest, I've seen a lot added to this game in every other facet of this game except the actual gameplay since the last time I picked this up (months ago). I'm going to give it more time but, in truth, I don't feel that motivated and games, in general, these days, can't be slow burn, learn to love it deals... especially one that takes it's very roots from one of the best pick up and play games of all time.  Easily skippable, but good for a few matches unless the gameplay is beefed up. I will give it a thumb's up (only because I don't feel people should avoid this game...yet)  but it is with a huge caution sign. I know this game can be better. WAY BETTER.

name: Disc Jam
review:
[h1] TL;DR Sum-up [/h1]
Disc Jam is desperately trying to play the modern-retro card by getting inspired by one of the best multiplayer games of the 90's, but trips over its own shoelaces and offers a limited experience with much locked content and few players online. 

Failing to really innovate, the game combines the flaws of its technicaly limited precursor with all the traps of modern game design. What could have been the next Rocket League ends up falling flat after a couple of hours, ultimately giving the feeling of a polished demo begging for more content, or a freemium in the middle of an existential crisis.

Fans of the genre might prefer getting it on sales, others may save their money. 
Not recommended.


[h1] TL;DR Sheet [/h1]

[table]
[tr][td][h1]In general[/h1][/td][/tr]
[tr]
[td][b]In one sentence: [/b][/td]
[td]Online Multiplayer Windjammer 2000[/td]
[/tr]
[td][b]Genre: [/b][/td]
[td]Tennis with a freesbee, in the future.[/td]
[tr]
[td][b]Playtime: [/b][/td]
[td]Till boredom[/td]
[/tr]
[tr]
[td][b]Get it for: [/b][/td]
[td]Windjammer Nostalgia, easy to pick-hard to master gameplay, multiplayer with friends.[/td]
[/tr]
[tr]
[td][b]Dont get it if: [/b][/td]
[td]You're expecting an extra oomph.[/td]
[/tr]
[tr][td][h1]Technically[/h1][/td][/tr]
[tr]
[td][b]Visuals, sound, tech: [/b][/td]
[td]6/10 - Could run perfectly on a Playstation 2. Extra point for the absence of bugs.[/td]
[/tr]
[tr]
[td][b]Art direction, music, acting[/b][/td]
[td]6/10 - Retro 90's style, polarizing and flashy. Terrible music. SOMEONE KILLS THIS HORN.[/td]
[/tr]
[tr]
[td][b]General experience, gameplay: [/b][/td]
[td]6/10 - Unfortunately under populated servers make online play a tad frustrating. Some design choices are fine, some are a catastrophe. Fun for 2 hours, then falls flat.[/td]
[/tr]
[tr]
[td][b]General rating, worth the price?[/b][/td]
[td]4,5/10 - Wants to get there, but knocks itself down on the way. Feels more like a polished demo than a full title. Fans of the genre might enjoy it on sales.[/td]
[/tr]
[/table]

[h1]The good, the bad, the meh[/h1]

[u]The good[/u]

+ Nostalgia
+ Novelty
+ Curve control system
+ Some intense rallies
+ Bug free

[u]The Meh[/u]

= Generic characters
= Generic arenas
= Little customisation options

[u]The bad[/u]

- Horrendous music
- Frustrating "Self Knock Down" system
- THAT BLOODY HORN
- Rather depopulated servers
- Way too much locked content

[h1]Full Review[/h1]

Year 3029, when humankind will be bored of balls and racket, and when extreme freezbee champion Gusto Gliderszky will be universally hailed as president of the United Planets, mankind will enjoy a sport very similar to Disc Jam. But it won't be Disk Jam. 

Just like Disc Jam this sport will be based on Data East 1994's WindJammer.
Just like in Disc Jam, you will throw a heavy disk of metal and badassium at your opponents, waiting for an occasion to get it passed them or lob it where they know they can't possibly run to. 
Just like Disc Jam, you'll climb the ranks hoping to become the point of focus when you reach the ultimate step of the ladder and sing Queen in your mind, so loudly your neighbors hear it. 

Unlike Disc Jam though, it will feel like a whole, finished, fun experience. 

See, DJ's gameplay includes a lot of good idea, especially the devilishly precise curve control feature which, coupled with a shield that leaves you immobile but can help you charge super throws, gives you the choice to simply rally like a ferret on crack or strategize your way to victory. The UI is also good enough, and the arcade ghost mode where you face simulations of actual players is a welcome addition. 

To be honest, every aspect of the game is almost great. 

Unfortunately, this 'almost' is what turns the title into an underwhelming experience. 

To begin with, the tension of the rallies often gets interrupted by knock-downs which occur when the disc hits a player in the back. This mechanism did work in Windjammer, where the arenas were minuscule but, here, where much more running happens, knock-downs feel more random than deserved. Why athletes would decide to turn their back on a killer disk hurled full speed in their direction is beyond me in any case.

Then there is the arena. The one, lonely arena in which you will play all your matches, which is devoid of spectators, and comes with a music that will make you regret the whole concept of having eardrums. A foghorn also blares every time a player scores. This foghorn is, in itself, an invitation to self harm. 

Speaking of harm... the only way to knock down your opponents is to wait for them to get hit in the back. Yes. Disc Jam is a futuristic sports game without any way to brutalize your opponent, which feels pretty much the same as playing paintball with a squirt gun.

And then there is the 'retro-modern' angle. The title desperately tries to pay tribute to its older brother by clumsily integrating 90's musical and visual style (which everyone is trying their best to forget), but loses the immediate fun of the 90's arcade (which everyone always welcomes back) by locking away a huge amount of content behind either grind or microtransactions. In the end, Disc Jam can't really pick an identity and suffers from it, both conceptually and technically.

Online matches could be a redeeming factor, but even with PS4 crossplay, too few players are online to enjoy a descent skill curve, or simply a hypothetical tense and fun rally.

[h1]Verdict[/h1]

Disc Jam is desperately trying to play the modern-retro card by getting inspired by one of the best multiplayer games of the 90's, but trips over its own shoelaces and offers a limited experience with much locked content and few players online. 

Failing to really innovate, the game combines the flaws of its technically limited precursor with all the traps of modern game design. What could have been the next Rocket League ends up falling flat after a couple of hours, ultimately giving the feeling of a polished demo begging for more content, or a freemium in the middle of an existential crisis.

name: Disc Jam
review:
Great game with great potential, really fun both in 1v1 and 2v2 modes. Played it in the free weekend a while ago and thoroughly enjoyed my time with it. However, as fun as it is, the gameplay itself is very simple, (altrough that could be fixed by simply adding elements to it like different arenas) and the 15 eur price tag feels like a bit too much for what it is right now.

I don't usually do reviews, and this is more of a plead to the devs but I feel compelled to speak up here because I don't want it to die - and it is basically one foot in the grave already. There are not enough people playing it and the main reason is probably the price tag. Change your model to be skin based, lower the price by like a half or even 2/3rds, do whatever you feel is best, but do something. 

Way more people will be willing to throw that on the table for what can, on first glance be percieved as some simple couch controller fun, only then to realize how fun the game really is, and what do you know, as these things pick up the skins or whatever will start selling aswell. Currently it is stuck in a loop of "noone wants to throw out 15 eur for a game they have never heard about, when for around the same price point you can get so many other already established games, even in the same category, e.g. rocket league. And those (myself belonging to this group) who have gotten a taste of it and would be willing to pay, wont because they know the game peaks at like 10 players, so it is not worth it, unless you have a lot of friends you can convince to jump in the hole with them. 

Again, only posting this out of love, after months since the free weekend, because the game itself is a lot of fun and I don't want to see it die. I never wrote anything back then because I honestly was expecting something to be done about it in the foreseeable future. I am not a game dev, but I really wonder why nothing HAS been done about it. Is it really better to let it die as it is,  just because of what seems to be stubborness or giving up hope, when I can't see it not succeeding if only it would be more open to people giving it a chance.

name: Disc Jam
review:
Mechanically, the game is fantastic. The amount of different shots you can throw due to the slick controls is amazing. The concept is brilliant althought technically it has been done before (OG windjammers) I think that Disc Jam is the superior game of the two, the "from the back" viewpoint suits the game much better and allows for true 3d.
 The game suffers from 2 major problems.
 1: Lack of content. There is a training mode, offline vs bots/local player singles or doubles, and online singles or doubles. That's it. And every single one of them is pretty much exactly the same. No interesting new arenas or new mechanics or mutators.
 2: Lack of players. I think this is directly related to problem 1. Although the player count has improved slightly since I last played around 10 months ago I suspect this is mainly down the the game being free on PS+, seriously the peak players on steam last month was 17. And most days had a max of 10 at peak time. Also, PS4 players seem to be steamrolling the stats board. 
 This tells me that the game is really popular with most people who try it, but nobody is willing to blindly commit money to it. Which is a huge shame as, even with its shortcomings, this is a fantastic competitive Esports title. I don't know why the devs don't just make it f2p and work solely on new skins/customisations. Would certainly bring new PC players to the game and more than likely get a lot of currency purchases. Something has to happen soon though as the devs won't be able to afford dedicated servers for a game that's not making money somehow.
 Even with the utter lack of meaningful content, this game is a gem, and best played doubles with a friend IMHO. The lack of content is also reflected in the price somewhat, its only £11 at full price, I have 40 hours in it, and would have a hell of a lot more if I didn't stop playing at the height of playerbase troubles that's less than 60p per hour of entertainment. Anyone who enjoys 2v2 reaction + skill based esports really should give it a go.

name: Disc Jam
review:
Had fun for a bit during the free period. Scratches that Windjammer itch, but found the lack of courts and player characters dissappointing. On top of that it has microtransactions and lootboxes.

[b]EDIT for Camaflooge:[/b]
After receiving your reply I thought I'd clearify a little.

I love Windjammers a lot. The amount of money that went into that cabinet could buy me a much better PC than I have today. Sadly 13-old me didn't realise that back then, but I know exactly how it feels and plays.

First let me tell you what Disc Jam does right. Just like Windjammers it's fun to play. You can easily spend 30 min or a month on this game. You might get a bit frustrated, but never really bored. For this game to be made by only 2 developers is impressive in and of itself. 

However, I still wouldn't recommend Disc Jam. It might give you the same feeling as Windjammers, it just doesn't play as good. It'll need improvement for me to really recommend as a must-buy and I'll try to tell you exactly why.

The skill in both games comes down to "Stand in right place, Push button on time, add curve". It's a fun cycle that has a real sense of speed and rythm when done right. In Disc Jam I'd sometimes press everything exactly right, the game even displayed it's blue "perfect" and curved arrows, only for the character to refuse to let go of the disc.

The court didn't help either. Your character isn't capable of crossing more than half the court and can really easily get baited. A save tactic is to always stay centered which leaves you open for slow tosses. Strong characters can forget reaching a slow-toss in time, but fast character might still have a chance. If reached in time you can return it, maybe even as a superthrow, but allas, you can't run a half-court AND be on time to fully charge your superthrow. 

Thus, most matches ended trying to bait and curve the disc around each other without real speed or a single Superthrow. Resulting in the fast characters feeling sluggish and the strong characters being useless.

With my personal opinion on the gameplay out of the way I'd like to go into the lootboxes and microtransactions.

There are 6 character in the game with 2 characters needing to be unlock with Js-points. The Js-points are earned from playing OR you can pay real money for then at the Price machine. 

The original loot of the Price machine might be only cosmetic, but at the same time you can pay real money to unlock 2 characters that can potentially give you an edge in-game. 

If that's your thing, go for it, just don't paint it over with the "It's ok, it's just cosmetics"-brush.

[b]EDIT for Dragonheart_420:[/b]
The graphics are nice. More courts and characters would be a big plus.

[b]EDIT for Crisilex:[/b]
"The game might not be perfect" is exactly the point I'm making. 

If I need to git gud before I can enjoy it a game, than maybe it's not for me (and the 46 people that found the review I made helpful). It might also mean that the game itself needs to git gud before I'd recommend buying it.

[b]Conclusion[/b]
While I played it the question was "would I spend money on it and recommend it to friends" and the answer is No, I would not.

name: Disc Jam
review:
TL:DR
Sadly i won't recommend at this point in development. There are to few features to justify the price.


I've played 73 matches since yesterday as part of the free weekend promotion. 
doubles matches are really fun, if you have a decent partner and opponents (rarely seen though, I blame the free weekend).

I find Disc Jam has a nice premise, and could develop into a very entertaining game with time.
The core gameplay (the matches) work very well and run fluently, although a few more moves would be a nice addition. It is easy to pick up, but hard to master, as a game of this type should be.

Although the Devs look very active and are engaging with their community, when implementing the request that come from their player base. The game has been released to early in development in my oppinion. 
The game could have benifitet from Early access at this point, simply because there are still a lot of secondary features missing and tweeks being made to primary features, with the help of the community.
A lot of people might be put of by this and never return, because what they expect when buying a released game, is a finished or almost finished pruduct.

ATM what we have is a fun party game suited for when you are having friends over and want to give them a beating with homefield advantage. This is emphasized by the fact that the game is also avaible for PS4 (The ultimate beat up you friends console)
The game could be so much more though, with an online league system that is more engaging then what we have.
When playing it seems like the only point of that system is to pair you off with players roughly with the same skills as you. But it fails at that somehow. I've had 50-0 matches both for and against me.
A singleplayer league with skill progression would definately increase the longevity of the game as well, and bring in a bigger player base.

I played Disc Jam during ALFA some time ago, and i must say that a lot has happened since then. 
Most important of all is stability. During my matches these couple of days I have only had one opponent drop connection on me, and that was during the first serve, compared to one in two doing so midgame when playing the ALFA.
This must be due to dedicated servers which have now been implemented.

One thing that is harder to fix is the time it takes to find a match. Even though the game is free right now. I have had times where it felt like ages before finding an opponent. And when that opponent declines a rematch and you have to sit and wait again for another opponent, it just becomes tedious very quickly.

These two Devs have really been doing a nice job. And I hope they will continue to do so. So that I can return at some point and change my review to recommended.

name: Disc Jam
review:
A fun sports title that you can play for 5 minutes or a couple hours. It has that addictive "one more game" thing going for it. It's easy enough to learn, but takes a while to get pretty good at. Once you do though, the games are pretty intense and it feels pretty fun, almost like a Rocket League version of Pong. I say Pong because your main goal is to get the disc into the endzone behind your opponent(s), so it's not quite like tennis.

The game isn't perfect though. Sometimes the connection is a bit laggy to other players, but it wasn't a big issue during my playtime. Right now there's a decent amount of people playing, but I have also seen a lot of the same players. I'm not sure how easy it would be to get a match after the free weekend though. There is an option to purchase in-game currency, but it's the same currency you can earn by playing. I've already unlocked 1/2 of the locked characters, and bought quite a few of these gacha balls that unlock skins, titles, victory poses, etc. just by playing the game.

Overall I'm not sure I'd pay $14.99 for it, but at it's current sale price of $8.99, I'm heavily considering it.

name: Disc Jam
review:
I have to admit, the game is very fun. The major downside is that it seems sorta like a polished beta. What I mean by that, is that there isn't much to unlock. Few characters to choose from, and one court which seems a little ridiculous. There really needs to be more courts. That would easily make the game feel like it has more depth. A simple change in scenery can go a long way. Given these two downsides, they don't (imo) take away from the quality of the game mechanics, that in turn determine whether or not this is a game to be recommended. So I do recommended the game, but only when its on sale. $17(CAD) seems pretty steep for a price with the lack of content. Pick it up while its on sale and you will have a blast.

There are just so many other games that are cheaper then this(at reg price), that have way more art assets, and much deeper game mechanics. For this to be worth almost $20 it needs to have alot more effort put into the courts and character selections. Like even three courts would do wonders for this game, even if they were just simple texture changes. Heck even a greenscreen room where every thing was just green would be more fun then having just one court.

End result: Buy it on sale you will have a blast, buy it full price only if more courts and characters get added.

name: Disc Jam
review:
Full of superpower stuff for noob players. 
Most times you cant see where the disc is going because the persepective and the camera are very bad.
The speed of the characters is too slow.
The dash is quite bad in some characters.

It's a fun game if you just dont care about it and go into it to play a few matches but if you go into ranked its just trash, you get into matchmaking with guys that throw badly that are clearly not at your level and then you get laggers and then you get the guy that only goes for the super shot(wich is absurd and shouldnt be in the game, at least you shouldnt be able to use it every single time..). It's basically always you vs someone way better or you vs someone that is complete trash, almost never you get a match that its actually same level vs same level. (I was like 200 on the singles list(PC) (500-450 global I think) yesterday, with almost 70% win ratio,in just 3 hours, so I'm not bad at the game. So avoid those bland comments)

The controls... sometimes works sometimes doesnt, how you control the disc is cool but 50% of the time just doesnt care about what movement you did on the joystick and just invents something that is similar. It wont destroy your match but can be frustrating.

The game itself is quite ugly, they clearly didnt spend much time tweeking post-processing effects and sometimes, randomly, just falls to 20-30 fps(from 60) and you can lose a few points because of this.

Aside of online and a bad single player vs AI mode, there's not much to do in the game. You might want to pick it up for local mode, that's good at least.

Buy it cheap if you want the game, but I dont recommend the game at full price. Needs to much work to be as fun as Rocket League is(obvious inspiration for the game).  It also has some kind of gambling system in it but uses ingame points, so there's that.

name: Disc Jam
review:
UPDATED!   After 90+ hours of play. Review still holds solid. Excellent Game. I still play every week, mainly in doubles with  friend.  I've updated the review a bit. Shorter, more precise.   


Disc Jam, a mix of badminton, frisbee and  PONG...that's right m***er f****ers.  PONG.

Devs say Tennis but nah I don't believe , in Tennis the ball can hit the ground...not in Disc Jam. Le Disc cannot touch the floor.


I will keep this review to the point.  

-  I played the game on a Free weekend, bought it for 8 $ cnd, it was half off.
-  I have now  90+ hours on this game at this time. 
-  I've played   215+   matches so far.   Game tracks stats  :)  
-  Only 1 map / court. It works fine. Developers have worked on better servers and such instead of a new court.  That is a good decision IMO, game works well.  New character added since launch :)


Pros

- Game is based on Skill. You play, you learn, you get better.    You  do  NOT  buy anything whatsoever that gives you an edge.

  It is purely skill based. You have no ability trees, no points to add, you just learn how to play and play.  Get better and learn.


- You  unlock asthetic stuff like costumes etc.  Cool stuff to unlock, tags, emblems, disc models (look only) 

- The developer is active on Steam.

- Very good server match making!  I wait from 0 - 3 minutes for a match.   US East / US West / Europe Queue.   

UPDATE:  Feb 8th a patch was released and Nintendo Switch version is out. 

- The game tracks a TON of STATS!  Matches played, Overall rank, Individual shot type ranking (curve, perfect, super, lobs), 
Win %  and a lot more!   You can split it from PC to Global view (cross platform). I'm currently  4th in Lobs on PC.  #selfbump

-  IT HAS CO-OP!    IT   HAS   CO-OP!         I've played approx 50+  Doubles matches,    INCREDIBLE FUN! ((many more doubles now after 90+ hours))

- Excellent stress relief 

====

Cons

- PC players are strongly recommended to use a controller. If you have an Xbox, plug the remote into the PC, if you have a PS3 / PS4 there is legal, safe software online that you can install to make those remotes work with PC games. Google is your friend.

** I played a good 20 matches with keyboard and while it was fun, with controller, it felt much more natural, optimised.**

I cannot think of any other Cons. 

==

Now my last point!

Read this review and watch the gameplay videos, I believe you'll enjoy it!

I recommend this game 10/10

name: Disc Jam
review: Apparently I have been playing frisbee the wrong way. This not only has a point but does not require a field that the frisbee will still eventually fly out of. This costs $2.00 less than my last wham-o professional disc and this comes with people to play with. As for the game, the types of throws and the way to control the disc's intended flight path are outstanding. The throwing is simple yet able to perform complex curves and arcs at varying heights, angles and velocities. You play singles or doubles with opposing teams on either side of a net. It looks a bit like tennis in that respect. You serve (throw) the disc and the fun begins. From here the gameplay reminds me of raquetball if raquetball had a net instead of a wall. There are no "out of bounds" on the sides as the dics bounces off the walls of the court you are playing on.  I have no idea if the disc reaches or bounces off the ceiling but since typing this made me think of that, I will try it and keep the answer to myself. I wish I had a real one of these courts and discs in which to play fetch with my canine friend. Back on topic, this is a sport that I did not know I wanted to play and I am thankful that Mr. Timothy Rapp of High Horse Entertainment was nice enough to let me give this a go.  This is recommended to everyone who had, has, or is thinking about a frisbee. This is also recommended to anyone who thinks Badminton needs upgrade. This is also recommended to Steve Rodgers, he will understand why. I have to also recommend this to fans of "breakout" and "arkanoid" type games although though there is no breaking nor arkanoia there are angles and reflections to use while throwing the disc. If you haven't checked DiscJam out, you are doing it wrong.

name: Disc Jam
review:
[UPDATE: 09/17/2017]

The developers have effectively killed and trashed this game. Do not support them with your money.

Content such as new maps, gamemodes, etc. were promised and never delivered. Instead the devs added microtransactions for the barebones content currently in the game. They try to justify this by adding a single character in the seven months since release and then locking it behind a massive grind-wall telling people "you can just skip it by purchasing it with your money."

Disgusting development practices such as this should not be encouraged, and it disappoints me to see what could have been a great game go to waste.

--

[OUTDATED REVIEW]

I've been playing since the alpha released for this game last year and since then I've seen it go through some major changes, updates, etc.

The biggest thing I think I can compliment is how responsive the devs are to suggestions. I don't think I've ever seen devs who worked this closely with the community to bring the players what they want and adapt to suggestion the players might have regarding features or balance changes.

As you can see, I have (as of this review) a couple hundred hours in the game already. Even though it looks simple in concept, the game has a ton of replayability that will keep bringing you back for more. That's not even including the future maps, modes, etc. that the devs have already stated they intend to add.

The game play is very similar to an old airhockey-esque game called Windjammers. The goal is simple, throw the disc into the enemy's goal and you score. First to 50 wins the set, and the first to two sets wins the match. Much like Rocket League, this game is very easy to pick up, but also extremely difficult to master. You'll find yourself destroying opponents left and right, only to turn around and get stomped and wanting to improve so you can be even better than the person who beat you.

For only $15 ($10 as of the review because of a sale) this game is a steal and everyone should be sure to pick it up.

review:
[h1]The Game Has Changed, Son Of Flynn[/h1]
Disc Jam is a multiplayer arcade sports title that pits 2-4 players against each other in a unique combination of air hockey, tennis and Disks of Tron. Players scramble to retrieve and throw a glowing disc while unleashing devastating abilities and defending variable-point end zones

[h1]Maximize efficiency, rid the new world of its imperfection![/h1]
"Disc Jam" has the right formula to become the next big breakout indie sports game. Although the matchmaking will need to improve and new content delivered regularly to prevent it from becoming just a passing fad. The devs at High Horse are addressing to expand the game. But with a high fun factor and strategic nature, despite simplicity, "Disc Jam" delivers.
[h1]Playing all the angles. End Of Line.[/h1]

review:
I have no intention on writing anything about how the game plays or functions. What you need to know is how the developers run thier studio. This is a group of people who have distributed thier game with a charity drive event only to use it for free advertising and then took the game back from the supporters.

This game was distributed with the Yogscast Jingle Jam Humble bundle, a Charity drive event where 50K+ people donated to support multiple diffirent charities in the month of december(30$ for 50+ games was the bundle this year). The developers distributed thier game with this bundle only to remove the game from everyones library a few weeks later when the game was released claiming that the keys for the people that bought the bundle were "Alpha Only". They removed the game from the supporters library to get them to re-purchase it on release. Which caused the community to ignore them and refuse to play thier game. They had problems on release of "not enough people playing" for that reason, they took the game from 50K+ people and ruined thier opening playerbase. They had to create cross platform support because the only people who had the game were on playstation because they got it for free from playstation plus. Then they waited several monts(7) and had a "free weekend" deal when they figured everyone who had seen thier behavior had either forgotten about how they abused a charity drive for free advertisement then tried to make people repurchase the game, or stopped paying attention to what they were doing.

Do Not Support this kind of behavior in any human ever.

name: Disc Jam
review:
I'm amazed at how well the game is balanced. 9/10.
Would've given 10/10 if there was more stuff and sometimes the lag is so bad you think the other player is hacking.
PROS:
*Fun game. Never bores you out!
*Amazing visuals.
*Optimized even for potato PC's
*Stress relieving, easy to learn and play but takes considerable time to master
*Has cross play with consoles so finding a game was never been easier.
*Has 2 player , local.

CONS:
*Music is reapeatetive.
*Once the skill level is high, you will turn salty if you're weak.
*Single game type but will be improved in the not too distant future or I hope so.
*Had blisters from trying to spin.
*A joystick is a must for PC players

9/10 Would want to get blisters again.

name: Disc Jam
review: I picked it up because of the weekend trial and within ten minutes, I became disillusioned. I expected the game to be a balance of competitive casualness, something akin to Rocket League. What I got was a fun premise with the execution of a Cartoon Network flash game. As soon as the game loaded, I'm told the game is optimized for Xbox controllers, an early death knell. Keyboard controls are obstrusive, a fatal error for a game on Steam, a market exclusively for PC. For what it's worth, the game does a lot of things first. It's the first game I played to lose local connection in Training Mode, the first to have a long loading screen for the main menu after a match, the first game where keyboard control schemes hobble players,  the first to have a poorly implemented tutorial. For keyboarders, there are only three possible directions for the disc to be thrown (A W D). Good luck throwing that disc with any sense of accuracy or precision if you don't roll controller. Controllers have five possible directions due to sticks. This game really doesn't favor this market and would be better off exclusively on consoles.
