name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Should this game be Steam Deck Verified? No. A bad control scheme and broken menu make this a frustrating experience.

The controller scheme is confusing and needs to be reworked in full. OK, so you press start to access the menues that let you restart or exit a level. I figured this one out by randomly mashing buttons because there's no UI cue. But first of all, once that's done, it's not obvious that I'm in that mode. Second of all, how do I navigate this? No one tells you it's done with the dpad. Third, how do I exit this back to normal editing mode? The only way I've found is pressing RB twice to enter and exit simulation mode, which is not obvious and seems like the wrong thing to do. After some guessing I figured out you press start again. Pressing B, like any other controller based game, should put you in the game's normal mode as well.

The clear solution to this problem is to have an actual start menu, rather than just focus the cursor on the edge UI elements. But the game devs probably don't want to do that because the game, designed for mouse from the start, has been how it is for ages, so why change now, for a completely different control scheme? Just cram the wrong UI into this with an awkward control scheme that makes no sense.

Additionally, why is it left trigger to zoom in and right to zoom out? It's literally the opposite of every other game that does this. Right is forward since the very beginning of games that used controllers with triggers, because that's how car games did it, and it's become the standard. Brige Consctructor does it backwards, and there's no configurability.

Previously, I said:
In the main menu, if you click the medal icon, you just get a black screen. I don't know what's supposed to be there. Achievements maybe? Who knows. Same if you click on the "More bridges" icon. Clearly saying this game is "Steam Deck Verified" is BS since the testers didn't even test the /main menu/.

However, it seems that either Bridge Constructor hung up my Steam UI, or it crashed on its own. Here's what happens after you reboot:
Achievements (?) button: you get an error page: "the specified profile could not be found" - because Bridge Constructor thinks my profile name is "0".
More bridges button: you get brought to the store page in the browser. Why not just show you the game if it's already in your collection? That would be preferable.

Finally, the music is short and annoying. Listening to the same short guitar loop coming out of a sampler, played mechanically by a robot sewing machine, can be infuriating around the 100th time you hear it. You sold half a million units of this game, and another million probably of DLCs, just buy some better music, there are infinite music producers out there. Someone will record something nice for you, on actual real instruments, just pay them fairly.

The graphics are... fine. If I were playing this on a large TV, I'd be saying they're getting long in the tooth, but they're fine for a hand held. This game isn't about graphics, but it would be /nice/ to have graphics that don't look primitive. Yes, the game is from 2013. Those are 2013 graphics. It doesn't mean they can't be updated, or that they couldn't have been, eh, more artistic back in 2013. We got a lot of visually stunning games in 2013. Imagine building a bridge over a ravine, or in Bavaria with the Alps in the background, or between two tropical islands with a beautiful sunset, or around the white cliffs of Dover. Now that would be something. Imagine if the areas had some life to them. A farmer with cows in the background, some wild life, a fun fair, etc. The graphics don't have to be bare-bone.

I've been playing bridge construction games on and off since the very first ones came out - but the experience on the Steam Deck ain't it.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Bridge Constructor, like the title suggests, is a game where you build bridges across gaps to let vehicles pass. It’s played on a grid, drawing lines to create and connect small sections of various materials. The catch is that you have a resource limit, so you need to build a bridge under that limit. Materials also have different costs and depending on the level only a few are available. Not only that, but there are also 3 difficulty levels, represented by different crossing vehicles with more weight.
That sums up the gameplay. Different levels have different layouts and restrictions, and there’re lots of them. The core game has 40 levels. A free expansion adds another 24 levels, where you need to build inclined bridges, which is harder than the main game. On top of that, 3 updates added another 21 levels, themed around Christmas, Easter, and Halloween, also with inclined bridges. Finally, a paid expansion is also available, with 18 levels where the vehicles are now trains, so the bridges need to be extra robust. So, you can have 100+ levels in this game, which is a good amount of content.
Every level is scored, with leaderboards for clusters of levels. However, there are some annoying things. There’s a lot of RNG involved, meaning that the same bridge can or can’t work sometimes, so following guides for high scores doesn’t always work, for example. To unlock the hard difficulty, you need to beat the level on normal, so you need to play all levels at least twice for 100%, and the solutions aren’t always the same. For example, you have extra resources after unlocking the hard difficulty.
Also, the game lacks some polish. For example, you can’t choose between a bridge and support section, it’s automatic, and sometimes you want that control. Or you can’t connect a new section to the middle of another one; you must destroy that section, split it in half and then connect to that split point. Things like that. It’s also an obvious mobile port.
While Bridge Constructor could’ve been more polished, it works and it’s a decent game, if you like building games. You can often find it discounted or in bundles, including the Trains DLC as well. In that case, it’s a good deal, considering the amount of content in here. At full price though, there are other options, like Poly Bridge.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
This game makes you feel like an engineer and like a dad playing his little construction game, having a great time with it. There is some strange beauty in building bridges. The way how a plan works out is a great and fun experience. Learning how the mechanics work, how to tweak and optimize the construction, being the designer is an interesting cool thing. I like it a lot and find myself coming back to it! More than I thought I would!

While the vehicles drive over the bridge you can see your beams coloring more reddish for you to know which part of the bridge holds the most stress at this moment. The first beam which breaks needs support, so redo and try again. This is done very quick and is fluent with the gameplay loop.

Four different materials which are not always available make it very interesting. Plus you have a budget and need to build within this limit. Every material costs different so management and a good strategy are involved too. 

There is much content! The main game has 40 levels which can be designed for different difficulties holding cars, trucks or tank trucks. The free add-ons SlopeMania (24), Xmas (8), Easter (8), Halloween (5) up the level count with 45 plus there is a paid train DLC with 18 levels raising the total levels to 103. Regarding that you need to build different bridges for the tank trucks for every level but the train DLC it's 188 bridges to build total. It's much and diverse content. Every level has its own twist and needs special solving!

Negatives I can think of are:
- Repetitive monotone music. The track does not change during one level. I would have liked more diverse music or more different tracks. You can spend much time on building one bridge and hearing only one track can get unpleasant.
- A bit unconvenient controls with mouse. Controller works better though.
- The physics engine is not consistent. Testing your exact same design twice can have two different outcomes.
- The physics engine has problems if you build very short beams. They start to wiggle and shake a lot for no reason. This raises the total stress on them, so that they can endure less when a vehicle drives over it. Fortunately you mostly want to build long beams than several short beams, because that's better for the budget.
- You can only see the total stress with the beams coloring more reddish, but you can not see if it's compressive stress or tensile stress (pulling). Thinking about the structure may give you the answer but it's not always plain to see. I would have liked tensile stress to be blue while compressive stress is red. But there is a workaround: You can observe in which way the nodes drift off once the beam broke. If they drift away from each other it was too much tensile stress, if they drift towards each other it was too much compressive stress. This may come in handy as ropes can only carry tensile stress and no compressive stress.

Overall this is a very good game in my book and draws my attention more than I thought it would. It's really interesting and educational while fun to play! Very recommended! (100% possible in 26 hours.)

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Great game. I hope there will be a new version with more details, helping me not only play but also learn more knowledge about bridge construction. I would like to contribute ideas for the next version as follows:

1. There is an additional section for instructions, detailing the types of materials, for example: what type of wood is being used, size (length, width, height), weight/m3, bearing capacity horizontally/vertically etc. similarly have detailed specifications for iron, steel wire, concrete pillars, it would be great to add bamboo or other types of wood.

2. There is information about the weight of the vehicles.

3. There are tools for players to customize:
a) Manually adding new materials and specifications (name, weight, cost, bearing capacity, etc.).

b) Customize new vehicles.

c) Create custom maps.

Players created materials, vehicles, and maps can be shared through the workshop, they are only used for them to explore and challenge each other (in sandbox mode) and will not be scored.

4. There are more options for vehicles to cross the bridge when there is an earthquake or high wind (with earthquake levels from 1-7 and wind strength from level 1-12), or multiple vehicles moving on the bridge at the same time. (instead of only 1-2 cars like the current version). At this time, the bridge will shake more violently.

5. Players can optionally display (or pay) to see more detailed information of the bridge such as: weight of the bridge deck after construction is complete, load capacity, vibration resistance , which displays force vectors (as arrows) to see exactly what happens when a vehicle passes.

Overall, I hope the game can be more detailed and realistic, so that it is no longer just a game, but will become a software that construction students can use to learn.

In order for it not to become boring, the game can be divided into many modes, the campaign mode will be the same as it is now, not too difficult to play and should be more interesting in the scenario, the sandbox mode includes the content custom as I stated.

review:
The game consists of building bridges with a set of available materials while keeping to a tight budget.
As far as gameplay is concerned, the game fares well to what you expect from the short description I've given.

Sadly, there is no explanation to how the physics of the game work besides "triangles are cool" or whatever. This leaves me oftentimes going online looking at real-life bridges and methodologies to build them, just to realize that most of this is not properly implemented into the game.

If the game had a short explanation of real-life bridge physics, teaching you how to build the basic models and then letting you create hybrid models for the more difficult levels, it would have been both fun and educational. As it stands now, the game is just a frustrating experience.

TL;DR Do not buy the game if you expect to get any explanation on how to actually implement the game's physics.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
[h1]Fun for what it is, but not what it appears to be[/h1]

Let's get one thing clear: this is not a bridge construction simulator. This is a puzzle game that has bridge-building as its conceit. What's the difference? A bridge construction simulator will let you build boring, practical, realistic bridges, and then challenge you to cut corners and make death-defying monstrosities (or go to the other extreme and build over-engineered sky-ramping masterpieces).

This is not that game. Bridge Constructor is a puzzle game, because it tightly limits your resources (funds, materials, and attachment points) to the point where a boring, bog-standard bridge is [i]not possible[/i] on almost any level.

Overall, I still have to give this a thumbs-up, because it's not a [i]bad[/i] puzzle game. But it's not the bridge-building free-for-all sandbox you may be looking for. (That's Poly Bridge.)

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
In terms of bridge building games I suppose this is as refined as it gets. You have just 4 materials, wood, steel, concrete and wires. The game is split into different islands, and each island consists of around 8 bridges to build. In general the complexity and difficulty goes up by each bridge you build. Different levels limit which materials you can use and there is always a budget to stick under adding a very challenging element to the game (especially trying to get the heavier vehicles across the bridge too). 

Overall I thought the game was pretty good, and the levels were well designed. My only gripe is personally being fairly new to any sort of bridge building game, I would have liked a little bit more of a tutorial explaining how things worked, especially the wires. I did get stuck on a fair few levels just trying to figure out how they worked by trial and error.

Otherwise a solid little game, received for free, and had some fun with it so nothing really to complain about.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
This is by far my favorite bridge construction game that I've found so far. It's not perfect but I enjoyed myself the entire time. It's challenging but not too difficult. Unlike Polybridge the bridges are straight forward - get the vehicle to the other side. Polybridge requires you to do all kinds of ridiculous "stunts" and complex machines; imo it's too hard to the point of not being fun. This is also the only bridge constructor I've played that feels like the outcome isn't completely chaotic. A small tweak isn't going to cause vastly different behavior; it's very easy to tweak your bridge to perfection once you get the general layout. And if you want to do ridiculous stuff it's very possible! I personally like having bridges collapse but still earn a victory. 

Bottom line: if you want to get vehicles over bridges with some solid, simple mechanics this is the bridge builder for you.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Very fun bridge builder game, with one major downfall. A lot of the levels in this game require inventive thinking and lots of trial and error, which can both be fun. But at a certain point your bridges need to withstand such a heavy payload that they are no longer that, but instead most people will realistically need to be looking up guides on the internet for specific builds to copy over. This isn't the case for all levels, but it certainly is for a lot. What I'm saying is quite a few of these levels will get so difficult, that there are really two or three very specific ways to complete them at most, which eliminates a lot of personal creativity and fun to be honest. 

At least this was all my experience. Take this review with a grain of salt, but definitely expect this game to be something along these lines. I wanted to recommend this game, and very nearly did, but in the end the guide-seeking and build-copying just outweighed the fun for me by a little bit. If you don't care for looking up a few guides however, or if you believe you can handle it yourself without it, then go pick this game up! Especially on sale, Bridge Constructor can really be worth it otherwise.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
So, the original Bridge Constructor is a good game on its own, but you really should start here, and then play the others, because after the excitement of the others, the vanilla game feels a bit too, well, vanilla.

This game though, does throw a lot of curve balls at you. First of all, after you've completed every bridge (on the base campaign) with the normal cars and trucks (fortunately, getting the truck across counts for cars as well), you unlock a "hard mode" of having to cross the bridge with a tanker truck, which is much harder. The bonus maps (Easter, Halloween and Christmas) have these tanker trucks replaced by appropriate holiday vehicles, which is a nice touch.

It does get tedious, I'm not going to lie. Most of the normal bridges just operate on the principle of "triangles, triangles and triangles", but the bonus ones seem to need some very strange solutions. I did have to look up a number of them, and one of them even involved having the vehicles swing across the gap, which didn't seem possible, but there you are.

The experience was worth the few dollars I paid for it, but it dragged a bit near the end.

Bridge Constructor takes it a bit further and adds several different scenes (including festive ones) with some really cute graphics and sounds and a really lovely soundtrack. It's a VERY family friendly challenging game for all ages.

And the best thing is it comes for all platforms. Personally i've been only playing the Linux version (playing on Linux Mint 18.1) with standard open source video drivers for AMD videocards and never had any problems what so ever. Always ran smooth. But regardless of your operating system, this is a game i can totally recommend. It keeps you busy for a while and the learning curve is fair.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Alright, let's get this out of the way first: I don’t hate this game. It’s fine and fun at times, it just has a few issues I want to get out of the way.

I recommend this game ONLY if it's on sale. I brought it during a 77% sale and I honestly think I got it for 10% off. For the price, this game isn't worth 10 NZD. Maybe 5-ish, it's hard to say. 

Another small bit of criticism (which is my personal bias) is that it is a Mobile phone game which hasn’t had the best port in the world. It is functional, yes, but you can clearly see it was ported from mobile due to the lack of options, the graphics and the fact if you zoom out far enough in testing mode you can see the black sides of the skybox you’re not meant to see. The only reason that I call this a small gripe is because the game itself has plenty of substance to it unlike, let’s say, Adventure Capitalist. In fact, if it was just this reason alone I’d recommend this game, but it isn’t.

Now for my actual criticism which: This game doesn’t do the bridge building genre well. If any indie developer who wants to make a game of this genre ever sees this, take note from this game (since it does physics well) and the free flash game “Cargo Bridge” ( https://www.coolmathgames.com/0-cargo-bridge ) which has better game play. 
What this game does wrong is limits the player with a budget restriction. I believe the player so aim to be below said limit (not forced under it) but if they wish to have creative fun with bridge building they should be allowed to uses as much money as they need.
The idea of the budget restriction seems very stressful in what is a pretty relaxing game.

Positives I should mention is that when that restriction isn’t hit, this game is incredibly satisfying to play. Honestly, if Vale allowed us to rate this game with something in between the thumbs up and thumbs down, I’d go with that. But since we can’t and the negatives outweigh the 

So, conclusion: The budget restriction seriously lets the game down, but it’s a game with substance which can be fun to play. Needs to be made slightly cheaper and (though not necessarily) needs an update to allow more options like full screen or resolution.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Bridge Constructor uses real life physics to build bridges across rivers, uneven terrain, and canyons using a variety of resources... or at least that was the goal. Unfortunately, it falls short of that goal.

I'm going to dispense with the usual Pros and Cons because this is a 2D game with no real story. You just build bridges.

The game is suppose to use real life physics to build bridges but that's not really the case here. I constructed a trestle bridge in one scenario using a real bridge that exists today as a model. It failed for some stupid reason. Not only did it fail, it failed miserably. No matter how I tried to modify it, it would always fail. Then I constructed a bridge that would never stand up in real life and it was fine.

Unlike Portal bridge constructor, this game has a budget that you must stay under in order to work. There were several times where I wanted to see what a particular bridge would look like and operate but the game won't let you do that. Once you hit your limit, it won't let you do anything else unless you start dismantling what you already built and start over.

At the time of this review, Bridge Constructor was selling for $9.99. I can't recommend this game because the physics are unrealistic and the developers apparently have abandoned this game. Avoid it.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Edit2: Some months later, they fixed the achievements. Thumb is up again.
Edit: They added broken achievements to Steam (but not to the game code, that's why they are unachievable), so until this is fixed, here's a THUMB DOWN.

Bridge Constructor is casual gaming at its best, I enjoyed it alot. You can play it in small doses, but the fun is there if you play it for hours, too. It's not a hectic game, you construct your bridge and press a button to see how it does when cars/trucks/tank trucks drive over it. Watching the physics simulation can be fun in itself, especially when the chaos of the collapse brings unexpected results. If the vehicles reach the goal even though your bridge is collapsing behind them, that earns you an achievement aptly named "throwaway bridge" and I got a broad smile when that happened the first time.

If you want to go for high scores (build using less materials, in perfection often resulting in a "throwaway bridge" situation) or compare your score on leaderboards, this game has that, too. It encourages experimentation with the construction options you have. And getting the achievements is also fun and 100%ing is possible with some effort but without pain.

The "sandboxy" character of the game shines when you unexpectedly finish a level because some weird accident happens and the truck gets thrown to the goal while your bridge collapses, or the explosion with the fuel tank truck happens in just the right way to propel its two parts across.

For the later levels that are tricky and require some experimentation, I found it enjoyable to watch other people's solutions on youtube, often even after I solved that level myself.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
This game is a mobile game. This is a problem for two reasons.

First, the controls are frustrating at times. Enough to contribute to playing fatigue. But in itself that's not even enough to make me give it a thumb down.

The second part of it, though, is that it seems to be designed with manipulative mobile monetization schemes at its core. Everything is pretty enjoyable, and the learning curve is very well managed, until you reach one level where suddenly you can't quite figure it out. Maybe you're thinking about it wrong? Maybe it's your fault? Nah. After an hour of iteration to achieve something nearly inconceivable based on previously acquired knowledge, it dawns on you that actually this is more likely to be where they introduce the pay-to-skip mechanic on the mobile version. Except there is no skipping for you, the silly person who got this on PC.

This game can burn down along with whatever's left of my sanity.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
This is not a bad game; it's actually quite decent and makes you think about the best way to solve spanning chasms: however, the sequel - Bridge Constructor Portal - is far more fun, far more quickly.

I've done the first two and a half islands and it is still getting from one spot across a straight horizontal chasm.  Admittedly, there's different distances, and different anchor points, but there's not the variety that BC: Portal has early in the game. Also, the physics are questionable in places.  I'm not an expert, but I have made bridges that are very very wobbly and look to defy gravity.

Given that BC: Portal is only a few pennies more expensive, I'd recommend getting that.  This, in the current year, is only really worth picking up if you have already played through BC: Portal and have a real distinct itch to do more.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
I really don't want to give this a solid "recommended" rating, but there's no middleground with these reviews. It's not so bad that it's outright unplayable and there's certainly a degree of fun to it, but it's hardly the quirky bridge puzzler I was hoping for.

As so many others have stated, the biggest issue going for the game is that the physics are inconsistant. The same bridge will not fail the same way twice a lot of the time, sometimes even netting you a win for the level. It's one thing to say your puzzle game has multiple solutions, and another to say it's down to luck. This is compounded by the fact that quite often the successful bridges don't look like they SHOULD work, this is especially true in later levels.

I'd say the first half of the game is fairly fun and thought-provoking, while the later half, especially once you add in the Tank Trucks, is more an exercise in frustration. You might try a bridge that should work but cannot find out why it doesn't, look up a guide and discover you were basically doing it right except one piece of wood was in the wrong spot. That doesn't feel rewarding at all.

Speaking of unrewarding, some of the bridges are almost exactly the same as earlier ones, except suddenly you only have wood, or have less money. So the bridge that barely held together before is suddenly even less practical and the solutions you find in guides are so far removed from common sense that it's a wonder someone found them. When they work, of course, since the guide solutions in my experience only work about 70% of the time. Again, it boils down to the inconsistancy of the game's physics.

That makes the experience unique, I suppose.

I did notice that certain ideas or solutions seem to repeat themselves a lot. Such as placing a short vertical piece of wood/steel under the connect points and then stringing along wire parallel to the bridge. It's amazing how often that works cause in reality it sure as hell wouldn't. I think that's part of why the puzzles don't feel like proper puzzles, cause the solutions don't really come from a logical point of view, but throwing crap together and seeing what holds on long enough for the vehicle to make it across.

I realize this all sounds fairly negative, despite the fact I'm giving it a Thumbs Up. Essentially I'm saying the game is not that great but if you got it on sale it might be a fun way to kill some time. And maybe a few brain cells after beating your head against the wall at some point.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Bridge Constructor is a simple bridge building game with varying levels of difficulty.

Pros:
-Easy to pick up and play. Simple mouse controls and drag & drop placement of bridge materials
-It has a decent amount of content, with 64 levels excluding the Christmas, Easter, Halloween levels and the Trains DLC
-It has Steam Achievements

Cons:
-The games physics feels incredibly poor. After building a bridge and watching it fail, you can try the exact same bridge again and it will fail on a diffirent point as opposed to before making it hard to gauge error and correct the mistakes in your bridge
-Graphics feel subpar and do not really come across as appealing
-At R109 (~$10) this game feels incredibly overpriced for what you get

This is an okay bridge simulator, but there are better ones out there. Get it on sale if you enjoy these types of games and want to give this a go.
6/10

review: Bridge Constructor is a game that highlights the dangers of government privatization. It allows me to play as a slovenly, morally-void government contractor building really ♥♥♥♥♥♥ bridges for vehicles for a nation-state consisting of several islands. They have really low standards as to what a bridge is supposed to do, just as long as a minimum of total vehicles get across. Did the bridge collapse after those two cars got across? That's fine, they've already cleared the check and the remaining budget goes right into your slush fund. Smarter contractors than I will take advantage of the game's really weird physics engine to launch cars onto the exit point, therefore saving time, money, and more money. The government finds this acceptable, presumably because they like a good show and will think you are some kind of bridge circus man or wizard. name: Bridge Constructor
review:
First off, I would like to address, that the "realistic physics engine" mentioned in the trailer is far from being realistic. When the road collapses beneath the vehicle, it (the vehicle) can fly up to 10 meters in the air, sometimes the tank truck explodes completely on it's own, and breaking bridge pieces do a 180° flip in the air upwards. Sounds realistic eh? It will happen all the time you fail, and it is not fun.

About the constructor itself: I miss a lot of tools in the editor such as copying, pasting, selecting multiple pieces at once, flipping, rotating the selection, and free (off the grid) placement. Add in the weird and inconvenient( and not configurable) control scheme and you get one hell of an editor. In order to move nodes, you have to click on them and hold the mouse button for some time. Sometimes moving them to a specific location is not allowed even though you can delete the node and rebuild it at that new location, and to delete it, you have to double click very fast, and the game does not adapt to your os specified double-click speed configuration. Though you can right click to delete things, doing so will erase everything that is somewhere arround your cursor making it inconvenient to use.

The content: The levels in this game have a lot of repetitive backgrounds. And in all, there are not many levels either. You get bronze medal for completing them with cars, silver for completing them with trucks, and gold for completing them with the very heavy tank truck that is unlocked after beating the game. The tutorial only tells you how to design your bridges, but gives you no clue about what is a "good" bridge design. Lucky me, I already have another game about building bridges, that offers a very in-depth explanation about how it all works, and how to build a good bridge regardless of the tools available. Unless you are good at understanding these physic elements, or had that sort of "pre-training", you are most likely going to quit this game near the third island( out of the 5). Every level after the first island feels like a broken "challenge" instead of a "problem to solve", and it is not fun. What happened, is that I made a very smart design in one of the levels, arround mid-game and that same design worked in most of the late game challenges where the same tools were available. The levels themselves randomly lock and unlock the tools, because solving the same problem (building a bridge in the same scenery, with the same length) five times with a different subset of tools and budget should feel like solving five different problems. It is somewhat challenging, but it is repetitive.

The environment is very low poly, the trees are all flat, and it's texture coloring and lighting sometimes does not even match that of the sky. Not even your own bridge pieces do something to feel different.

Aaaaaand a DLC pack that is being advertised through the whole game. Hell, the entire game is all bark and no bite. Even the trailer is just for the sake of having a trailer. Dude, I am glad I did not fall for that and did not buy this game at all.

I would like to have a lawyer read my rights out loud because I probably received this for a crime or something. (Seriously, since when do games just randomly appear in one's steam library?)

Join the fun... what a scam.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
[h1]TL:DR[/h1]
[i]Be an engineer and build bridges to make people feel sketchy about where they live.[/i]

[h1]The Good Stuff[/h1]
+A fun physics based building game focused around getting people from one side to the other
+Build bridges of all types, including ones for cars, trucks, tankers and even trains
+Good set of achievements for all those hunters out there, this will give you a good time getting them all
+Get a better score by using less materials and heavier vehicles. Make everything truck proof! 

[h1]The Ok Stuff[/h1]
=Repetitive, but that's pretty clear
=The difficulty goes up as you progress providing more intuition and general common sense to complete levels (I lack the latter part there). However there does seem to be a sudden spike in difficulty after the first island, as I passed the first island without having to retry any levels due to failure
=Four types of materials to use for building, but it's random which ones you use in a level versus unlocking them for permanent use
=Not really overpriced, but since there four games in this series, try to get them on sale if you truly enjoy them

[h1]The Bad Stuff[/h1]
-The physics are iffy in this game, sometimes being entirely random where a bridge will just catapult into the air or snaps five seconds after the vehicles are completely off of it. Sometimes bridges will break when I first test them before a car touches them, and sometimes they stay just fine when the cars go on them. Like I said, entirely random at times

name: Bridge Constructor
review: Very fun game. My child loves coming home everyday from school just to play this game. Its a little unfortunate that its tearing away me and his relationship because its the last pint of family I have after my parents were gunned down in a shopping mall and my wife was stolen and put into sex trafficing. At the very least its allowing him to cope with the loss of both his arms and legs in a bombing that happened at out local JC Penney. Anyway enough about me and more about this game. Its very fun and I finally got a chance to play it after smothering my son to death. It really gets your noggin joggin when you're trying to figure out how to make a bridge. One of my favorite features are the sound and music which were promptly both muted by me as soon as I started the game. But this game has a deep meaning that relates to the deep meaning of life. It's very depressing to watch the lifeless vehicles drive into a valley to their doom and as a side note this really makes me sad. The drivers of these vehicles could have families that they will never see again. I also love the variety of materials I get for building bridges like wood, steel, cable, and one other that I can't think of off the top of my head because there were just too many to even count. But the most important feature has got to be truck proofing your bridges because nothing else has ever made me wish I were dead more than watching cars easily cross a bridge just for some trucks to shatter it to pieces. In conclusion I have to say Poly Bridge is the superior game although I have to wonder what kind of person would buy a bridge building game in the first place?

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
I am the Michael Bay of civil engineering, my bridges most of the time collapse in a spectacular fashion sending cars and trucks to their doom in the precipice below. Cables snap, supports break and tank trucks explode mid air. My creations are a sight to behold and will probably give any real civil engineer recurring nightmares but I do love them like a proud father holding up a deformed baby, "he is a special one" I say as the midwife gives me a mournful look. My favorite bridge was actually a success, at least according to the game, as I sent trucks down that bridge, the first support glowed an ominous red, when they cleared the midpoint of the bridge that support collapsed starting a slow downward movement of the left side of the bridge, that was okay though because just as the whole thing collapsed by first truck cleared the bridge and the second one was tumbling onto the road on the right side of the bridge. A spectacular success!!!

On a serious note, it's not a bad game but the physics don't feel right and the resulting bridges that succeed according to the game look like abominations you will never see in real life more often than not. Get it on a sale but don't expect realistic physics.

review:
[h1][b]★★☆☆☆[/b][/h1]
My enjoyment of the game was always covered in the shadows of the rubble that was left by my [b]im[/b]ploded bridges.

That's actually not solely a joke. Bridges and vehicles often times fly straight up; wood that should break, just fly of in a unrealistic direction. These physics is where the problem of this game lies. Since the game is all about physics, I can't recommend the game to anyone when that crucial part doesn't work.

Multiple reviewers on Steam listed out a great array of examples of how inconsistant and unrealistic the mechanics are. You might be able to ignore this, as I tried to, but getting stuck on a map only to discover in guide that the reason you were failing was that you were using TOO MUCH wood, is just aggrevating. I discovered (for myself) a consistant, succesful type of bridge that would work most of the time, as long as the cables were available. Later on, I noticed that guides were using the same type.

The type: a wooden bridge with just one or two lines of cables paralel going under it, saving an amazing amount of money everytime. It's also completely unrealistic, considering I've never seen a bridge like this in real life. When you do try and make a realistic looking bridge, which every player undoubtedly has tried, it would just break down and insulting you by doing so. For reference, look at the screenshots above. These won'l work on normal or hard difficulty (this is easy, as you can see cars, not trucks).

Despite the great amount of levels you get for the price ( I paid 5 bucks including all DLC), I just couldn't bare more of the game after completing the main and first islands. My suggestion is check out Poly Bridge.

My only complaint is that the materials you can use are changed up with every puzzle. In other words, you don't "earn" the advanced materials and keep them as you continue play. The game just artificially constrains what you can use, so that's kind of annoying.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
How do I Best Describe the Game.
Imagine you’re an architect and you have been hired by a strange country that has no bridges to design and build all the bridges for them with a set budget for each "level" and an endless supply for test vehicles.
the game is just a click and drag, select the pieces for the bridge and click the area to start building then drag them out to when you need them... repeat till you have a bridge built then go see if it can get cars/trucks/trains/Santa to get over it, or more fun to see it fail.

Things to Know.
Don’t worry you can edit the bridge even if your bridge breaks down completely.
The suspension cable can solve almost any level (if you’re allowed to use them).
You can edit position and length of any parts by holding down on a link and moving it to your new place (it only stays in the new place if it’s a valid position).

Last Point.
Give each level a good go before you look up a guide, you might find you can get that train over that 700m gap with nothing but wood planks.

29 Hours played; had a good time on it and even surprised myself when some of my ideas worked first time.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
I found this game sitting in my Humble Bundle library and I have no idea when I got it. It might have been a gift. I decided to give it a try and it is ... okay. I guess. There is two serious problems, however. 

Firstly, the physics is wonky. I created a bridge which held up for the most part, but failed close to the end. I went to reinforce the part that failed, but I needed to know exactly what part failed first. When I reran the trucks across the bridge a second time, it worked perfectly. I then noticed this happening a lot. You could rerun a bridge if it barely failed over and over again and sometimes you would get lucky. 

Secondly, there are better bridge builder games out there. Poly Bridge, for example. That game has a better physics and more variation in the puzzles. 

I would only pick it up if you can get it on sale. Even then, I would recommend looking at the competition first.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
so there are a few annoyances with this game that prompted me to give this game a thumbs down

- the bridge building physics are bad (you basically cant reinforce any joints with more braceing because more often then not trying to brace a joint will actually make it weaker because instead of diverting the strain elsewhere like it would do in real life the game reads it as there is now more weight on that joint and therefore the piece that was already strained is more likely to break)

-when one piece breaks it doesn't matter if any other part of the bridge could or would be able to take the load, they all are suddenly unstable and brittle and WILL break

- to compound on the inability to brace joints they also have very few "braced points"(and or starting placement points" in this game) which with only having 1 point on many levels on each side of the gap makes it increasingly difficult to make any type of stable bridge 

-the wood and metal beams must be made of a heavy paper because they can hardly hold up their own weight (bridges HAVE to be minimalist in function because of the weight of the materials is too heavy for their strength basically trying to have a huge bridge is impossible)

- building the bridges has a few glitches, quite often like every 20th click when placing a bridge piece (not deleting it) it will delete any other pieces located in that area. about every 30th click your delete button will not regester no matter now many times you click with it until you then click on a available building material

so considering what i've complained about is the MAIN MAJORITY of the entire gameplay I would say even buying this for 3$ I am not satisfied and that I don't recommend it just wasn't worth the time

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
I wanted to like this one, as it seemed like a fun little game to kill the time. But:

- Physics are awful, and inconsistent.
- There's a complete lack of tips, explanations or how to; you're left trying things again and again and again for no apparent reason. Which goes for everything basically, because...
- There's a fair bit of repetition, and unwanted repetition at that: did you finally understand how the game wants you to use wooden planks when you've got noplace to lean them on? Great, now do it again a couple of times, with minor changes that you have to figure out yourself by failing again and again and again. And that's my biggest complaint:
- It's limited. What you can construct is limited. What you can imagine is limited, down to minor details, and these details are so hardcoded into each level that they take away all the fun.

Nope. Thanks but no thanks.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Never trust a bridge you find in Camatuga.  Go well out of your way to find a ferry, or go all the way around the river to get to the other side, but don't go across a bridge!

Bridge Constructor is a game where you build bridges in the fictional lands of Camatuga.  Your budget, and sometimes your available supplies (wood, steel, concrete, and rope) are limited, which means that sometimes you have to get creative.  But beyond that, it's all about figuring out how you can build a bridge, on budget, that will carry across cars, then medium trucks, then tanker-trailers.

While it may sound like a fairly straightforward physics building game, the wacky bridges you'll often wind up with and the explosions of wood and steel when things don't quite work out, make it hilarious.  The game is both a lot of fun by yourself, but also a lot of fun with friends, especially towards the more difficult later levels, where you can pass around the mouse and try your own approaches to building spans on low budgets.  Inevitably it will take a lot of tries to get it right, and laughter will ensue as unlikely bridges collapse - or occasionally prove just good enough to support the load, despite their "I'd never drive across that in real life" appearance.

Among the Bridge Constructor series, I'd put this one and Medieval at pretty much equal for gameplay, as Medieval introduces some interesting new elements, but the three vehicles types per bridge extends the challenge, in a good way, of each bridge here (while not preventing you from moving to the next one as long as you have cars getting across).  However, this iteration gave me much more playtime before I'd completed most of the game, and they're the same price, so my recommendation would be to get Bridge Constructor, have a blast, and if you're still wanting more, picking up Medieval after that.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
How come this game has been greenlit? I have played many bridge constructor games on Flash, standalone or mobile apps; not all of them were good but most featured better physics and playability/UI than this one.
The physics really sucks: it's slow like it was slow-motion, moreover it's impossible to speed it up! So one waits for way too long for the trucks to cross a bridge. Even my girlfriend who knows only about Flash and casual games, looking at the game and watching the trucks fall off a breaking bridge, said it looks amateurish.

Note to dev: scaling down the objects & world so that the physics simulation would run more realistically on Unity (which the game seems to be made off) is really no big deal!

Some GUI textures are even 'optimized' with a 256-colors palette, like if our computers lack bytes of VRAM...

Everything from the game system to the GUI feels mobile (which is an excellent thing... on small touchscreens), nothing seems to have been redesigned for computers... except the steep price!

I played up to the middle of the second world; I wanted to give this game a chance. But it's frustrating and repetitive, and the appealing settings/bridges shown in the promotional screenshots had yet to come into play.

This is definitely a bad game, boring and disrespectful to the players who are just treated as customers (what's this omnipresent 'Share on Facebook' on a single player premium computer game?!)
Avoid at all cost.


PS: This game is one more example of Greenlight picking crappy uninspired games (mostly from the genres/models the people already know) while disregarding many unique gems.

If you want to place multiple pieces on after another, after you place on, the next one is automatically connected, which, while may seem useful, is not. It mainly gets in the way and makes red show up way too often.

Second, the physics. The physics suck. Minecraft has better physics.
Also, the wooden structures and wires do hit the road. if the top of yout bridge falls over, it falls through the road.

Read other negative reviews for the physics. I cannot express my dislike well in words.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
I think it needs to be said that the game does fail as a physics based puzzle game, in that I've had at least one moment where simply running the simulation a second time without changing anything achieved a different result than it did the first time I ran it (a part did not break at the homestretch of my bridge, netting me both the win for the level and the Pinocchio achievement).  I can only report this happening once because I instantly came here to point this out in a review, and before now I hadn't even tried such a boneheaded maneuver when things went wrong (I only did it this time to reevaluate what was wrong with the bridge!).  I'm having trouble recommending a physics based puzzle game where you can get different results by trying the same thing more than once (isn't that the definition of insanity?), so I will have to give this a thumbs down.

However, I would also point out to prospective buyers that the game actually is a lot of fun, so I'm hitting "no" on a bit of an edge case.  If the game seams appealing to you, you probably should still buy this, it just cheapens the experience a bit to realize that there may be an (indeterminable amount of an) element of pure luck involved here.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
TL;DR it's a dumbed down, oversimplified, easier, and crappier imitation of Bridge It.

The level design is extremely easy and extremely repetitive.  Only a few levels in the entire game, using the biggest truck (the tanker), actually took more than 1 try.   I found myself building almost exactly the same bridge a dozen times on a dozen slightly different maps.  Then I had to repeat each of those maps again with a bigger truck.   Bridge constructor has slightly more levels than bridge it but the levels are so repetitive that effectively Bridge It has 3 times more levels.

The building materials are ridiculously strong, to the point that you can bridge a big gap with nothing but road layed in an inverted arch.   Also, there's less variety in building materials.

When you mouseover beams it often highlights a different beam from the one that will actually be affected when you click.

It panders to you with stupid achievements and crowd cheering sounds that you get just for breathing.   This is typical of pretty much every corporate game since Wrath of the Lich King, but still, it's really stupid.

You figure what the game wants to work, then mix and repeat, over and over and over. 

If you can grab this for $5, Go ahead! But...... I don't think that it is fully worth $10.

I was spoiled with World of Goo. Buy that instead. If you already have World of Goo..... don't expect that from this game.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Warning to potential buyers: This game has some bridges that won't be solveable with the only structures the game shows you, you'll have to make some weird sh** up and I know this because I had to look up the answer after failing some of the bridges over and over. The game does little to show you which structures you should be thinking about (other than triangles), and often you might not really see if something is working that 10% better than your other builds, and that's often a crucial difference. It also doesn't really explain what materials might fare better beyond the absolute basics of (steel is stronger than wood), and it would've been awesome if they did more to show how the rope can help to support tight budget bridges by connecting along the underside of your bridge from end to end. I only know it distributes weight better this way because some walkthroughs used it. Other materials can be a bit fiddly with load distribution depending on how you've arranged them.
I have played through a bunch of this game and BC: Medieval and while I like both just for the physics puzzle challenge, I find the Medieval one's solutions end up making more sense than this one. It still barely guides you but at least those structures kind of make sense.
In this version you can build diagonal roads (which might be the only way to solve some of these bridges, it's certainly the only way I could find for some) and they end up letting you hold them up with ropes from nonsense structures on the sides because the stone pillars are too expensive or not available.
I still recommend it because I've enjoyed it either way, the physics are simple but pretty consistent, and it's a good little brain work out, but if you want a well-paced puzzle game with some actual help on what to do, this game fails a little on that front. I personally looked up a few answers and still enjoyed trying to figure the level out before I gave up.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
If your looking for a review on this game you've come to the wrong place, but if your looking for an outstanding pie recipe you've come to the right place.

Ingredients:
1. 6 tablespoons unsalted butter
2. 1/2 cup white sugar
3. 1/2 cup brown sugar
4. 1/4 cup water
5. 1/4 teaspoon cinnamon
6. 1 pinch salt
7. 5 apples - peeled, cored and sliced
8. 1 pastry for double-crust pie 

Directions:
1. Preheat oven to 425 degrees F (220 degrees C).
2. Combine butter, white sugar, brown sugar, water, cinnamon, and salt in a saucepan over medium heat. Bring to a boil, remove from heat and set aside.
3. Roll out half the pastry to fit a 9-inch pie plate. Place bottom crust in pie plate; pour in apple slices.
4. Roll out top crust into a 10-inch circle. Cut into 8 (1-inch) wide strips with a sharp paring knife or pastry wheel. Weave the pastry strips, one at a time, into a lattice pattern. Fold the ends of the lattice strips under the edge of the bottom crust and crimp to seal.
5. Pour butter-sugar mixture over top of pie, coating the lattice, and allowing any remaining sauce to drizzle through the crust.
6. Bake in the preheated oven for 15 minutes. Reduce heat to 350 degrees F (175 degrees C), and bake until the crust is golden brown, the caramel on the top crust is set, and the apple filling is bubbling, 35 to 40 more minutes. Allow to cool completely before slicing.

review:
I've played three of the Bridge Constructor games, and find this one to be the hardest, but not sure if it was the bridges or the insane physics.  This was the most frustrating of the three, mainly because a bridge may be successful one time in 10, but for different reasons - jumped funny, strange ramp effects, or tumbling incomprehensibly.

There are two parts, the main continent and the unlockable slope world.  The slope world is the twist on this version of the game that salvaged the review, as the main continent was simply three bridge designs in different lengths, depending on what supplies you were granted.  Slope you have to think about it, and deal with the crazy physics.

Bought in a bundle for about 75c US.  Played for about 18 hours, with 4 of that on just a couple of bridges (see my guide on the worst one IMHO).  NOT worth full retail of US$10, but if you can get it for 2.50 or less I think you will find it to be a pretty decent game.

5.5 / 10

Bridge Constructor is a fun simulation game for all ages. 
It includes fun, challenging levels that are full of colour and life. This game really gets your brain working as use your imagination to create some of the most bizarre and unsafe bridges in the world! Watch as you fight against the laws of physics and the weight of vehicles to try to create your 'dream' bridge. Your ideas can be as imaginative or simple as you want but, will always get you thinking in different ways to suit each level.
As you progress through the game, you are given stronger materials and a higher budget to make your bridges more unique.
This game will keep you entertained for hours on end and is always fun to come back to after finishing it! Defiantly a must buy if you enjoy building and simulation games.

review:
Played during an Indie-Game-Saturday event. So I have also officially played all three Brdige Constructor games. Overall, I really hate not recommending these games because they are fun, especially in a group (hot-seat style). I just wish that the developers would make better decisions (even with the material they have - not much more developing money would be necessary).

Pros:
1. Fun, wonky bridge construction that *mostly* follows logic
2. Medium level of game content

Cons:
1. Music is SUPER repetitive - especially since these are puzzles that take time
2. You don't so much learn to build bridges as you learn to 'play the game' or break the system lol
3. Medium level of game content
4. The game lacks overall creativity in challenges, levels, music, menu design, and basically everything. So much wasted potential here.

Not to compare with that game, however, this game is still bad. Most levels leave player no choice but to construct the bridge exactly same to the solution you found on web. There are too small test case, no run around for example. Speaking about arts, most of the time you'll force to build asymmetry bridge even thoug the envorionment is symmetry! And the most annoying is that the physics in this game is wrong, which degrade this aim-to-be-physics-savvy game to an electronic junk.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Bridge Constructor is a nice and simple way to waste some time. The story in the game is very straightforward. There has been a devastating earthquake that has destroyed the bridges on the islands of Camatuga and the player is in charge of their rebuilding. The problem for the player is that the available budget for the bridge constructions is very tight, so it is not possible to make too advanced solutions or to use the more expensive materials as much as would be ideal. After building the bridges, they have to be tested by letting some cars and/or trucks cross and if the bridge collapses a rethink of the design is needed. When all bridges on Camatuga are functioning for trucks, the game opens up for tests using heavy tanker trucks on the newly constructed bridges, with an often sad and explosive result prompting a redesign of the bridges.

Sometimes the statics of the bridge constructions seem a bit off with collapsing at unexpected places, but usually some engineering common sense and experimenting will solve the problems. It would have been nice to be able to play some of the levels with unlimited budget in order to test some truly exotic bridge designs, but the game is good enough as it is.

This is not a game to spend full evenings with because of its repetitive nature, but it is a relaxing game to play for a couple of minutes now and then. Quite a bit like sudoku.

name: Bridge Constructor
review: a funny little physics puzzle game where the goal is too simply get the vehicles to the other side. though this gets hard to do when your on a limited budget and get materials taken away from you in some missions. This game gives you the weird feeling of having alot of freedom and very little at the same time.  You have your freedom of being able to complete each mission your way with your own custom made bridge but u also feel very limited because there are only 4 different materials to construct your bridge from and it's rare that you even get to use 3 of them at a time (2 in most missions).  but it can also be pretty humerous watching your little cars go flying to their doom or watching them desperatly trying to outrun the colapseing bridge behind them. If you enjoy physics based puzzles this is definentaly worth checking out.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Decent game. 

Occasionally you will have to employ less than conventional approaches in order to successfully engineer some bridges.  More often than not 'common sense' engineering works.

That being said: Not a bad time killer, would play again.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Bridge Constructor is sort of like TIM2 (shout out to my MS-DOS peeps), but with bridges. You have a limited amount of resources to create a sturdy bridge that spans a gap. 


The good:

I like the idea behind it. As I said, it reminds me a lot of the old The Incredible Machine games, but with bridges. 


The ugly:

The graphics are passible, the music is okay, but a little bland and repetitive. 


The bad:

EVERYTHING ELSE.

Oh, ho, no. I'm sorry, I'm doing my best to be polite, but this game is entirely unplayable. The physics are just straight-up broken in a game about physics and engineering. Cars go flying for no reason, trucks are magnitized to debris, and the actual construction logic makes no sense whatsoever. You can brace a bridge as much as you want, but it's going to go everywhere.

This isn't fun. This is the opposite of fun. This isn't even good enough to be an app game.


Last words:

I wanted this game to work. I really did. This could have been a fantastic physics simulator. In fact, my friends specifically told me to not buy it, but I did because I believed in the idea. It's just too broken. Even if there was a patch (for everything), I would still doubt this would be playable. At ten bucks, it's a borderline scam.


Score: 1.5/10 (and I'm being generous)
Cost: $9.99 USD

I've played a few bridge construction versions over the years. Compared to the previous one I played, this one added some features and took some others away. New to me were concrete, slope mania, and tanker trucks. Gone were two-way traffic, moving bridges, and the ability to adjust the 3rd dimension (bridge width). Overall this version results in a polishes experience that gets it just right. Sometimes it's hard to build a nice bridge, sometimes you can get away with crazy cheap designs. I enjoy it quite a bit.

Slope Mania was fun as well, containing a mix of both hard and trivial challenges. It allows for some bridges that totally collapse while the tanker truck races downward and still makes it across.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Bridge Constructor is a brilliant game that puts you in the position of a builder constructing bridges across the 5 islands within the game. Each level tests your skills at being able to solve how to build a bridge in a means to get vehicles across. Anybody who knows of a program called West Point Bridge Designer will find this game to be quite similar, but the achievements and challenges presented will hook you right in. While you are able to build a sensible, well-thought bridge that will do the job perfectly, it's extremely fun to be able to build crazy bridges with massive jumps that still transport vehicles. The physics engine is so fun to play around with through each bridge design - launching cars off your "ramps" in the hopes of reaching the end - and the ability to do what you will when constructing bridges to finish each level is amazing.

A game that I strongly recommend to anybody interested in purchasing this game.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
Bridge Constructor surprised me, it sure looks very casual and is as easy to get into as you'd expect. For veterans of the genre who played some of the old hardcore bridge building games though, it not only has a nostalgic effect, but also kinda kicks yer butt in places if you get too cocky. If you have played bridge building games before, you will have an easy and reasonably fun time flying through the first playthrough of the main campaign, which only took me some 2.5 hours. 

The main campaign maps then open up for testing your bridges using the "tank truck", which makes things pretty tough and you have to solve levels differently (with some more budget) compared to your first playthrough! In the Expansion/DLC part of the game, you find some rather different and creative levels that at least to some degree challenge your engrained senses for the rules.

So while the levels in the first run through the main campaign become very much "seen that before", especially if you're playing them quickly, I am still positively surprised by the game overall. If you like games like this and haven't played any in a longer time, give it a go! If you've played them all, then wait for a sale.

Note: The game's difficulty often doesn't leave much room for errors or poor decision-making. Despite it's cutesy appearance, the game would potentially be frustrating for younger kids without guidance.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
So after playing this game for about an hour I have to say it was fun at first, then it became quite boring after a while. Sure you get to build bridges and test to see if cars or trucks can make it across safely or not but after a while it gets repetitive.

While building the bridges you do have a spending limit which makes you choose your building material wisely unfortunately you are not granted all of the building material from the get go which is fine. When you do finish building your bridge you get to test it with either a car, trucks or semi trucks. If your bridege can support the heavier vehicles you get a bonus added to your score. If it can't support the heavier vehicles you get to watch your bridge fail, you also get to watch your bridge fail if you did not build it correctly. Another thing you get to see when you test your bridge out is where there is stress. After seeing the areas that have a great deal of stress you can go back and redo that aree if you would like or you can leave it alone. I usually went back and tried to redo the area.

 Unfortunately I do not recommend this game at all.

name: Bridge Constructor
review: Bridge Constructor is a game that highlights the dangers of government privatization. It allows me to play as a slovenly, morally-void government contractor building really shitty bridges for vehicles for a nation-state consisting of several islands. They have really low standards as to what a bridge is supposed to do, just as long as a minimum of total vehicles get across. Did the bridge collapse after those two cars got across? That's fine, they've already cleared the check and the remaining budget goes right into your slush fund. Smarter contractors than I will take advantage of the game's really weird physics engine to launch cars onto the exit point, therefore saving time, money, and more money. The government finds this acceptable, presumably because they like a good show and will think you are some kind of bridge circus man or wizard. So if you feel like screwing the taxpayer, why not give this game a go?

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
There are objects in the world.  Some fall under the category 'Games'.  Some fall under the category 'Art'.  But is there a category for an object that falls under both?  There is.  That category is called 'Bridge Constructor'.

Perhaps you think, "No! Bridge Constructor is not an art game; it is an engineering game."  To which I say, "Non Monsieur/Madame/Mademoiselle, non!"  Then I finish my thought in English because that is all the French I know.  Remember, you are [b][i]not actually constructing bridges in the real physical world[/i][/b] (apologies if you thought that this game might let you do that).  You are creating simulations, models, dreams of your inner self.  What you create on screen is you; you are what you create on screen.  I repeat: once you play this game, there will be bridges inside of you.

It should also be known that this game tells the story of a world.  Do you wonder what drives the cars, the trucks, the tankers?  Wonder no more my friend: it is a race of little insect people.  Ants?  Bees?  Weevils?  I do not know: insects.  All I know is that they cannot fly.  If they could, they would not need bridges.

-------------- The Story of JD7QW --------------

Designate JD7QW buckled the seatbelt around his carapaced waist, its legs quivering as it did so.  It tried to hide its fright, but even from outside the tanker truck the Planner noticed.

"JD7QW," the Planner said, its mandibles clicking furiously, "this is no time for fear.  You are performing the function for which you were produced, as am I.  By testing this bridge we are expanding the reach of the Colony and celebrating the greatness of the Queen.  That is all we can do.  That is all we shall do."

Not daring to look at the Planner, JD7QW nodded while fixing its gaze straight ahead.  The bridge in front of it was very wooden and very straight.  It also appeared very fragile, like sunlight on water.  How could it hold the weight of the enormously flammable tanker?  "I imagine that there are supports underneath?" JD7QW asked, doing its best to hide its anxiety.  "Steel?  Perhaps concrete?"

"Such questions!" the Planner snapped.  "I will answer, but know that I will be filing a Protest when I return to the Colony."  It shook its engorged head.  "It appears that the Breeders are producing defective Designates."

Again JD7QW did not dare turn around.  Instead it looked up.  The sky was cloudless and blue, so clear that the ghost of the moon could be faintly seen even in the daytime.  [i]I would like to fly there[/i], the Designate thought suddenly.  [i]Such wonders I would see![/i]  But of course it couldn't.  Only the Queen was allowed to have wings.

The Planner continued.  "There is no steel.  There is no concrete.  There is wood and only wood.  But -"  The Planner's voice softened.  Surprised, JD7QW peeked over.  The Planner's mandibles had slowed, its multi-faceted eyes had grown clouded and dreamy.  "- but oh, the design, the joins and triangles and descending supports, the interlocking wheels of pressure and release, the. . ."  Caught up in the Planner's vision, it allowed itself to think: [i]Someday I shall be allowed to dream as well.[/i]

Then the Planner shook its head.  Its voice turned to jagged stone.  "No more!  You are impudent, JD7QW.  Drive now.  Go."

It stepped back.  Unwilling and unable to disobey, JD7QW depressed the gas pedal and the tanker truck rumbled forward.

A short segment of paved road lay between the truck and the bridge.  It took both an instant and an eternity to cross.  Then JD7QW felt the wheels beneath him [i]clunk[/i] as it rattled onto the wooden bridge, and right away it knew that it would not survive that day.

The bridge was already beginning to sway, the surface to buckle.  A series of grand snapping noises echoed within the ravine, each one like the shattering of an enormous tree.  The truck began to [i]descend[/i]; frantic, JD7QW pressed the gas pedal with all its strength.  The truck jerked forward, and for a moment the Designate thought that the vehicle might simply plow through the air to the other side, an invincible and impossible metal beast.

Then the wooden surface below him imploded, and the truck fell in earnest.

JD7QW barely had time to squeeze its eyes shut, to gag at the vortex in its stomach, when the fuel tank exploded.  Free of its burden, the cab of the truck shot up into the air, and the sudden reverse in sensation caused JD7QW's eyes to fly open in surprise.  And what it saw through the broken windshield was blue, endless blue, with the ghost of the moon traced far above.

It knew there wasn't much time left.  With legs that were no longer trembling, it pressed the release on the seat buckle.

The Designate shot out of the vehicle immediately.  Below him, the truck cab had already slowed and was falling back to earth.  [i]But not me[/i], JD7QW thought.  [i]Now I shall fly.  I shall fly to the moon.[/i]  And it began to smile as it dreamed of what it might find there.

----------------------------------------

There is a JD7QW inside all of us.  If not for yourself, play this game for it.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
On the surface, this game appears to be shovelware. Half-finished collision boxes and clipart menu graphics leave a bad first impression, but play into it a bit, and at its core are a collection of solid and well thought-out physics puzzles.

My main problem with the game is that the physics are not consistent, which can be a severe detriment to a puzzle game (See Bad Rats), but here it doesn't seem to be a problem at all unless you're going for the "Your bridge broke but you still made it" achievement.

I was surprised by the creativity of the puzzles. The first bridge that gives you a "Checkpoint", or a location your vehicles must drive through before reaching the goal, places the checkpoint almost directly below the starting location, forcing you to think up some insane motocross ramp to reach it safely. And it only gets wackier from there.

Overall a thoroughly enjoyable puzzle game, if you like that sort of thing, with only a few problems that almost never seem to get in the way of gameplay.

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
I recommend this game but only because I have had so much fun with my friends as we scream and cry at trucks as they fall through our poorly built bridges. If I was an antisocial type who played this game alone, I would have hated it. 

The "campaign" is reasonably long, but it gets repetitive. The only way to add variety is to try stupid designs that would not work in real life, but do in game. For example, I used a counter-weight to create enought tension to support my bridge. The levels are very challenging, but not in the sense that they challenge your understanding of physics, because anyone who knows anything about building bridges with get very frustrated with the idiotic placement of build-points in the map. In combination with the restricted use of certain materials, and the third-world budget for purchasing said materials, bridges often can barely stand their own weight, and beating a level often only means that your load got a cross even though the bridge collapsed behind it.

Which brings me to the reason I actually recommend it. The level of challenge results in hilariously inadequate structures that make any educated person cringe. It's addictive to try something hilarious and then watch as cars plummet to their death with satisfying crunches. In a sense, the game is more fun to lose than to win. And if you have a group of friends nearby who can make fun of your attempts to build and when you fail push you aside to attempt their own idiotic designs, it gets fun and competitive. However, this is not a feature of the game, but a feature the way you can choose to augment your experience.

My chief complaint, though, is the lack of a sandbox mode. The game never gives you a chance to experiment with different properties of physics or the materials you are working with without restricting you to a budget and limited build points. And a sandbox mode would not be hard to implement, considering that all it would need is custom maps, build points, and unlimited access to the already available resources.

So, if you are looking for an educational experience that will teach you to build bridges . . . go somewhere else.

If you are looking for a hilarious cautionary showcase of how not to build bridges, particularly with smug friends nearby, buy Bridge Constructor. (Which should have been named "Bridge Destructor")

name: Bridge Constructor
review:
This game is seriously fun.  It is very well made, and has beautiful graphics.  While it is a bridge building game, I'm not lying when I tell you it is intense and insane at times.  I have literally yelled at my computer several times when I was only a segment away from making my bridge work with trucks, but it was worth it. 

While it appears simplistic, it feels realistic.  Although realistic it provides the perfect amount of goofyness to the game to keep me relaxed after my yelling.  The goofyness does nothing to detract from the gameplay, and only adds to it.  I can not express how much I love to play it.

Only downside is the musical tracks, which pretty much becomes ambient after a while, which really makes it not a problem.

Overall I give it a 9.5/10 due to the linear/limited gameplay aspect of it, but other than that, very well polished and well worth the money spent.

name: Bridge Constructor
review: This game is awesome. It’s a simple premise: You build bridges. In reality it is a puzzle game with many solutions. Each level has a set of materials and a budget to work within, but it is up to you how you want to end up bridging the gap. That’s not to say most of your “solutions” won’t fail miserably. You are also constrained by physics and the properties of the given materials. In addition, it is not enough that your structure simply doesn’t collapse under its own weight; it has to be able to support traffic of different loads. I’ve lost count of the number of times that some trucks barely made it to the other side as my bridge built with spit and good intentions fell into a shattered ruin in their wake. This makes even the failures entertaining as you look what when wrong and iteratively adjust your design until it works. I definitely recommend it to the engineering-minded as it will occasionally force you to think outside the box to solve the problem at hand.

review:
Cannot reccomend this game why?

This awesome game is tied to Ubisoft uplay/ubisoft connect. To get the game to run you will need to 

1. download Ubisoft connect 

2. uninstall uplay 

3. Sign into or make a Ubisoft account

4. Take your steam game key and redeem it on Ubisoft launcher 

5. You can now play this game on steam

Seems easy enough right? Well Ubisoft is tanking faster right now than a merchant ship hit with a spread of 4 torpedos. Some think they will either go bankrupt or be bought out by another company in the coming years some say as early as 2025 but it’s just speculation.

 If that happens for however long you will presumably not be able to play this game due to it needing to connect to Ubisoft servers to launch the game. 

You can kind of see this Happening with other Ubisoft titles from around this time with similar DRM measures. Like Assasins creed 2 where you cannot play paid dlc anymore due to servers for that being shut down around 2022. Or games like The crew no longer being playable at all. Or farcry 3 and assassins creed 3 where dlc cant be played anymore

If the servers go down you simply you won’t be able to play your game anymore  

(This is more or less a buyer beware your game could potentially become unplayable in the near future. Or atleast unplayable for an unknown period of time if Ubisoft goes bankrupt/closes down/sells its company off)
