name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
A good game for fans of hex-based tactical simulation or 40k.

The art style is quite detailed and looks good. Armageddon really looks like a grim, war-torn environment. Of course, the environments do get rather samey over the course of the campaign.

As expected in such games, you use terrain to your advantage and figure out which units work against which. Damage is predicted quite accurately so you won't face many surprises (unless you scout badly).

The story is presented in a mature way, with believable characters (orks will of course be orks). You really feel like you're in a desperate war against the orks, and the war feels like an actual war with proper strategy being used.

Mission variety is pretty good in Act 1, in Act 2 there's less variety.

Once you get access to space marine units and the high end Steel Legion units, the dynamic of the campaign changes quite a bit and you feel way more powerful. At least on normal difficulty, Act 2 felt far less challenging, but after being bullied by the orks for the entire first half of the game, I can't say I minded it too much.

AI will focus fire exposed units quite hard, but is also very passive in missions where they're the defender (on normal difficulty).

There is a big variety of units, which doesn't mean all of them are viable, but it's fun to play around with regardless.

The game has decent replayability if you want to attempt a higher difficulty, or make different choices (the choices aren't super impactful except for a few times where you get branching missions, but still worth exploring if you're curious about the different outcomes).

Every mission has a certain amount of 'requisition' and units unlock at set times. You usually have more than enough requisition to fill out all your army slots, which doesn't incentivise using cheap troops to get a bigger army (though you could do it to have more requisition left to replenish your wounded troops, I suppose) so the extent to which you manage your army comes down to trying to maintain veterans and choosing the 'right' units.

There's a good amount of content here, towards the end it did get slightly repetitive, once the 'tech tree' had been fully explored, and started to drag a little, but perhaps that's because I binged it and it did keep me engrossed enough to carry it through to the end with no major breaks between sessions (a very rare thing for me).

A solid 7.5./10. I recommend picking it up on a sale.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
This game is greatly underrated. You'll find few Warhammer games that have such a coherent and immersive story campaign as Armageddon does. If you don't judge the game by it's 2D-visuals, you'll find a challenging strategy game that will keep you hooked, mission after mission. 

You'll interact with plenty well-written characters during the briefings and debriefings, make choices on which front to fight and build yourself an army to command against overwhelming foes. 

If you are a fan of Warhammer and/or strategy games, do yourself a favor and get it now!

review:
I absolutely love this game. I tend to agree with others though that you should hold out for a sale (I snatched it up during a skulls event for less than $25 for everything). I can't add anything about the game that hasn't already been said, and probably in a better way than I can manage at the moment. 

I'm here to point out one thing. I keep seeing a lot of comparisons to Panzer General, and yeah, it is more or less, but ...It's also pretty much a functional version of Final Liberation. Which was pretty much exactly this game, but with really sweet (sometimes goofy) live action cut scenes in it. Great game, if you've got an older pc around, and like to tinker with game code that was never quite right to start with I highly recommend it...also in that vein only requiring a lot more work to get running (assuming you can find a copy) is Chaos Gate.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Actually half-decent Warhammer 40,000 strategy game.
Difficult, even on normal difficulty. 
Huge unit variety, that is the most attractive feature of the game.
Access to Steel Legion, Titans, Ultramarines, Blood Angels, and Salamanders. Assorted infantry, support/scout vehicles, tanks, anti-aircraft, walkers, super-heavy tanks, aircraft etc. It's all here.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
+
Excellent turn based, hexagon, Panzer General style game. Based on Warhammer 40k, a sci-fi game with humans versus greenskin Orcs with lasers.
Campaign with very many, varied,  scenarios. Troops improve in quality. Limited resources.
Added campaigns for Space Marine super-soldiers from various Chapters. 
Tactics are vital.  Have you troops in trenches with a machine gun behind them. It will fire in support and pile up those dead greenies.
Varied storyline, choice branches. 
Many troop types, so you can replay campaign. For example play tank or infantry or Titan (mech) oriented, as you wish. Sniper spot far (arty shoots far!) but get eaten if they get too close.
Full text and voice scenes between scenarios. Pro voice actors. Pro artists and story (from WH40k).
Vary unit mix to win.  {E.g. I like basic infantry, but needed to upgrade them to Ogryns for city fights).
No bugs [except in mine a couple of the between scenario pictures were corrupt. Didn't damage game].
Choice of difficulty.
-
Vast horde worries. Lodsa Orkies. Kids will cry as their scattered forces are overrun by the green tide... Stay together and you can't get all the Objectives. Lose units a lot and they wont get experienced...
A bit pricey for an older game...
Not all troop types get used. (E.g. I didn't bother with aircraft.]
Game graphics are showing their age [compare old Panzer General to Order Of Battle].

+++ If you like it you can buy the Orc campaign with Thraka and his attack squigs! +++

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
TL;DR

For 37 euros this game is ridiculous. Buy it on sale, only if:

- the discount is at least 60%, imho 10 bucks is realistic for this game
- you like Panzer General and/or you are a Warhammer 40k fan
- you don't mind barebone graphics and sound
- you don't mind playing a game which feels like a veeeery budget title from all aspect

Update: I played this game for around 10 hours more and yep, it doesn't get any better. AI is still brain dead, if it moves units around (or does anything at all, apart of counter attacking), then it's scripted as fuck, like only attacks mission critical units, or when it's an invasion scenario. The only difficulty comes from two factors, one is that time to time you are totally drained of requisition points for a scenario or two and the second is that orcs are present on the maps in a much higher number and they have access to vastly superior units. Shame, this easily could have been a pretty good game, albeit it seems Slitherine/devs do nothing but churning out overpriced DLCs.


Wall of text

Ok, so I love turn based strategy, especially the Panzer/Fantasy/whatever General series and I like Warhammer 40k, but never played board / tabletop games, only read the books of Ian Watson. This game is basically a Panzer General clone, with very bad usability / quality of life value, but decent fun to play (except at that price, which is fucking insane for a 6 years old game, but Slitherine's favourite sport is charging an arm and a leg for a game, which doesn't worth that much).

I can't talk about the authenticity to the Wh40k lore, since I don't know it in detail, but it really scratch that Panzer General itch of mine. Although I have to say, you have to make big compromises at this game and the sad part is, the some of it could be easily patched.

Stuff which I particularly disliked (played on normal difficulty):

- AI is borderline to non-existent, I mean enemy units don't really do anything at all, unless it's scripted to do something  (e.g. you approach an enemy unit too close, trigger an event and such). It happens to an extent, that (for example) you can comfortably use snipers to wear down huge orc melee units, because they don't give a shit. While if the AI would be so gracious to move the orc melee units 3-4 tiles, they would easily mow down my snipers. But they don't, because of reasons. If you played Firaxis' Civ games, then I can say that AI there is something like a genius, compared to this. So I would say it's more of a puzzle game for me, rather than a strategy game. It's also impossible to call it a strategy game, as for the first couple of maps, those are way too small to apply any tactics at all.
- Some fluff/flavour text/lore/description for existing units would be appreciated, because if someone is not a Wh40k buff, then most of them don't mean shit to him. That's especially a part I don't understand why is it missing, as this doesn't require huge production value.
- There are unit slots, which I did and still call the laziest fucking possible way to balance a Panzer General like(ish) game. Still, at least one slot means one unit, not like Panzer Corps 2, where you have to sacrifice multiple slots even for infantry transport. Not a big comfort for me, though.
- Usability / quality of life is basically non-existent and/or the developers just didn't give shit. For example if the enemy moves it's units, you have to watch it (which is normal). But if enemy movement is covered by fog of war, you still have to wait for it, making moves. So for a large enemy army on move, it means you will stare at the screen for 10-20-whatever seconds, until stuff happens hidden by the fog of war. I mean WTF?!
- I don't know if this game adapts the tabletop rules per se, but some stuff is fucking annoying for someone who just likes TBS games, but doesn't know Wh40k games in details. Like air units can't occupy the same slots as ground units. It may make sense for enemy units (due to weapon range), but for friendly units, it's very annoying. Also, air units work an odd way, e.g. my gunship was taken down by a flamethrower unit. I mean da fuck, effectively a melee unit took down a flying unit?!
- Some missions are simply annoying and/or sloppily explained. For instance order is to attack and hold 4 victory hexes. Ok... then an enemy counter attack triggered and I get the order to move at least 4 units to the rightmost part of the map. Ok... but the original victory hexes are still there, and the mission information is not updated and still says to hold 4 victory hexes. What...the...fuck?! Am I supposed to move 4 units to the right and meanwhile hold the victory hexes, or move all units, but four minimum, or...? It turned out to be a full retreat, but it is really not told anywhere at all.
- Other mission briefing is like check crash sites for a pilot, but there are multiple ones, so I have to check all them. Mission information says I have to hold 2 of them, marked with flag. But there are like 10 or more crash sites, all of them marked with special hex symbol, two of them with flags. So what...the...fuck...?! Am I supposed to check all of them, then at the end hold two, or just focus on the two, or...? Oh, by the way it turned out that only the two ones matter with flags, the remaining god knows why are there.

Ok, I stop the rant, my point is that this game is on the market for 6 years, yet neither developers, nor Slitherine gave a shit about fixing obvious issues, but obviously, overpriced DLCs are out there as usual. Costing you over 60 euros for the whole shebang, which is the same amount, you will pay for a AAA game developed from like a 100 million USD, which fact makes me feel like this:

https://media.giphy.com/media/Vg0JstydL8HCg/giphy.gif

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I gave it a second try. I listened to the people who play Warhammer 40k. I tried the multi-player.  THAT was god awful. The rando that played it was not very sportsman like. The Warhammer 40K gaming community is HIGHLY toxic. Even for those who play a video game.

 After playing this video game with a toxic 40K community member, I decided to get rid of all 40K models/bock/ paraphernalia in the real world.  It is time to step away from Games Work Shop. When you compare this video game and GW, both have a high model count required for a game, or in a game. This is sort of a forced consumption of consumerism and materialism that is beyond unhealthy. I get the impression the high number of model counts is a mental illness like a hoarder. Event he high numbers in the video game fuel this.   

  We (myself and a pro-twitch streamer) evaluated it on a live stream of it. That went over like a dirty diaper on the lunch table.  Multiplayer was not an option. Viewership went from good, to noone. Top complaints were the color pallet being all brown, the bad animations and graphics, and the extreme boring game play.  The in game dialogue was pretty harsh and set off a TOXIC melt down with multiple people.  As one of the viewers put it, “I have a shitty life, I play (video) games to escape, I don’t want to listen to some game put me down, nah f**K that”. The game itself promoted the toxic culture by verbally abusing the player in the cut scenes. I felt in necessary to apologies for the game and for us deciding to play the game. Despite all the research we put into, nobody found it entertaining. We couldn't even convince ourselves to keep it going. 


The summation of the game is to pound infantry with artillery. The summation of the game is to pound infantry with artillery to ad nasuseam. 



Original review below


This is about the stupidest and most obnoxious game I have ever played.

I have reconsidered my original opinion, my new opinion is Games WorkShop (40K), and everything about Games WorkShop needs to go away.  The whole game model is just a tedious grind where you get more infantry to acts a shields while your armor beats down your opponents infantry.  This game, the rules and the whole concept is dated, like 1950s bad, dated.  No tactics, no planning, no thought beyond clink and point send a mad horde of infantry and armor to overpower your opponent, before the turn counter runs out.   Seriously, who the heck wins a war by throwing away armor and life, in reckless and needless fashions?  Games Work shop and all it products are the worst games imaginable for strategy.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
The only 40k game that lets you have 25,000+ models on the map over large scale distances.
Each icon on the map is made up of individual models.  For example a hex with a unit icon in it like the will have a number under it.  That number is how many models are apart of that group.  When an Icon attacks another icon, the models are all rolling for their shots.  So if you have  baneblade that has 2 under the icon, that means there is 2 models in that group. The models all have their own wounds, which you can see when you hover over the icon.  It is just like the table top.  Only you get to control so much more.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: This is an excellent wargame which balances interesting mechanics to model the the complexities of warfare in the 40k universe without becoming excessively complicated (like Gary Grigsby's War in the East/West). Each unit has a number of stats that determine their effectiveness in different situations: vision distance, initiative, armour, weapon damage, armour piercing, movement level/type etc. From, looking at the new 40k Gladius also from the publisher Slitherine I think I prefer this because the individual scenarios each with different objectives and maps seem to be more tactical with a deployment phase, with limited requisition points that carry over from mission to mission and are also required to reinforce your squads. Furthermore, the squads gain experience from their kills and loose it when some of the squad dies. Squads also carry over from mission to mission as long as they survive and within their unit category (Infantry, Vehicles, Walkers, Armour, Airpower, Titans) can be switched to different unit types as the mission requires. You therefore need to value the lives of your squads and learn to give them wise orders. All these mechanics, the huge unit variety along with varied routes within maps to complete objectives opens up a space for more strategic as well as tactical decision making. The Armageddon story is also interesting with good voice acting. I like the graphical style of the game which provides a pleasing form of abstraction like a board game. I wish they would add more campaigns, a skirmish mode different objective types against the AI and more races to the game. Like the Tyranid invasion of a Cadian Guard Jungle world. Adding Tau, Necrons, chaos Marines, Eldar etc would make the game even more awesome. Voice overs for the infantry would be good also 'For the Emperor!'.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
'Do you see that?' he asked quietly.

 

At first, only silence followed. Hesitantly, the Guard soldiers began to cast glances at each other, uncomfortable with the Chaplain's presence and confused by his behaviour. All eyes were on him now. Grimaldus aimed his mace out at the advancing hordes. Thousands. Tens of thousands. And only the very beginning.

 

'Do you see that?' he roared at the humans. the closest ranks flinched back from the mechanical bark that issued almost deafeningly loud from his skull helm.

 

'Answer me!'

 

He received several trembling nods. 'Yes, sir...' uttered a handful of them, the speakers faceless within the masses of their rebreather masks.

 

Grimaldus turned his back to the wasteland, already dark with the teeming, chaotic ranks of the enemy. At first, his helm emitted a low, vox-distorted chuckle. Within a few seconds, he was laughing, laughing up at the burning sky while aiming his crozius hammer at the enemy.

 

'Are you as insulted as I am? This is what they send against us?'

 

He turned back to the men, the laughter fading, but amused contempt filling his voice even through the inhumanising vocalisers of his helm.

 

'This is what they send? This rabble? We hold one of the mightiest cities on the face of the planet. The fury of its guns sends all skyborne enemies to the ground in flames. We stand united in our thousands - our weapons without number, our purity without question, and our hearts beating courage through our blood. And this is how they attack us?'

 

'Brothers and sisters... a legion of beggars and alien dregs wheezes its way across the plains. Forgive me when the moment comes that they whine and weep against our walls. Forgive me that I must order you to waste ammunition upon their worthless bodies.'

 

Grimaldus paused, lowering his weapons at last, turning his back on the invaders as if bored bu their very existence. His attention was focussed entirely upon the soldiers below him.

 

'I have heard many souls speak my name in whispers since I came to Helsreach. I ask you now: Do you know me?'

 

'Yes' several voices replied, several among the hundreds.

 

'Do you know me?' he bellowed at them over the firing of the wall guns.

 

'Yes!' a chorus answered now.

 

'I am Grimaldus of the Black Templars! A brother to the Steel Legions of this defiant world!'

 

A muted cheer greeted his words. It wasn't enough, not even close.

 

'Never again in life will your actions carry such consequences. Never again will you serve as you serve now. No duty will matter as much, and no glory will taste as ture. We are the defenders of Helsreach. On this day, we carve our legend in the flesh of every alien we slay. Will you stand with me?'

 

Now the cheers came in truth. They thundered in the air around him.

 

'Will you stand with me?'

 

Again, a roar.

 

'Sons ans daughters of the Imperium! our blood is the blood of heroes and martyrs! The xenos dare defile our sacred city? They dare tread the sacred soil of our world? We will throw their bodies from these walls when the final day dawns!'

 

A wave of noise crashed against his armour as they cheered. Grimaldus raised his war maul, aiming it to the embattled heavens.

 

'This is our city! This is our world! Say it! Say it! Cry it out so the bastards in orbit will hear our fury! Our city! Our world!'

 

'OUR CITY! OUR WORLD!'

 

Laughing again, Grimaldus turned to face the oncoming horde. '*Run, alien dogs! Come to me! Come to us all! Come die in blood and fire!'

 

'BLOOD AND FIRE!'

 

The Reclusiarch cut the air with his crozius, as if ordering his men forward. 'For the Templars! For the Legion! For Helsreach!'

 

'FOR HELSREACH!'

 

'Louder!'

 

'FOR HELSREACH!'

 

'They cannot hear you, brothers!'

 

'FOR HELSREACH!'

 

'Hurl yourselves at these walls, inhuman filth! Die on our blades! I am Grimaldus of the BLack Templars, and I will cast your carcasses from these holy walls!'

 

'GRIMALDUS! GRIMALDUS! GRIMALDUS!'


 
Grimaldus nodded, still staring out over the wastelands, letting the cheering chant mix with the howling wind, knowing it would carry to the advancing enemy.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Just let's keep this short.

You find yourself as the commander of an imperial defence army and have to kill orks to save the hive world Armaggedon. Fair enough.

You do so by commanding a small army chosen from over 70 different units consisiting of infantry, tanks, vehicles planes and artillery per faction. Each unit has its own combination of weaponry, so basically no two units are the same. The turn based combat is sometimes unpredictable and scouting is hard, since each unit has only one move per turn.

There are some fairly easy missions, and then there are some crushingly hard ones, even those that you are not supposed to win, long time no seeing that!

All in all I love it! One of the best turn based strategy games for me since the battle isle games on my old DOS computer. 

Completed the main story (Imperial Steel Legion-Campaign) in about 30 hours.
So far I did not play the DLCs.

Too bad it lacks a multiplayer and has some strange missing file bugs during cutscenes, which will give you a headache.

Panzer General 40.000: Armageddon, is a good tactical strategy game. I'm about 2/3rds through the main campaign, and enjoying it quite a bit. Those people complaining and obsessing about the turn limit, don't seem to get that this is an integral part of the challenge: you have to complete your objectives in time! THe turn limit is not just effective for the gameplay, but also realistic.

Obviously this is not an AAA title. Some of the voice acting is not so great, the units are not fully animated, etc. But I think it is all fine, for the genre. Not sure it's worth almost 37€ though. I would probably have price it at around 25€.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Simply having every single forgeworld variant of a GW model a good game does not make. It's Final Liberation yet... not as good.  

Nothing new to bed said that isn't said in other reviews, so in summary:

-Hideously overpriced - I got it on sale for $20 and still felt a bit ripped off
-Rushing is pretty much the only viable tactic given the time constraint
-The enemy are legion, and you really get curb stomped a lot of the time as your raw recruits get hit by veteran Nobz en masse
-Having so many unit variants is great... unless you aren't incredibly familiar with GW 40K. I've been involved for over 20 years, so I'm pretty savvy on the differences between a Leman Russ Exterminator, Punisher, and Vindicator.... but for a "lay person" it's just overwhelming. Less is more guys! 
-The overall gameplay feels shallow and unispired, especially when you can get a SIGNIFICANTLY better game with Panzercorp, or the newer Order of Battle: World War (don't be a dupe, it's NOT free to play - it's a bit of a dickish way to give a demo you then pay to play various campaigns)

That is all. Glory to the Emperor.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Barely passing grade. The game works (crashes every once in a while though). The game turns go pretty fast. I really don't mind that there is very little animation, the actual unit sprites look good and the sound effects are not bad, some of them quite good actually. I'm satisfied with the graphics and the interface, and the game has a decent amount of content. If you create your own rules for yourself, you can make the game challenging and somewhat interesting, using a wide range of well known Imperial guard and space marine units. 

Even though this game is more polished, I would say that I still prefer the old Final Liberation, which was a lot more dynamic than this game. It had its own ballance issues, but I think it had a better morale/ victory point system, and there were more interesting differences between the strengths and weaknesses of different units. 

I don't know if I've ever played a strategy game with such one-dimensional gameplay before. It doesn't seem to encourage any kind of complex tactics, and the gameplay is pretty repetitive. 

There is a huge variety of units, but they're poorly ballanced, especially not on higher difficulty. 

The best tactic seems to be to rely very heavily on the toughest units.

The Furioso Dreadnought unit seems to be the best unit in the game, followed by the other space marine dreadnoughts, which are also excellent. At max difficulty, most units just seem to be virtually useless, as they cease to be effective even against the units which they should have a big advantage against. Armour and durability is absolutely essential for most of your fron-line units. Most infantry just sucks at the high difficulty settings, it dies way too quickly even when trying to fight lower quality infantry out in the open. 

That being said, I kind-of prefer using infantry, but it seems to make more sense to do it on a lower difficulty setting. You need to create your own restrictions so that the game isn't too easy on those lower difficulty settings though.

Only infantry can enter buildings and a lot of objectives, so you will usually need a couple of infantry units for that

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Fantastic Game! Can't speak for every DLC, but overall, this is a great game. I can only begin to theorize about why the critic reviews are lukewarm.

I think the game is beautiful, with amazing animations taking center stage for the visuals. They remind me of the explosions of world in conflict, that's how good they are. It is really fun to play the imperial campaign, and the story is certainly entertaining enough for a Warhammer 40k game, and the voice acting is quite a bit better than I would expect from an independent project like this one. The sound is a little less impressive, but it does a good job at setting the Warhammer 40k tone nonetheless. The tactics are good, and the progression through the campaign alongside your units is absolutely fun to say the least. Most of the time, missions are "more of the same," but that is a good thing since the tactics, combat, units and action are great. The game includes an amazing roster of units as well, which is sure to please any Warhammer 40k fan. I absolutely love how these units play and feel, and most of them are great fun to utilize.

That's not to say that there aren't any flaws. For one, the game is a bit unbalanced. There are certain units that are just so obviously better than others in terms of cost/benefit ratio that some of the units will, doubtlessly, just never be utilized, which is a real shame. Another thing I should mention is that mission objectives can be somewhat ambiguous on some missions, requiring a restart after an unexpected "defeat" screen. But this is rare, and thus not too bothersome. Another flaw often mentioned is the AI. I played the main campaign on "challenging" difficulty, and this is probably the one the game is balanced for. I think it's a lot of fun to play on this difficulty, but it is true that the AI sometimes makes quite questionable decisions which can be exploited to great effect. Then again, the enemies *are* orks, so what can one expect? It would be nice if I could change the difficulty after starting the campaign, but I have not seen the option. That is a problem too. Finally, there are some graphical bugs that can require a game restart, but really, I don't find that any of these things detract too much from the fun of gameplay during single player. Due to the imbalance, multiplayer probably wouldn't be quite the same amount of fun, however.

Overall, I think that this is a fantastic game. I will play the DLCs, and I will probably purchase the ork "expansion." I really hope that we will see more of the same content from the developers. I would love another game where we play Adeptus Sororitas vs Chaos or Dark Eldar, for example. Any combination of factions that aren't usually covered in these games would be amazing. I would gobble it up. But, for $40, the game is kind of a difficult sale, being such an indie title relying on such ancient game engines, so I understand why people might be so harsh on it. Still though, I had more fun playing this game than I have playing at least 60% of AAA titles. Honestly! This game is great, as long as you can look past the flaws and think of it less as a seriously competitive title, and more as a fun, challenging, and relaxing experience on a weekend when you have the time. If you are a warhammer 40k fan, and you can appreciate turn based titles, you owe it to yourself to get this game. It is amazing.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
WH40K: Armageddon is a nostalgic throwback to 20+ years ago for me. It blends the feel of Games Workshop's tabletop Epic 40K variant with the mechanics of early hex-based PC strategy games like Battleground Ardennes (from... 1995?!?? I feel so old, sigh).

There's familiar fun to be had here for someone like me who has fond memories of the classics that inspired this game. It scratches a specific itch for me, but I can see why some folks would hate it and dismiss it as dated and clunky.

Below I'll add a list of good and bad stuff as I think of it.

+ If you are a fan of classic hex-based strategy and 40K, this game will appeal for sure.
+ Simple gameplay that is easy to pick up and play a mission or two.
+ Personally I love the basic, clean graphics.

= Mediocre voice acting and plot, but I can live with it.

- UI is massively dated, and not in a cute retro sense.
- No way to rebind keys in game. Surely any game released in the 21st century should have this function?
- Arbitrary turn caps. In some missions I finished 10 turns early, on one occasion I 'lost' after hitting 15/15 with every Ork unit dead and a unit 1 hex from the remaining victory point. Really annoying.
- A low unit cap means you can't choose quantity over quality, e.g. you cannot use dozens of guardsmen units as a meatshield. You are railroaded into fielding elite units, so it turns into an Astra Militarum-skinned panzer game.
- Loads of units to choose from, but only a few worth using unless you want to roleplay.
- Confusing naming choices for many units. Some will definitely stick out as confusing or plain wrong if you know 40K lore.
- Implementation of flyers seems incomplete, rendering them basically useless.

The game appeared overpriced to me for what it was, so it sat on my wishlist for a long time. I finally grabbed it with all the DLC for about £7.50 on Fanatical, and I'd say it's definitely worth it to me at that price.

There are also mods for Tau and Tyranid if that is of interest to you. If you like either WH 40k or the hex strategy genre this is a must buy.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
-Probably a bit too tedious for most people. This game is missing some quality of life features, like finding units that have and haven't moved or attacked. There is a way to cycle through units, but it just doesn't help much.

-They AI didn't seem very responsive. Sometimes they would just wait for many turns, not moving, not attacking, just waiting for me to trigger some script.

-The units stick around from battle to battle, and you can change a unit's role/job before each battle without penalty. Plus, the unit keeps its experience. Nice.

-The ability to pick your dialogue in conversations is a nice touch. It makes the game seems much more immersive. A really old feature that feels fresh.

-I only got into a few a of the story scenes, but I had a hard time caring about what story was. Was there a story? I don't remember.

-Personally, I would pick up Battle for Wesnoth instead. The game play feels about the same, but I liked it much better.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Very fun tactical wargame in the vein of Panzer Corps (in fact it's very similar). It gives you the sense of massive 40k battles quite well. There are a huge amount of units that you unlock throughout the campaign so that means you can go back and try different army compositions. The main campaign can get pretty hard, but is fair and ultimately beatable (unlike some of the DLCs). There are a few improvements that could be made like, air units being able to stack with ground units and the possibility of scouting units, but hopefully these could be incorporated into Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon 2. Overall recommended if you like turn-based wargames.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Love turn based strategy, love 40k, and this has many of the features that would make for an excellent game much like the earlier games in their vein from GW, and Axis and Allies, Panzer Corps etc.

Sadly the level design is awful, the script was written by someone who has never played 40k and/or failed creative writing, the units are too samey, the only strategy is to out-range your opponent, none of the development mechanics are ever explained, if they do anything at all, and every mission feels exactly the same: Find a bottleneck, hold it.

Its so sad to see two of my favourite things put together so badly.

The imperium, even the forces commanded by you, seem terribly disorganised, you never have any intelligence and know nothing of the forces you face, your auxillary troops start isolated and without any support, their only role every time is to die whilst you get your troops to a bottleneck. Why you couldnt just deploy them at the bottleneck is never actually explained... And most missions you go into with no clear objective... Then the AI will litterally sometimes sit out of range turn after turn and get blown up by your artillery. 
This last one I'm actually glad of, as it makes a change to the usual find a bottleneck and wait approach, but it is still clearly a failing.
Gawd, the list of awful just goes on an on.

The good:
- Looks. The units are detailed if not animated, the firing animations are varied and pretty. It isnt going to win any graphical awards, but someone has put a lot of time into the units and it shows. Maps are ok, but unexceptional.
- Voiced Story. They had the right idea, they link together the encounters and break them up. Sadly the script sucks and the voice acting is average, this last part I dont mind at all, I'll take average voice acting over a terrible script 100% of the time. I want to feel more fervor from the imperiums xenophobes, I want to fear the commisars, not give them backchat, and having a random comms officer tell them they are wrong is bananas...
- Unit progression. It...at least looks like... it has unit progression. And you keep your troops, this is excellent! Sadly I havent the faintest idea what gathering experience does, there are no abilities to unlock, stats dont appear to improve, other than they have a little bar of skulls that goes up and down (as casualties are taken).
- Enjoyable, untill you realise the "progress" is all an illusion. Units gain nothing, the "story" is a linked series of random encounters, with random units, deployed in random places for presumably random reasons.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: fun game to play BUT when the save game function keeps crashing the game it is impossible to play!! this is something that NEEEDED to be fixed in a patch  before now. save your money

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
It was an OK game. The DLC wasn't really worth it because the base game wasn't enjoyable enough to warrant the purchase. 
I not really sure how I'd improve the game, so I can't really offer many suggestions but it was definitely missing something

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Pro:
- Only W40k game in this "style" so far
- It's rather simplicistic but still a challenge to some point
- The variety of units is awesome

Con:
- You need some time to get used to the game
- A little bit of brown in brown and grey in grey

All in all I really enjoyed the game.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: A very nice wargame with a cool vibe. In the first missions you are purposefully put in losing situations, making you feel even better when, later on, your army start to push back the enemy with your now experienced (and beloved) troops. It's hard, but extremely satisfying. I suggest to play at the almost hardest difficulty: it really helps to keep the player always on the edge of a terrible situations, so you have to consider very well if it is better to use your few resources to reinforce now your battered infantry team, or to wait and buy a new tank at the end of the mission, or to stockpile them for an even bigger tank later on.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: fun game if you like the turn based wargames and it has a warhammer setting. bought on sale. no regrets.  not much replay value but it was a good go. i still have the DLCs to complete eventually.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
15$ Game
45$ Price Tag

Looks horrible and plays pretty bad.
Campaign is pretty boring and just sorta not fun, mission objectives are rather 'obscure'

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: like the game reminds of an older title cant quite remember its name but game mechanics are very much the same.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Fun game but because of the round limit most of your creativity comes in the form of unit composition and placement.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: I thought this was a good game to play.  It's only four years old, but it reminds me of the old Panzer General games from the late 1990s in both graphics, sounds, and features.  I got it on sale for a fair price, which is why I went through with the purchase.  However, after playing it for a while, I didn't really see the point in any of it... sort of like Panzer General.  I guess that if I had some care about the Warhammer universe, then maybe that would have added something to it for me, but I don't know.  Really, for the $17 I paid for the complete edition, I didn't think it was really worth it, which is why I refunded it.  To be honest, I would recommend this game in its complete version if it was being sold for $4.99.  Above that, and I am not sure you would get your money's worth.  But as far as the genre goes, it is nevertheless fun.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
This game started out alright as a functioning turn-based strategy in the WH40k universe and something to play when limited to a laptop without much gaming power. It showed some promise in the gameplay style despite a general lack of production value and polish. The interface is not great and it was difficult to determine the controls for some functions.

The real showstopper for me though were missions that couldn't be completed, not because the play was difficult or even challenging, but because the poorly-indicated success conditions didn't work even when achieved. There are some really unfortunate, even amateurish, problems in the premise and execution of certain missions' design. I could probably forgive if it was just nonsensical objectives communicated poorly, but it's worse than that: sometimes the game just doesn't give you the win even when you've verified on the dev's own forums what the actual win conditions for a particular mission are.

When I first bought this I played ten hours and gave up on it because I couldn't progress in the campaign due to the above issues. I took a few months off, tried again from the beginning and gave it another honest shot. Same issues, despite the devs having clearly taken some steps to recognize the probems players are having: responses of instructions and walkthroughs on the game forums, videos and screenshots from beta testers. One ugly bit is that they patched some ingame "tips" in: essentially minor spoilers pushed on the player to mitigate the bad mission design. If you have to do that, the problems run deep. If it's unwinnable without knowing the twists ahead of time, and STILL unwinnable even then because the game simply does not function as intended, that is a bad situation.

I wish I had not supported this game and this developer. Avoid.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Such a awesome game, you won't be disappointed if you like hex based combat games. They did a good job with this one, especially if you like the Warhammer franchise.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: A decent game.  I am playing it for the Mac OS.  There are occasional crashes.   The main campaign is fun.  The expansions are a little repetitive.  I have played through Vulkan's Wrath and I am half way through the Blood Angels' campaign.  Aside from minor improvements, like a slight gain in requisition points per turn,  I don't really notice any differences in the DLC so far.  Hopefully that will change in the other expansion.  As of now, I give the game a 7/10.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: This is a great top down tactical war game for 40k.   I've got all the expansions and have played through most of them.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I haven't gotten very far into the game yet, but I can tell it's pretty good. The turn-based combat is quite involved, and I'm enjoying it. 

My only gripes, and the reason I wanted to write this review, is that some of the writing is abysmal, and while the extensive voice acting is of good quality, they mispronounce a ton of the lore-specific words.
 
I guess this is my way of saying that this game is very close to being pretty great. I just wish they'd hired better writers who understood the lore of the game well enough to avoid sounding fairly silly. 
Still, it's more than most games have.

The animations leave a bit to be desired, but it's generally a fairly decent-looking game.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Very plain gameplay, bad tutorial, miserable sounds, no music, no effects, no atmosphere...

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: I would recomend this to anyone who likes a strategy game.  The only shame is there isn't more content to buy.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
This is an almost perfect Warhammer 40k game. Add in a mod or two (look it up online, you will be glad you did!) and the game gets even better. 

Also: it frequently goes on sale for a very reasonable price. 

I highly recommend it :-)

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: It's a Panzer Corp knock off, as Rites of War was a Panzer General knockoff.  It's a very different game though and not in a great way.  I like this game, but I really love both 40k and Panzer Corps.  It's nowhere near as complex as PC or as a good, but if you really love 40k and hex games it's 5-10$ not too badly spent.  I wouldn't get it at full price.  The DLC is a bit of a rip off too.  I really hope that when Panzer Corp II drops we'll get a better iteration.  I can get somewhat frustrating to play this game given that the difficultly levels are not set very well, so watch out of that.  On the hardest levels it's pretty close to unbeatable.  You should also savescum liberally.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I thought it would be a fun turn-based strategy, but man was I dead wrong. Horrible UI, mechanics that just don't make sense (It tries to copy the tabletop in a hilariously bad manner), and it crashed within 15 mins of play. Another 40k game loaded into the garbage pile. 

I'm SO glad I didn't buy this at full price, because it's not even worth $10. Thank god for refunds!

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
**RECOMMEND - GETTING ON 75% OFF SALE**

It gives a nice relaxing and scale-accurate look at battles in the 41st Millenium. 
Want to send Legions of Imperial Soldiers to die in battle against tides of Orks? You can.

Want to have a Vanguard of Sentinels scout ahead with elite infantry and armor sweeping in behind for the killing blows with Artillery support? You can.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
If you like turn-based games quite a bit like the old Warhammer 40K Tabletop games, this is your game. Overall I like it and it is a relief that this is decent considering the garbage over-hyped Warhammer 40K games have been such as DOW 3, Space Marine, the RTS, etc.

The only negative I can find is that the story in the campaign is very hollow and doesn't really immerse you into the Warhammer 40K universe very much and the game play gets old after a bit.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Here are my impressions of the game and its DLC campaigns, I wrote them each as I progressed through the game, I picked the middle 'challenging' difficulty and I think that was the right choice to have a good balance of difficulty and fun, not difficult enough to require constant reloading of saves to get perfect attack rolls all the time, but still hard enough to only win by a hair on some missions.

some overall positives and negatives:

[b]+[/b] hex-based turn based strategy in the WH40k Universe
[b]+[/b] the story is typical, but fitting
[b]+[/b] no bugs/crashes encountered
[b]+[/b] not too easy and given the 5 possible diffiulties offered everyone should find their place
[b]+[/b] good combat and atmosphere sounds
[b]+[/b] 100 hours of playtime if you do all the DLCs
[b]+[/b] the outcome prediction before attacking really helps making the right decisions and learning

[b]-[/b] no cinematics (if you paid full price this might be a let down)
[b]-[/b] the units have only limited animations(ie when attacking expect some scale fitting fireworks)
[b]-[/b] the difficulty curve is a bit off sometimes from mission to mission
[b]-[/b] some of the mechanics have to be found out the hard way
[b]-[/b] the mission objective descriptions are a bit lacking, like for extra objectives or changing goals
[b]-[/b] there is a big selection of units,but some units are just fluff / minor weapons changes

[u][b]Base Campaign[/b][/u]

This takes you through the whole story with text dialogues inbetween missions, sometimes giving you choices and fitting voiceovers. The campaign is divided into multiple acts and several missions each, its easy to get 30-40 hours out of it. You play with Imperial Guard units in the beginning and around the middle of the campaign you get some access to Space Marine Units and finally Titans for the last third or so. On the challenging difficulty mode it was indeed sometimes challenging, but if you planned solid from the beginning of the mission a total restart due to turn limit constraints was only two times necessary for me. Nothing stops you from using savegames whenever you want to scout the map or try different deployment options and positions. The campaign is actually really well done in the sense of turning the tide feeling, going from desperation to relieve and the last missions you can field enough powerful units to rightfully avenge the fallen.

[u][b]Vulkan's Wrath - Salamander Campaign[/b][/u]

This is the perspective of the Salamanders, so story line wise you start around in the middle, drive the enemy back, liberate and sometimes hold the line against desperate enemy attempts to overrun you. While you get some story dialogues like in the base campaign, they have no voiceover which is a bit jarring. The missions are in a way harder than the base game due to the limited selection of units, you have to find a new way to get things done, especially if you are used to full artillery + titans, however the Salamanders have above average amor thus their tanks and Dreadnaughts are sturdy enough to get things done, and their selection of infantry can have a big impact too, however be prepared to take some losses. The maps for the mission are sometimes very large and require you to cover some distance or manage your own and alot of additional units, but its all managable and may the emperor be with you. Many of the missions have side objectives that reward you with extra units if you manage to complete them, however due to turn limits and massive amounts of enemies it might require to find a whole new strategy to complete those on top of the original mission.

[u][b]Angel's Death - Blood Angels Campaign[/b][/u]

Not really a favorite of mine, no voiceovers for the inbetween mission speeches, no side objectives encountered, at least you can pick the battlefield of your choice for the last mission (another hive indoor, or an easy defense). While the inside hive missions force you to use alot of infantry units, the amount of strategy you can use to get things done faster is rather limited, especially with the enemy reinforcing his units at every chance you give him (3 units adjacent right next to an enemy will completely stop that, but good luck with that, better kill outright) Just as with the Salamanders, Dreadnaught walkers made the backbone of my army again.

[u][b]Glory of Macragge - Ultramarines Campaign[/b][/u]

About as enjoyable as the Salamander campaign, a good mix of mission types and an epic battle (I picked the open field one) at the end. The sidemissions are quite hard if you play on a high difficulty and it would be nice to know if a mission as a side mission from the start, instead of stumbling on it or not. With the sometimes vast stretches between objectives you have to split up alot, disbanding units that have achieved their goals in order to have enough ressources to repair/fill up others that havent yet was not a rare occurence). Anyway a good conclusion to the Spacemarines action on Armageddon.

[u][b]Ork Hunters Campaign[/b][/u]

So far the easiest campaign, a bit shorter than the others too it seems, no optional mission goals, but you get to decide which end mission you want to do. The Ork Hunter units are quite OP (like each infantry unit is a team of soldiers as seen in the orignal predator movie) vs the feral ork units (and others too actually) but after some grueling missions in the space marines campaigns it was sort of a relief to have a relaxing orc stomping here and a bit of a continuation of the base campaign story.

[u][b]Golgotha Campaign[/b][/u]

A real nail-biting to the last man exhausting but fulfilling campaign. Next time an Exterminatus should be the option of choice for the planet. Anyway expect to have little ressources, weak units and be forced to play every trick (and savegame) up your sleeve to beat these missions, the number of easy ones I can count on 1 finger. Its actually my favorite of all DLCs, but I highly recommend playing all the others before as a preparation.
