name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: cool stuff

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
This game is quite similar to Panzer Corps, which is a previous game made by the same studio. It is a significant improvement over the previous game in terms of having a lot more intressting units. If you liked Panzer Corps, you are probably going to like this one. If you tried and did not like Panzer Corps, this game is probably not for you.

If you have not played Panzer Corps, you might need an explanation of how this game works. It is a turn based tactics game with the main meat of the game being a campain. During the campain, you start of every mission with deploying units based on a point system, there are a LOT of units, and every single unit costs points, varying from 40 (a millita with pistols) to 2100 (a single giant mech). You can basically spend your points however you like until they run out or you run out of deployment slots, which are mainly there for stopping people from going all in on the millita with pistols. (which I would not recommend anyway)

Because of this system, this game is not about having the best units, it is about finding the units that are the most cost effective. Some units have specific traits, like commanders that help your units not get tierd and wanna go home, and support units that help providing counterattack vs anything that tries to hit stuff close to them. Building an army is a real puzzle, with a lot of valid options. Also, you get to keep your army from your previous mission, but can scrap and change units at will. This means that if you have found something that works, you don't have to re-build it for every mission which is nice. However, if you wanna do a full rebuild, you can just scrap everything and get your points back.

During your typical missions, you have to caputre and hold specific hexes. All missions are restricted to a specific number of turns, which means that there is some pressure for getting things moving vs taking things slow and not take as many losses. (Also, getting transports for your footsoldiers and stuff like that). Building up a working army and using it is a nice puzzle and I have had a lot of fun playing the main campain.

The few complaints I have are:

I would say it is a bit expensive for what it is, there is almost no animation, and only the main campain have voice acting (the DLC only have text). The graphics are functionall but won't set the world on fire. If you are unsure about if you like this kind of game, Panzer corps is about half the price and quite similar.

The difficulty settings are a bit rigid. There are 5 difficulty settings, and the jumps from one to another is rather large. It would be nice to have a few more. The difficulty can also not be changed after starting the campain, which made the end of some of the campains easier then some of the earlier missions as I understod the game way better at that point.

It would be nice to have some way of sorting your units better. There are filters, but even filtering only on say, tanks for the steel legion, you get like ~30 results. For example trying to find tanks that are good vs other tanks is kind of annoying, as the options you have are spread over a lot of different categories. This is not a major issue, but towards then end of the campain it gets a little silly.

Warrning for units with movement left when clicking "end turn" is not enabled by default... It is there in the setting, turn it on, will help a lot.

THE DLC:

So, I got the imperium eddition on sale, so I have tried all the DLCs. Basically, each of them introduces a new campain with a different imperial faction. It is nice to have something different to play if you get stuck on a mission, so I would recommend getting the bundle with all the DLC. The DLC campains are not so much about adding new units as they are about removing the standard tools forcing you to play differently. 

The spacemarine chapters have no cannon fodder soldiers or long range artillery, which forces some really different army designs. Those 3 DLC are quite similar, as space marine chapters are quite similar. Finnished the one for Salamanders, played a couple of maps of the other two.

Ork hunters are like steel legion but know what they are doing and bring knives to every gunfight... In that campain you are also fighting a lot of very poorly organized orks, but considering you legions motto is "bring a knife to a gunfight" that might be required to even the ods.

Haven't gotten far into the last campain yet.

CONCLUSION AND PLAYTIME:

So, in about 80 hours, I have finnished a playing the main campain once, and the salamander campain. As well as playing a couple of maps in all the other campains. I think Ill try the main campain on a harder difficulty again, as now I know all the units. The game features a "tutorial campain" which I highly recommend, it teaches you all you need to know. I haven't touched the easiest difficult setting, so I don't think this game would be that hard to enjoy even if you are not good at these sorts of games. 

There is multiplayer, which I haven't touched but there seemed to be games open. Might be fun vs a friend. Would expect this game to be about 160 hours of fun with all the DLC, a lot less without.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Panzer General/Corps with Orks, Titans and Space Marines!

Really, that's pretty much it.  If you don't know what Panzer Corps is, you should go and play it; it's an absolute classic wargame at this point.

If you don't know what Warhammer 40,000 is, I don't even know where to start explaining it.

Okay, there are some specific pros and cons to this title.  Firstly, the unit balancing is pretty terrible.  Most of the Steel Legion infantry options are unusably awful along with various other units on both sides which are either far too weak for their cost or just much too limited to be of use on any map with terrain features getting in your way.  The same with all the fliers for both sides.  They can do a bit of damage, yes, but they're going to be massacred as they do that bit of damage and are not useful as scouts.  Also, most Ork units are pretty weak for their cost, which only really matters in multiplayer, but is important if the multiplayer option is of interest to you.

Other than that, it's all good.  The graphics and sound are pretty much par for the Panzer Corps-course.  The mission design is pretty good and thankfully they leave the inevitable "hold out against overwhelming forces for X turns" until later in the campaign, not at the very beginning.  By that stage, you're pretty attached to those units and their precious experience, so watching them crumble and burn is especially painful, but in a nice piece of plot/design, the very next mission's just the point when you would likely be voluntarily disbanding a whole lot of these units in order to access some pretty phenomenal new shinies, so it really works out well.

All in all, a good choice, though if you dislike either the WH40K universe or Panzer Corps, this isn't likely to sway you.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Warhammer 40,000 Armageddon is a turn based stratgy game about using tanks to blow up orks! The voice acting is good, the art is nice, and the gameplay is smooth. You have at your fingertips many different units to command(of varying effectiveness).  8/10

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: So... that was it?

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Surprisingly good game! A quite worthy Panzer General successor in the grim darkness of the far future, above and beyond my expectations. There was even one map which utilized maglec rail lines where your army could jump back and forth along the rail network while Orks attacked from different quarters. The campaign story was also surprisingly good, and true to the detailed background. The mysterious and seemingly sinister cult hinted at by the communications adjunct (who by the way helped drive home the massive casualties of the war) added an intriguing sideplot, and the game ending in the way it did was just right! The journey goes on.

Hats off to the developers! Well recommended.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
This game isn't bad, but it's far from good.  It's a mediocre game all the way and medocrity spoils any long-term fun.

The idea of the game is fun enough and the first few missions are great, but ultimately the fun you have wears out as you slowly realize how shallow and poorly balanced everything is.  There are TONS of units available in the game - a good representation of the table-top offerings, but they're abysmally balanced and you won't use 90% of them.

There's very little agency in the units you field either.  Ostensibly, your units carry over after each mission to be used in later missions.  Realistically, however, even your most veteran squads will be mere cannon fodder as better units become available (Space Marines etc).  

I was ultimately disappointed in the purchase.  For $15-20, I'd have had no problem, but for +$35 there's just so little under the hood.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I would recommend this to anyone who likes Wh40k and strategy games. For me it's the first time I've played a hex-based strategy game and I am very happy with my purchase.

- There's tons of scenarios. I'm six hours in and I haven't even finished Act 1. Even without the DLC you'll have plenty of content.
- The huge unit roster gives you a lot of options to customize your army. Also adds replay value. 
- Many difficulty settings, for newbs and veterans alike. I'm playing on Normal and it feels like a challenge. 
- Feels like a Wh40k game. Atmosphere is good and ofcourse you're playing in a very famous war. 

I rarely write reviews, but I felt like giving something back to the devs for this great game.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Fun to play, moddable, captures the feel of WH40K universe pretty well. Love the range of units, all the way to Titans. Could use a scenario generator to improve replayability.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: 9 hours in and I am just in love with this game. I am reading books like Rise and Fall of the Third Reich and many books on strategy and this game sure satisify my war itch more than Panzer Corps. Sure Panzer Corps suppose to be realistic but this game is not too hard but still challenging and has scenerios that reflect how battles are really won. The graphics are better than many other type of games from this genre but not what Warhammer fans are use to. I love the story line of another ork  invasion too. The units are interesting and fun and I love using the terrian to its advantage. The game is lot more user friendly than other tactical turn based games. I cant believe im so deep in this game and yet only on the first chapter. Well worth the price to play.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
first off if your looking for a hardcore 1:1 recreation of the tabletop this game isn't for you. If your a fan of old school turn based strategy useing a hex system of movement on the same level as Panzer Corps then this game is great! 

Along with some of the mods for this game mainly the "Tens of New Units" gives you a great deal of units to choose from.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Nice game. Sort of Panzer Corps but not boring! Zillions of units to choose from, in fact probably the best variety of Space Marine, Imperial Guard and Space Ork units available. I found myself spending a lot of time going through all the land and air units because this will give you the edge in combat - though there are some good tutorials to teach you the basics. Several campaigns based on Armageddon lore, loads of scenarios etc. and user made missions too. Nothing too complicated, just good old fashioned turn-based strategy, but sexed up considerably for the Warhammer 40k universe. I hope they keep adding dlc to the game.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Had this game before and returned it ,but I always wanted it and felt I never gave it any proper playtime ,finally gave in and play like crazy ,I love the low graphic ,large unit ,simple game style ,been playing for days ,I bought all the DLC's and have thouroughly enjoyed it

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Warhammer 40K: Armaggeddon is a hex based game and is worth all the money that you'll be dishing out. The story is about the 1st invasion of Hive World of Armaggeddon. Which in case you don't know is a planet of huge mega city towers with everything wrong with china on steroids. In this polluted, firey wasteland; you are forced to repel and protect the planet from gajillions of Orks and DA BIGGEST BADDEST WARLORD AROUND Margaret Thatcher. 

The story is rather competent and is brought to life by the amazing cast of voice actors and art. The main story is divided into three acts, each of them all very meaty. If you manage to snatch this game on sale, I'll guarantee that you won't be testing out that refund button anytime soon.

I always found hex-based strategy games to be rather tedious due to how much number crunching you had to do but I have to say that the UI of Armageddon is the strongest part of the game. You can check what each stats do by clicking the stats and with the ability to compare units, I have to say I actually found out that I kinda knew what I was doing (A rare occurence I must admit).

There are so many different units in the game, all so full of flavor and tasty fluff. Commanders waving power swords, walking gun fortresses, and midget snipers. Combined with decent animations and sound effects I felt like I had a proper commander of the Steel Legion.

Now to summarise, despite my glowing review I did get overwhelmed by the sheer variety of options but take it slowly and check out different options and button. Experimentation is key. You'll notice that as the ball starts to get rolling, that it'll get harder to stop.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
A awesome take on 40k! the campaign is challenging and the multiplayer is exciting.

Pros:
- SO. MANY. UNITS.
- great level detail
- a level editor
-many more good things 
Cons:
NADA

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I actually love the artstyle and graphics of this game. I mean its not hard to look at the fucking screenshots to see if you are going to like it or not!. 

This is one of the best warhammer games i've played and one of the best Hex-based combat games too.  I can see $40USD for this might be an issue for some people but i got the game and all the DLC for $15 so im pretty pleased with it.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
This game is definitely easy to learn, difficult to master. The basics behind the gameplay are deceptively simple, but as you unlock more and more units as you advance through the campaign, there are more options available and more potential "builds" for your army.

The campaign itself is both forgiving and rewarding.  With each new scenario, your units who survived the previous scenario will still be around, restored to full strength and retaining the combat experience they earned in prior battles.  However, at the same time you are given plenty of points to buy new units--even a whole new army if, say, you want to go from an infantry-heavy build to a few tanks and a titan. So it doesn't matter if all but a handful of your troops got wiped out the last mission (it's to be expected, what with the Imperial Guard and all), you can buy new ones next mission, or experiment with that new piece of artillery you just unlocked.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Terrible production values.  I'm used to strategy games having poorer graphics but this was really bad.  It is as if they just stuck a Warhammer sticker onto some no-name strategy engine and kicked it out the door.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Absolute CRAP. If you like Warhammer 40K, even to the slightest degree then DO NOT waste your money on this festering pustule of a game. it is NOTHING like 40K. The basic mechanics and gameplay is OK. Could use a bunch of small improvements like seeing a highlighted range/movement area before you move and shoot but it's not game breaking. What IS friggin game breaking is the B.S. unbelievable crap that infantry can do. Countless times I had a decent tank utterly destroyed by a pack of orcs with shotguns and cleavers. No, not heavy weapons or rocket launchers but goddam pea shooters and kitchen tools. This is just like the ludicrous stuff you see in the CIV games where some roman spearman can take out an Apache attack helicopter because...reasons. Also stuff appears out of goddam NOWHERE. You have extremely limited line of sightand many times your movement will be interupted as you come crashing into an enemy vehicle that you couldn't see. The game doesn't stop you 2 hexes short and ask if you want to continue, no, you just smack into it and auto attack if it's in your path, causing many expensive losses. It's great that you can earn experience but then again it doesn't seem to improve the units at all and they will all  die horribly before you get too good anyway. Even if you have a flying vehicle you can't see past 2-4 hexes. Sure these guys have interstellar travel but they have no concept of surveillance or even bloody binoculars. Oh and don;t expect Space marines or cool stuff like a Land Raider cos you're imperial guard in the Campaign so basically theres nothign cool. This is a ONE star game, end of story.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Well.. I never thought this would happen, but this title sucked me right into hex based wargaming.

Honestly I'm extremely narrow minded with my strategy games. Most of the time you can lose my by saying "Turn Based"... let alone "hex based", but after watching a few let's plays this one lodged in my mind and wouldn't desist until I tried it myself.

It really does look like arse... like you're fiddling with a playset made of kitchen magnets, sliding them across an out of scale representation of this industry and war wracked planet... buuuut spend a little time with it and you gradually find yourself mentally filling in the blanks... noting the wee access ramps in the trenchworks; correctly ascertaining the changes in elevation. Yeah, it's a computer game that makes you use your imagination. 

And really there's no excuse for that... at full price it's forty bucks for a title without a single animation (other than some weapon effects)... but... it's good.

It's actually really good. Ineffably good in fact. Watch some videos. It will either grab you or it won't... if it does it might very well convert you to a turny, hexy gamer (if you weren't already).

annnnnnnd it's better than DOW3

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
[h1]Turn-based tactics lite - with 40k flavor.[/h1]

I've been playing 40k video games since the days of SSi's Chaos Gate and Rites of War. Overall, this is an above average fare of turn based tactics and strategy. The strategic elements are light, and the difficulty isn't too high - any veteran of turn based hex strategy games will be able to breeze though.

[b]Breakdown:[/b]

[u]Story:[/u] 6/10 - For those familiar with the 40k universe, this is the 2nd war for armageddon. This game plays it safe and does nothing with the story other than tread through some basic plot points. Nothing to write home about, but the 40k world is rich in details and atmosphere, so it gets a slightly above average score for me. 

[u]Aesthetics:[/u]  8/10 - Solid showing of the 40k atmosphere and tone. There is some basic voice acting and still shots between missions with 40k art that feels like a part of the universe. The maps are pleasing to look at for the most part and the unit spirtes look about right. With that said, there was nothing that stuck out to me as being particularly exceptional. 

[u]Gameplay:[/u]  6/10 - This was a rather bog-standard hex based strategy game. The AI seemed rather overly-cautious and at no point did I feel like they were difficult. It is definitely fun moving units around and using superior positioning to destroy your enemies - but there's one major element that works against the game: a turn limit. This force a particularly aggressive mindset of play, and so the game will HEAVYILY favor certain units over others simply due to movemen rates. This feels like weak mechanics to me, as you will find yourself using the same tactics over and over to defeat your opponents. By mission 25, I had basically run an optimial set of units and tactics while the AI struggled to make a dent in my forces. 

[u]Stability:[/u]  8/10 - I had a very small handful of crashes during my playthrough. The game can be set up to save before every turn starts however, so no progress is really lost.

[u]Value:[/u]  4/10 - If I had purchased the vanilla game at full price ($39.99 USD) I would actually feel a bit disappointed. There are other DLC to purchase that will increase playtime - but I haven't tried it yet. Considering that you have to pay for it, that doesn't change my mind one bit regarding the value. I would think that $25 - $30 is a far more fair price for the game that you get.

tl;dr: If you liked SSI's Rites of War, it is basically that game but better. Also, if you like reliving the 2nd War for Armageddon, it does do that. With that said, I'd be hesitent to buy at full price, but it is definitely worth it around the 25-30 dollar mark.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Quick! What's a medusa seige gun and what's the difference between the medusa and the armageddon pattern medusa?

If you can answer this question, congrats, you understand the main draw of this game. It has a stupidly in depth unit roster that reads more like forgeworld's catalogue. It has detail in wierd places, from Von Strabb's speaches, to even naming the rivers you're fighting over. It's pretty fun if you like 40k and know armageddon because you can understand how many grots you just blew up.

Other than that, the timer really REALLY kills the game. It sets the pace for the almost every mission. So many missions had to be repeated because you had it in the bag but couldn't move a unit fast enough for your last turn. I really wish that the AI was just cranked up to be a bit less static and it's not a race to go from 1 strongpoint to the other within x amount of turns.

Also the price, 40 bucks is a steep price to pay for a game with some issues in UI department and the fact that it does have work put into it, but it looks really really cheap. Good sale title if you like turn based strategy and also like to get all grognardy about 40k. But seriously a community mod to take away that timer in exchange for a more genuine difficulty might be greatly appreciated... get on it lads.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Good game, but haven't played for awhile.  Plan to come back.  High end game for cheap when it's on sale.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Playing different turnbased games (CIV V, Stargeneral, Steelpanthers: World at war, 1914, Battle Isle 2 u.a.) WH 40.000: Armageddon is disappointing. Why?
- High price (36 €). I have bought for 18 € on a selling offer day. 18 €? Right. Also expensiv. Maybe ist just 8 € worth?! I do not know.
- I played Wh 40k tabletop years ago. But the feeling from the past cannot be found at this game
- Low graphics seem to be from the early 2000s.
- Variety of units is unnecessary because comparing units leads straight to an army compilation consisting of specific units you don not change until you find stronger ones. There was no reasing using a different types of units.
- Aircraft are expensiv to buy and to maintain and eventually very vulnerable.
- Artillery has high range but low efficiency. Thats strange.
- Experience levels exists. But i can feel no advantage. More damage? No. Maybe a little bit more but it is not decessive.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: I love this game, I'm currently on my 3rd play through of the main campaign and can honestly say that this game deserves praise. the story is well told with good voice acting and even a couple of dialogue options in it. while the graphics aren't high end to me that adds charm as well as ease of use due to the low requirements of the game. If there was a complaint I would have about this game it's that it doesn't stagger out unit advances fast enough causing you to get access to a better unit AND that unit's upgrade at the same time which sadly makes the Macharius heavy tanks redundant since a Baneblade just works better. I will admit that 40$ is a bit rich for this game but I lucked out and got the game with all DLC for 23$, an awesome deal since those are all great as well (well maybe except for one). if you're a fan of 40K or just getting into it like me, or maybe you just want a little sci-fi in your wargames then I would definitely suggest you pick this one up.... on sale of course this is Steam.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Purge the alien, the mutant, the heretic. The Emporer protects.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
It's Review time!

another review from me where this time, I review Warhammer 40k game, it's about 2nd-3rd Armageddon Wars where both wars are between Imperium and Orks and what I like most from this game is: More Realistic Personnel damage system than even 40k Tabletop game(in Tabletop, Astartes has no more wounds than Average Person(other than they're tougher and wears Power Armor) while here, Space Marines have more Hit Points than average Guardsmen) and this game plays lot like Final Liberation

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Captures the essense of the Second War for Armageddon. Commissar Yarrick would endorse your participation in this war.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: While it does have a hefty pricetag and weak graphics, Warhammer 40k: Armageddon still delivers copious amounts of deep strategy, customization, and sheer fun. The game, while it can seem a bit obtuse at first, has a certain degree of raw charm that helps committed players overcome the pricetag, graphics, and lack of overall animations. This is, afterall, a computer version of a tabletop wargame and, thus, the gameplay itself must truly shine for it to be worth playing while bereft of many of the bells & whistles gamers have grown accustomed to. I am of the belief that it does! I love this game and think it's worth the money, especially if you can manage to get the Feature Game + DLC bundle on sale. Hope this helps!

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
It is just simply fun to me. I enjoyed it a lot and just find it is so hard to stop playing. 

By the way, I am a strategy game fan and I like the theme of Warhammer 40k. The feature I like most is the oppotunity of mixing Guardsmen and different Space Marine Chapters. It's THE only game I've played that allows players to do that.

Hope they can make another game with more races. It'll be even cooler!

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
As an avid games workshop fan from back in the 80's, I have experinced Gazkhul's emergence from a pre battle feild write up, to pinnacle ork character table top personality, and into fabled legend through the armeggeddon summer campaign way back in 3rd ed where I did the majority of my own gaming.

   This video game reconnects me to the nostalgia of those moments and leaves me with a kind of glee to approach each new tactical puzzle to see how this game models diffrent aspects of long familiar combat dynamics at a pseudo Epic scale. Although the rule set is blatantly diffrent and the voice acting on the verge of corny, I feel like the enviornment is wonderfully '40k feeling' immersive. On top of that the game that it is is VERY good. I started playing on hard difficulty right away and quickly foun d myself having to think about how to use my forces diffrently to acheive success of objectives while completely minimizing losses where I could.

   The sheer ammount of content translates to being able to play any particular favorites (although sadly not my beloved Space Wolves) and having as much as I want of poking around with as much variance in approach I want to take. the sheer AMMOUNT of diffrent units availible ensures I will have as much as my appetite craves. Wolfsinn's summary; best video war game I have bought in a decade.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Not a bad game, but kind of expensive for what it is, if you're a 40K fan and it's on sale it's worth a go.

The DLCs are something to avoid, they are basically extra maps with a more limited troop selection, you'd need to be really into the game to enjoy them.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Great game. It is NOT Panzer corps in 40k setting, as they changed the mechanics:
- No free replacements (they always cost XP, even the automatic end-of-mission ones): I had an elite infantry unit defend a choke point, killing multiple units, began the mission ~350 exp, finished with >500. But as it took losses, after replacements it was down to 380. So all that experience was in fact lost.


Compared to Final Liberation, I really hate thgat all of a unit's weapons are fired at once, at the same target. I guess that's because it's at a "higher" strategic level, not like Final liberation where 1 unit was exactly 1 unit. One downside of this is that artillery units which can fire over obstacles, can also do so with their heavy bolters, which should not happen.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I really enjoy this game, regardless of its simple graphics and sounds.
It makes more fun to play than most of the 'modern' titles,
and the story is quite nice (although the way it's told is like the rest of the graphics no longer up to date).

But what really matters is that the game is a real game,
no movie to play or sth similar, it's just a very good game, with some suprises
no more, no less

In short: I recommend this game to all fans of strategic games who are not in need of up to date graphics. If you try it, you will really be rewarded

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Not a bad game if youre a 40k fan, it can be pretty fun and has a nice variety of units and maps but i have a couple issues with it. For one the price is stupid high for what a basic game it is (thats gamesworkshop for you) and if you play on anything but easy you get harldy any turns so you have to just rush the enemy and you cant experiment at all.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Very atmospheric and dark, like warhammer should be.  Ham acting from voice actors straight out of an over the top Shakespeare play. I can just see them spitting on the "S" and showering the mike with spit.  Missions are sometimes extremely tough,, but enjoyable enough to want to replay.  8/10

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
This game managed to combine two of my favourite things... Panzer General and Warhammer 40k. If you like those two things you will love this game. For those familiar with Panzer General, this is essentially the same game mechanics with a 40k theme.

The story is slim, but I'm already familiar with the lore and so I was just playing for the gameplay. Some people may find this style of game very dry, but if you like your old school wargames you will like this. 

One thing I would like is for there to be a little more branching of the campaign. could add a whole lot more replayabilty.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Wanted something to scratch a 40k itch. This doesn't do it.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Great game i have no problems paying full price for this game but i will say i am a strategy & 40k fan so that does influence my opinion on the game.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: I loved this game, however for the last few months, for some reason, whenever i try and load or save a game i just get a violation error that closes the game. It's completely unplayable!!! It's a real shame as was very much enjoying it :(

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: The game mechanics are stupid, but it's epic 40k.  Gork approves, Mork does not.  Gork gave em the foot (or was it Mork?) and it ended up with a positive after re-rolling a 4++.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: DLC Waaayyy too expensive but a decent game.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I had this game on my watchlist till my brother told me to try it. And I have to say that I am not regreting it. 

As a Warhammer 40k Fan I like the story, even if there are no real cutscenes. You can decide on some points what you want to do in the next mission. And what I like the best is my army. You have money and deployment points, which are getting more as the story goes on, and buy your army. They can get XP, you can change(upgrade) the units in its unit type, for example you can switch the a tank type a to tank type b or infantrie a to b without losing xp. 

The battles are good. The graphics are minimal, but gives you enough for you fantasy, the firing sequences are funny enough. The difficulty is different from mission to mission, but I think that depends on your playstyle and chosen army.

The tactics are nice, you have to keep an eye on some factors like initiative and moral, have to decide when a unit should rest, to regain accuracy and some health or give some xp for not only healing but restocking the unit.

I am looking forward to play the rest of the campaign (bought the "complete" game in sale with all content), still sitting and fighting in the first one.

It would be nice if there are plans for more DLCs or even better a part two. Some little complexity added, some better graphics (especially the largness of the units) and I would definitly look into buying it.

So far to say: thumb up!

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
You have to like turn based hex battles to play turn based hex battles. If you do and also have a liking for Warhammer 40K, you will like this.

It does look like it had been released in 1999 and I personally really detest Slytherine games, which usually are technically horrendous, but for Armageddon I made an exception. I expected nothing, but being a huge Warhammer enthusiast went for it anyway and was positively surprised.

It's not a great game, but it's fun. Don't expect a slow gamepace though, the turn limits require the player to rush, always, everywhere.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: This game is fun,plain and simple.  So far the campaign has provided a variety of missions that keep it interesting.  The RPG like carrying over veteran core units from mission to mission make every little move count.  If you are into sci fi and lighter strategy wargames, you should definitely get this on a sale.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Thrashed Thraka. Repeatedly.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: This game could be fun, but unfortunately opts not to be. Instead of intelligent turn based combat, the missions are just timed rushes to reach the objective that leave the idea of strategy in the dust. If you enjoy thoughtful, planned out warfare games, you won't enjoy Armageddon.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
To the people who wrote reviews saying this game was boring: I ignored you, and I'm sorry.
I remembered Fantasy General from way back in the day and thought this game would be as good as the one I remembered.
It was not.
I did not enjoy this game. The animations and bits of story did not help immersion, but showed how shallow it was.
If you like old, old school turn based hex battle games and Warhammer, you might like this. If not?
You won't like this.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Fantastic game. If you're a fan of early Games Workshop PC games then this plays like Rites of War mixed with Final Liberation.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: If you like Warhammer 40k & "Order of Battle" style strategy/war games, this is for you.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: I tried this based on the strength of thier Order of Battle Series.  I wasn't dissappointed!  I am about 8 hours into the game so far (forgot to close the launcher and wracked up crazy hours accidently) still in act one and I have been impressed with the entire affair so far.  This was definitely worth the sale price.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
WOW - this game is FUN. The reviews initally had me hesitant but I bought it on sale and loving every minute. If you're a 40k player get this. If you no nothing about the universe you may enjoy this but knowing a bit of 40k adds to the fun. I like the story paths, massive unit selection, and tense battles.

Get this if you:
like warhammer 40k
like the panzer general type hex games
love sci fi
don't mind a somewhat "older feel" to game play

Cons
Really dont have any except that the graphics could be a bit better.

Rating 9/10 as a warhammer 40k strategy game
8/10 as a generic strategy sci fi game

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Completely thought this looked like a lame, old timey style strategy game, but turned out to be an advanced, turn style based game, with many units, and pips each one has and specializations, and many missions and choices. Pretty much the only negative I have about it, is that a unit is able to fireback to every single attack, and having a turn limit till the missions over.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Classic style, full WH40K!

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I really don't actually recommend this but since there is no other alternative this goes 55-45 on the plus side. 

As a turn-based addict and a fan of WH40k this was a true no-brainer for me. What I don't like is the relatively small campaing and the fact that you should buy the additional campaings. It is cheap, true, but still ... somehow feel like its a rip-off.

So all in all, its not a bad game, I actually had fun playing it. Its just that there only so much to play and for the rest you have to pay extra.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
NEED HELP!
Currently the game does not work for me. It works up until I try to command units, then it starts "stuttering" (most notable the red damage numbers do not fade away and begin overlapping) and the game is effectivley unplayable.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Warhammer 40.000: Armageddon, turn base hex game, I love it, it has a good story to it, I has nice content to it, I love to see more stories and campaigns spring up from this reforge templaite of fun.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
This game is fun but it does require a lot of strategy to play. This game captures the large scale battles that Warhammer 40k is known for, and adds in every single piece of military hardware for each faction. The story is pretty much the Second War of Armaggeddon and eventually the Third (So nice Ghuzkul nuked it thrice), and there are hints at the First War. 

The bad comes from a certain error that happens mid campaign for me, when you get to Hades Hive and meet with Yarrick. There is a error code that has not been addressed. I recommend this game but I also need to warn other players about it.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
After great disappointment with cheap shit aka Battle of Tallarn Commissar has dropped his vodka rations by half and finally bought the game; here is his Steam review:

Solid, well done turn based strategy game made in the Warhammer 40K universe; although the price is a bit step its only half what AAA games you will play few hours and abandon cost and this little gem can bring you hundreds hours of fun. Much more polished than most of WH related products released lately (I am talking about your Batlle for Talarn) with no bugs nor glitches; Only thing this game lacks its a bit of personalization; you don't get to choose your avatar, get no medals (nor Commissar bolt to the face) during the long campaign and your units lack the named leaders feature from authors last tittle the Panzer Corps. Still its as close as you will get to table top without spending houndred of pounds on miniatures and manuals and worthy turn based strategy game on its own.. Don't understand the complains about the graphics to be honest if it was fullly 3D tittle wiht AA, filed of view and voiced dialogues we would get a short campaign a few scenarios and Multi Player only; the graphics is neat table top like and adds to immersion without turning it into CPU killer. Highly recommended modern classic for obviously prestigious Crowd. :obviously:

They even fixed Total Biscuit point about lack of Titans v by adding a half dozen of variants to game; now after 3 years its complete mature game well worth buying and playing. For the Emperor!

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I really enjoy this game. I love the Warhammer and 40k franchises, so this was on my wishlist. But, unlike Dawn of War (which can get boring and repetitive), Armageddon has kept me very enticed. It's challenging, engaging, and really what I've been looking for in a strategy game.

As a fan of Civ & the Total War games, this is unique in that you're focused 100% on battle & strategy. You will make wrong decisions, so making adjustments is crucial. You can't just overload your army with top tier units, or win by sheer numbers alone--and there is no autoresolve. You must be very strategic with your unit balance, and your money, as you only have so much per battle and it will not refresh--it's smart to save your money/reinforcements, as you might need to call them in to attack/defend a new objective introduced mid-battle.

This game truly has been fantastic, and has re-sparked my interest in Warhammer. The tabletop games got too expensive and time consuming for me, but I feel like Armageddon has allowed me to have fun as if I was playing 40k on a table, but can do it cost effectively via a computer game. It'd be really cool if they could make a game similar that (essentially) uses this style of gameplay to recreate the tabletop gaming for these franchises.

tl;dr - fantastic hex, turn-based strategy game. Great for Warhammer fans, or anyone looking for a new strategy game.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
What a terrible WH40k tie in.  This game is a poorly coated WWII hex based adventure to boredom town.  Total ripoff as the visuals are from 2004 and the sound effects are strangley bad.  Sadly, as a huge fan of 40K, I have played over my refund time waiting ....hopeing.......

DONT BUY.  THERE are so many better RTS or even turn basted stratedgy games out there.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
After reading all the negative reviews I was a little bit wary, but I still enjoyed the game as much as the original. The units available are somewhat limited (what - I can't buy a Basilisk?), the most noticeable one being no fire support infantry in the early battles. They beome available later, but it's very hard to keep infantry alive until then.
I also noticed that the AI seems smarter and willing to take advantage of any opening. If I make a bad move, don't bother hoping the AI won't notice because it will.

I enjoyed playing it and will play it again, ratcheting up the difficulty level each time. This may not appeal to the slash-and-grab types (who really should be playing the Orks), but as a wargame I quite like it.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: bought this on sale......glad I didn't waste a lot of money on it.  Pretty boring game.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
A really fun game, while not as in depth as Panzer Corps it still remains challenging. The variety of units from the 40k universe is also very impressive and allows you to make a very diverse army that need not look the same on different play throughs.

The story is also told quite well and stays faithful to the established 40k lore. The sheer number of missions is also impressive.

I do think the game is a bit overpriced at $40 USD so pick it up when it's on sale.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Lots of 40K universe flavor.  Great look.  Better with a human opponent.  Is this a 'grand strategy' game. heck no.  It's a fun little tactical romp so you can play with your space marine toys on the computer.  As such, it excels.  Highly recommend for those that love the fiction behind Warhammer 40K.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I played this game for a while and very much enjoyed it.  But I had to uninstall it.  On reinstalling and starting up the game, the opening animation is fine, but the rest of the game the resolution is not only messed up but I can only see half the screen.  Support says the game is suppose to auto-detect, but something is wrong.  The manual says I can change screen resolution on the options page, but if it is an option it's on the part that is cut off and unusable.  So unfortunately means that I can't play this game anymore.  

The only reason I can think of is that I moved to a different monitor before the reinstall and it's a smaller monitor.. and dispite a full uninstall it for whatever reason is locked on the old monitor's resolution or something.  Regardless it's something that should've been a good game but now feels like a waste of money.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Warhammer 40k Armageddon is one of the few 40k themed games that stayed true to the tabletop game Warhammer 40k. The game is played in such a way that somewhat resembles and reminds me of an Epic scale 40k game or even a nice Apocalypse game. The texture and graphic are beautifully made with a plot that mostly resembles the story of the second war of Armageddon, where the heroes of humanity fought the brutal, mindless orks across the desert wasteland of Warzone Armageddon.
The game has very little to none glitches or bugs. It improvises a slow yet challenging gameplay. Every turn is a decision of life or death. Late into the game, you can even command legions of titans or baneblade heavy tanks and witness the epic clash of giants.
The game offers a huge variety of selection of troops including the Steel Legion of Astra Militarum, the Blood Angels, the Ultramarines and the Salamanders. You can fully customize your army to your likings, to the extent of altering the wargear of individual troops such as Gravity cannon, heavy bolter, incinterator or melta gun.
If you are a hardcore fan of Warhammer 40k, familiar with the rules of deployments and the strengths of troops, and seek for a game that transforms the tabletop apocalypse game into simple clickings, then this is the game for you.
The Emperor Protects!

Specs: Intel i7-6950X 3.50GHz CPU, Nvidia Quadro M6000 w/ 12GB GDDR5 VRAM, 128GB Corsair Dominator DDR4 RAM, Samsung 850 EVO 2.5" 2TB SSD. Running at 7680x4320 resolution.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Probably the closest you will ever get to a digital version of Warhammer 40k, with stats lifted right from the tabletop codexs, environmental modifiers, and some of the most extensive unit variations (including Forgeworld variants) ever seen in a Warhammer 40k game

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
On sale this is a great buy. It is a lite Panzer General like game using Warhammer units and mechanics. 
The story is simple but good and the game currently is very stable for me (No crashes). I do wish there were 
more graphics options and effect settings you could tinker with.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I think this game might have taken away some of my will to live. (not even 100% joking)

5: You cant target different enemies even though you have several guns and enemies are in same direction.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Wonderful game. I love it.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Very fun, simple game.  Panzer General like with Warhammer Units.  I wish it had a few more races, like Chaos, Tau, Eldar, etc etc, Necrons.

Well worth the sale price

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: It's basically the video game version of a giant Warhammer 40k table top battle. I've just been playing it on easy and it is fantastic.  The turn based strategy system is perfect for controlling such massive scaled wars. If it were in real time, it would be far too much to manage all at once. This system lets you really startegize your next move and attack while maintaining total control of where your units are at all times.  I dont even mind the graphics and the scroll zoom works great. I highly recommend this game (and the 3 Space Marine DLC's) to any warhammer 40k fan that wants a chilled out, board game style game.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Refunded after trying the bundle in the sale. Was more clunky than I thought it would be. I would expect this to run on a 486 DX2 x86 Pentium 2, 60 Mhz, and would have loved this when I was a teenager, when still exploring Space Marine table top game. It could have been a lot better, and there is not a lot to it at all.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I've been a longtime fan of 40k and I regularly play the most glorious faction, the Imperial Guard.  Playing this game gives me a very similar feeling to the actual tabletop, much more than Dawn of War or similar games might.  Many of the models are present in one form or another, and the game actually accounts for multiple weapons on single units, use of morale in the game, and include many units that are very rarely seen on the tabletop (usually due to rarity/price) so it's a lot of fun to see it all there.  

If you've enjoyed Panzer Corps as well, I think this game is a great way to continue that experience in a new setting.  Although the game doesn't do anything radically different compared to it, the new setting and fun campaign makes it a worthwhile purchase.  

My only major complaints about the game is that the UI doesn't feel nearly as good as Panzer Corps.  Whenever you attack an enemy there's multiple shots that go out, and it can be somewhat harder to figure out if an attack is going to be a success.  The graphics are also pseudo-3d models it seems.  It's a personal preference, but I definitely preferred the drawn models of Panzer Corps.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Great Game, especially for those who like Games Workshop games.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: good game,go through the histroy of 2nd armageddon war to experience the bloody and ferice,full of strategy especially in very hard mod and fog on.also a challenge to manage your army not to mess together.no flank is a pity.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I like the Warhammer 40K universe, and this game manages to catch a fair amount of the atmosphere of that world. It works well as a strategy game, with its well-tested hexagon turn-based gameplay, the variety of units as well as a plausible campaign story. I also appreciate that said story is narrated with voice actors, even though they are all a bit cliché - but this is Warhammer after all, so this is acceptable.

However, the graphics are not really impressive. There are no cutscenes when the campaign story is told, just static portraits and backgrounds - they did not even animate the faces, like on a communicator screen, which would have been quite easy. Also, the units look nice and stylish, and the weapon fire is visible, but neither is the marching movement nor the effect of being hit. Moving units are just shifted like Warhammer action figures on a table-top, and dying soldiers merely disappear. Even though it is not a dealbreaker, I do feel that this is not the standard we should have in a game of 2014 either. I do not expect refined graphics in the style of the Total War series, but a little bit of unit animation like in Civilization V would have been nice. The room for improvement is so obvious that in this form of presentation, it just feels a little cheap on the outside.

Still: On the inside, it is an OK strategy game, and I give it a 6.5/10.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: It's Panzer Corps meets Wh40K.  If you love both, like me, it's a must have title.  That being said, the original asking price of $40 is absurd for what is basically a reskin of an already existing game.  I assume Games Workshop is at least partially to blame for the price.  It's a $20 game that you should try to get on sale for $10-15.  Space Marine DLC's have lots of content (units and a campaign), but Golgotha and Ork Hunters are a bit of a disappointment (just a couple short campaigns).

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: For WH40. fans is obligatory.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Simple mechanics, fairly good lore-wise, rather enjoyable dialogues. Better to wait for sale rather than paying full price, but definitely worth playing.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: As both a Warhammer enthusiast and a panzer corp fan, I absolutely loved this game. The vast majority of complaints appear to be people whining about the turn limit or the absolutely ludicrous amount of unit variations. I can understand the time limit stance, but if you think about it, if you are fighting orcs you need to stay mobile, if you are in a single field of combat for too long you WILL be over run, so the turn limit makes sense. Any dedicated warhammer fan will be able to understand the differences between the different Leman Russ patterns and Infantry units, and in my opinion it is one of the positive points of the game. The versatility of the Steel Legion is clearly demonstrated by this large selection of units, and only a small number of units wouldn't do them justice. It's not a perfect game, and there are definitely missions I wish I had a turn or two extra for, (LOOKING AT YOU HIVE INFERNUS,) but that's just Warhammer for you.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Basic Overview: Alright, having played this game a few hours, it's fairly easy to sum this game up as the gameplay is fairly standard turn-based stuff. Basically this is very much like many of the classic turn-based military strategy games. You move pieces across a board, they kill each other, and there's a very complicated version of rock, paper, sissors based off of the Warhammer 40k universe. It's set on Armageddon during the 3rd Armageddon war.

Graphics: The close ups on the 3d models seem to be a bit unpolished compared to many games nowadays. This may bother some people but to me graphics isn't as imporatant as gameplay. Also, it's worth mentioning that units don't have moving animations, they slide across the board. They do, however, have firing animations.

Music: Honestly I can't remember any specific track from this game. The music is just kind of there, but it's not bad by any means, it just didn't stand out. Which may be what they were going for to keep it from getting too distracting.

Sound Design: As someone who works with music and audio stuff a lot. I really love the sounds in this game. They're smooth, tanks feel heavy, the firing sounds (to me) feel satisfying, etc.

Unit Selection: This game has a ton of different units to choose from here, infantry, walkers, vehicles, tanks, aircraft, artillery, titans (yes, you read that right it has titans in it! Quite a selection actually! :D), etc. Factions include Imperial Guard (including Armageddon, Armageddon Hive Militia, Ork Hunters and Penal troops, and Mordian Iron guard sorry boys, no Kriegs yet), Orks, and a few different Space Marine factions. As someone who fielded an Armageddon Steel Legion army myself, I'm very happy that they took the forefront in this game.  The variation in units may be overwhelming for some players and will undoubtedly confuse and perhaps frustrate someone new to Warhammer 40k. But let's face it, if you're throwing down $40 for a turn-based strategy you're probably hopelessly addicted to the 40k setting like most of us.

Gameplay: Sadly this is where the game sort of suffers. It's turn-based and does a good job of capturing the feel of the Third Armageddon War. But sadly while the game is a little entertaining, I mean I've put in a few hours and I plan on putting in a few more, it just doesn't have the umph that titles like Dawn of War have and I could easily see a lot of people calling this game 'boring'.

Story: While at first I was going to say I'm not going to spoil the story here, I realized the codex I first read this story from was printed in the year 2000, and that's sort of like saying I won't spoil the plot twist to Lord of the Rings... But that said, this pretty much is the story of the conflict of the 3rd Armageddon war and to hardcore W40K fans, it features some legendary names like Commissar Yarrick, Ghazghkull Thraka, and Herman von Strab.

Final Thoughts: After going through this game and playing it for a few hours. While I did enjoy my time, I don't believe this game is $40 good. The gameplay is sadly just too repetitive and unengaging to keep a lot of players captivated for a long period of time. I believe that for many gamers who like the more slow-paced turn based strategies, or want a classic style turn-based strategy this game will be a good fit. At least if they're familiar with the 40k Universe enough to know what the units are. If you're looking for an action-packed adrenaline rush, you won't find that here, and I'd strongly suggest other 40k titles like the hit Space Marine instead. All in all I liked the game, but asking $40 is a bit much to ask for what you're getting, but that's just how Game's Workshop is, isn't it? :3

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon is a fun game.  It's easy to get hooked on and all of the DLCs available make it a lasting experience.  I like managing units, attacking, and defending areas.  Each unit has it's strenghs and some units are much stronger as the game progresses.  I recommend this game to younger and older gamers.  It's not great graphically but the simplicity of the graphics are one of it's strongest suits.  I also recommend Panzer Corps.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Boring

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Good:
- Immense variety and attention to detail for every available unit function, and lots of small but nice touches to the unit profiles bring the armies alive 
- Campaign is genuinely challenging, and bringing a unit or two through the whole thing is a satisfying accomplishment
- Exercises long term strategic thinking, but never makes it feel like a chore unlike the predecessor from x years ago, Final Liberation
- Also unlike Final Liberation, game tempo remains high during firing resolution; no waiting 3-5 seconds for each Basilisk
- BASILISKS ARE GOOD.
- Titans break the game wide open... for a while, and then have to be carefully shepherded as they face their Orky counterparts. Frequently occurs throughout the tech tree to varying degrees
- "Accurately" and satisfyingly represents a "Space Marines Save The Day" scenario, without it feeling forced
- Nearly any play style and army doctrine can work 

Bad:
- Art is largely non-descript, or re-used
- Music is bland
- Occassional hiccups in Fog of War and sprite placement after playing for an extended period
- Voice acting a bit forced some times, though Yarrick and most of the Marine leaders are agreeable

Other:
- While table top unit types are present and do perform generally the same role, their effectiveness is not always identical. For example the Wyvern isn't the grim death we know and love and thus the combat system has to be properly absorbed to get the most of it.
- Air power is very delicate and requires a lot more control and thought than would otherwise be expected

This is a Good Game and has a Fun story Campaign with some interesting diversions. The only thing really letting it down is the presentation, but if you can ignore that you'll find a 40k experience which should be considered a high water mark for things to come. It is also Better than Final Liberation in pretty much every way.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
This game started off good, but quickly turned into a tedious rush to secure objectives (some of which were hidden) before the time limit expired. Couple this with overwhelming enemy forces, which easily massacre your weak army, and you have yourself the perfect recipe for gamer rage. 

There's little strategy in the game. Not when you're forced to rush, rush, rush, on every mission. There's little difference in the mission design, either. It's the same objective each and every time; capture objective markers. It's boring.

Not sure why there's a seperate soundtrack for you to buy, either; I swear I've only heard one track on an infinite loop the entire time I've played.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Did you like SSI's Panzer general series but always thought it would be cool to play a Warhammer 40k reskin. Well, this is it.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: I absolutely love this game.  Don't expect fast action, awesome sound effects, or mind-numbing graphics.  This game is purely about strategy.  Reminds me of military madness or advance wars.  A large variety of units, but the fight sounds and animations are very old school.  A very relaxing game since you don't need to pause to walk away.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Do you like Panzer General 2 or Panzer General 3d? Good then buy it. Some of scenarios are a bit weak and limited turns is not a selling point but, buy this for the multiplayer.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
For those of you of a particular age who have played Final Liberation, this is the spiritual successor in pretty much every respect. Granted, there's no Commissar Holt, but we get Yarrick instead. Good enough for me. 

The graphics aren't going to wow people new to the 40k universe but the little details that fans are aware of are there (usually in the form of upgraded units). The sounds are par for the course, but generally what is expected from a title that's carrying the torch for Final Liberation. The story, set during the Second War for Armageddon, is relatively spare if competant enough. However, who plays a strategy title for the story? 

For someone like me, a 40k fan for at least long enough to remember the old 90's PC games, this is a nice game. For someone expecting a game that pushes boundaries, this isn't for you. I'd recommend this title if you like strategy games, especially if it's on sale.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Sadly missing features that make these games more fun and replayable ie medals, promotions, multiple epic campaigns joined together etc The story becomes fixed without any unique aspects to me.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: This game is a decent 40k game that mostly shines in its vast array of vehicles and some units. Some are pretty close attention to detail and it's a great medium for the quasi turn based table top experience. However the game lacks in game modes and multiplayer experience. The DLC'S should also have voice actors due to it costing money.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
If you played and loved Final Liberation then this is the game for you. While it doesn't possess the awesome FMV cut-scenes of its ancestor, the gameplay is brought firmly into the 21st century.

The game is simple to pick-up and play. The basic tutorials are quick and easy and part of the actual story-arc. Hidden away in the missions menu there are also some extra advanced tutorials that you may want to check out.

For fans of Warhammer 40K the playing out the Second War of Armageddon is awesome. With each battle, more troop and armour options (and later Titans) open up. Each mission has a maximum number of turns and differing victory conditions to keep you entertained. In between some of the missions you partcipate in some dialog and your choices there influence parts of the game.

In terms of turn-based hex-based wargames it plays very similar to Steel Panthers World at War. You have predefined deployment hexes, and each unit has a visibilility range which determines if that unit detects an enemy unit. Units have various ranges for any ranged weapons they possess and have differing types of damage based on whether the target is armoured or not.

I have all the DLCs available at the time of writing but I have yet to play any so unfortunately cannot comment on them.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Oh, yeah. Finaly. A strategy game without tons of visual features (but still good-looking). Pure gameplay. And deep Setting, never forget about setting.  I wish all new strategies use this approach....

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I got introduced to Warhammer 40k through Dawn of War 2 and really liked the setting. So I got up to speed lorewise by visiting Lexicanum and the Warhammer 40k pages on 1d4chan. I don't play turn-based strategy games all that much, the last title being Fire Emblem: The Sacred Stones all the way back in 2005 for the GBA. So yeah up front I don't have much experience with hex-based and turn based strategy games.

I have played through the main campaign, part of the free DLC "Untold Battles", all of the paid DLC. In terms of value, this game is AWESOME. I got the whole game and DLC 33% off.

Graphics wise... it's very simple and 2D. The artwork itself is richly detailed and authentic to the source material. Combat animations are pretty simplistic although there are a ton of them modelling a large variety of weapons from Warhammer 40k. Units simply turn to whatever they're attacking and attacked by and some audio cues. Numbers float over units that are attacked indicating damage.

Here's where the game initially gets confusing. Many of the units have strength and hp. After 50 hours or so I finally understood. Infantry tends to have only 1 HP, but with 30 strength. Units that constantly need their strength replenished lose veterancy at a rapid rate, thus the more durable or indirect fire units tend to accrue more experience. This and a few other mechanics could have been explained better.

Mission design can be described as bland and straight forward. Plain military stuff like form a perimeter, capture objectives, hold ground, escort, and the occasional rescue. The campaign structure has some replay value in the form of mission branching.

At the end of the main campaign I pretty much fielded the elite units. It's a little freaky to have so many terminator squads, dreadnaughts, super heavy tanks, and so few guardsmen on the field. Oh and Titans. So in terms of lore... probably not as authentic, but I suppose if I wanted to match the fluff with the grind I could have had some fragile guardsmen with their lasguns at the forefront.

Overall I am very happy with my purchase. Plus the voice acting and story were surprisingly well done. Unfortunately only the main campaign and none of the DLC feature voice acting.

TL;DR Lots of value. Very basic graphics. Some confusing gameplay elements. Campaign is really fun and follows the Armageddon setting faithfully save for all the elite, elite units, stomping around at the end of the campaign.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
Bottom line (on top): This game could definitely be better, but because it's 40k a lot is forgiven.

This is not a strategy game. Keep this in mind.
This is a game of crushing xenos (Orks) with the ceramite soles of your Astartes' boots (wait, you're still using infantry?).

I have completed the IG campaign twice - on the default difficulty and on "Hard". The latter actually presents a challenge, but only because the 60-pack of gremlins suddenly became 160, etc. But it is still more interesting.

There are a few issues: balance, and AI and level design.

Balance-wise, there's hardly any. For example, the Imperium is known for using their subjects as cannon fodder - that's their army's bread and butter. There's no need to play the game this way. You just replace everything with titans and borrow some dreadnoughts from the Marines.
The superheavies in the game is something you want your army to consist of exclusively, as opposed to powerful super units that still need support of infantry and lesser tanks. Not here.

Level design doesn't help. Because the AI is stupid to no end (I often had situations where they could obliterate my whole army in 1 turn, but they would just skip theirs), you just literally push through the map to meet the dreaded turn limit - this is more of a concern on higher difficulty levels, where you are really pressed for time.
You can't try different formations, diversions are useless, you just have to push through.
Or you have to defend a point for a set amount of turns, which makes much more sense. These missions are usually most challenging, and you actually have time to play with the positions of your units, because otherwise every turn you spend regrouping might cost you the game.

Overall, instead of letting you feel like you control an army of the 41st millennium, you have to be concerned about stupid shit all the time.

But

It still feels awesome to play as the Marines or IG, and if you enjoy turn-based strategy, you might still give this a go. It's not strategically challenging, but it is fun and has the spirit.

As is tradition, this wargame sports 2D graphics, but unlike so many others, the visuals don't really suck. Personally I think it would be nice if this game had better visuals. This game's selling point is immersion into the 40k universe for me, and that would really help. But here we are, it keeps the sprites and stuff.

Yeah, about the cost, I think it's proper unreasonable. It seems this game is a reskin of the developer's previous game, Panzer Corps. The mechanics are largely the same (which is hard to completely verify because the game is very vague about the way weapons work), so they had to draw new sprites, and make new stats for units. Maybe I'm missing something? Don't get me wrong, they've done a great job, but this game costs nearly as much as a proper AAA title with a much bigger budget (and then there's DLC).

---

Still, this is a game I thoroughly enjoyed. I'm still am going through some DLC campaigns, but feel like I've seen enough to say what I said.

And I feel like I'm missing a lot, but I'm still yet to play multiplayer. I hope I can convince my friends soon.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
+++ Panzer Corps meets Warhammer 40k

- not as complex as pcorps

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
If you remember the old Warhammer hex-game "Final Liberation" -- I consider this game it's spiritual successor. Much like many games of it's ilk (just looking at screenshots should give you flashbacks to other games in the same vein) for those who enjoy the WH40k universe I call this a solid title. The game mechanics are fun, intuitive, and dare I say balanced (as balanced as any battle against an unending tide of Orks can be I suppose!) -- there is good voice acting and some real strategy to this strategy game -- for example I have needed to make choices such as bottlenecking weak enemy units to cause jams at choke points, where just mowing down enemies simply paves the way for more powerful units to break through -- I've seen keen initiative and calculated risk win through over a slogging sweep and clear (which often plays into the hands of enemies that are merely just getting stronger the longer you take your time) -- the atmosphere is right, and the mechanics are fun -- and CHALLENGING. Can you afford to groom experienced core units, or must you cast them aside to make room for the cannon-fodder you'll need to meet the enemy throng? Will there be air support? Can you afford it? placement and choices for troops matter and remain meaningful from turn to turn -- and that is the ACID-TEST for a good turn based strategy game. 

The cons? : I haven't played the whole initial campaign yet, but it seems like a lot of content is day-1 DLC, especially relating to space marines, which lets face it are the reason many people love WH40k so much in the first place. Maybe they'll make an appearance at the end of the basic content... maybe... but making us pay extra to enjoy some of the best things about the game from day 1 is typical of many of GW's franchises slimy bilking tactics (like space hulk having 80% of it's day 1 content pay to unlock, but that roast is for another post, or most notably how TOTAL WAR: WARHAMMER has CHAOS as a pre-order playble faction only -- that's as slimy and manipulative as it gets.

So in short -- a great game like so many GW inspired titles are, and like so many of those, they similiarly have some slimy microtransaction crap to make you regret loving them to some degree. 

Bottom line, most games would be verbally evicerated for pulling that kind of crap -- but I'm having too much fun holding trenches with my Ogryn long enough for my Basilisk Artillery to break the Greenskin hordes as I pray to the machine spirits that my Leman Russ' will endure long enough to hold the line down. For the Emperor!

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
This is a very solid panzer general type game. The strategy involved is quite deep and yet still easy to pick up and play. The voice overs and long campaign are also a nice touch.

The main people who would enjoy this are 40k fans as the attention to detail for selecting your army is incredible and that is much of the attraction to the actual table top game.

If you are the sort of person who can name at least three variations of Leman Russ AND care about the difference then you will probably enjoy this game.

The price is the main barrier to entry here - pay full price if funds are no issue but it is hard to recommend at full price otherwise. If it had a detailed skirmish mode like battlefleet gothic armada which added a lot to replay I would have paid full price but it does not.

However recommended highly for the gameplay.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: Great mash-up of 40K and Panzer Corps, campaigns are a challenge, and unit variation is immense. For any fans of Final Liberation, Panzer Corps with a 40K twist, or Warhammer fans in general. Only negative for me is fighting only Orks gets a bit boring after a while. Hopefully future add-ons will bring more 40k races into the game.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I played this briefly on a friends account and I bought it for mobile and I have really enjoyed it. The AI is challenging and the objectives and turn limits add a sense of value to the decisions one makes. 

One thing that I was surprised wasnt in the game was a map editor or skirmish mode, something that I think would add a ton more replay value to the game. Think about it, designing maps based on real hives, on or off of Armageddon and the freedom of making a terrain both challening and fun for yourself and others if an online playermade map database was created. I just want to have the freedom to make trench lines, put my guardsmen in them and face the ork onslaught.

Make an update for a map editor! (mobile too pls)

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review: fun Game turn based gaming at its best

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
By my fury, they shall know the Emperors name!

Good game, very simple and easy to learn, and with pretty solid gameplay.
The voice work is decent some characters are better acted than others, and it's fun to interact with big characters such as Yarrick and Dante.

The game also takes place in a pretty well documented and well known part of Warhammer 40k lore. The story is quite solid as well. All in all, it's a great game to pick up if you're a fan of the setting, but don't expect anything overly flashy.
That being said, you don't need to know the lore to appreciate the gameplay, and you don't need to be a 40k lore buff in order to understand the story of the game.

So if you're a 40k fan I really recommend that you check this one out. If you're just looking for a fun turn based strategy game, then that works too I guess.

name: Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon
review:
I was on the fence with this game for a long time. The main thing holding me back was the price. Holy pricey for a game with low production value batman! I read many reviews here and other places, browsed the Slitherine forums for ahwile, and decided to bite and fork over 50 hard earned dollars. Of course 4 days later it goes on sale 50% off! Needless to say, "steam" is coming out of my ears right now...but I digress.

The game is fantastic. Not quite as deep as "Panzer Corps", but deep enough to make you think about what you're doing. Do I pick up another support unit for my core force or another armor division? Do I deploy in one giant wedge formation or split my force into two battlegroups? Do I upgrade my artillery units to Deathstrike missiles for maximum effect against armor or do I keep my Earthshakers for better versatility? Do I use a turn to rest and refit my elite units or do I press on? Every mission had me asking myself these questions...the first time I played them of course.

The game has ZERO replay value; once you know how the scenarios will unfold almost all these questions will be answered for you. For this reason alone it's very hard for me to recommend the game at full price. If you're a big fan of 40k and wargames I can say this is a winner, but it's way, way, WAY overpriced. Wait for a sale, or get more value for your dollar and pick up another wargame. 

I will close by saying that after 100+ hours of play I can say that most (but certainly not all) of the negative reviews I have read are from people who thought this was 40k tabletop on PC and are disappointed to find out that it's not, or are from people who are not wargamers and somehow ended up buying this. It's a solid wargame with no replay value and a way too hefty pricetag. If $50 is chump-change to you, then I recommend this to any wargamer.

One thing i forgot to mention...the "manual" is garbage. How can you not explain, or for that matter even mention "Zones of Control". This is an absolutely critical mechanic to understand if you want to succceed and enjoy a game like this on all but the easiest difficulty. If you're not well versed in wargames and are thinking of picking this up I strongly suggest you look up ZOC on forums or whatever. You will get alot more out of the game, trust me.
